docs.title: A Field Guide for the Working Scrub Tech
docs.text:
This is not a textbook. It is a reference built for the OR floor — for the tech who wants to get faster, get sharper, and stop getting caught off guard.

Every procedure in this book is structured around three phases: Approach → Procedure → Closure. Every specialty chapter includes Quick Facts, Before You Scrub checklists, Watch For callouts, and closure tables by layer. Appendix H has one-page quick-reference cards for every major procedure.

docs.title: Disclaimer
docs.text:
This book is intended as an educational and clinical reference for surgical technologists and surgical technology students. It does not constitute medical advice and is not a substitute for formal surgical technology education, institutional training, or the guidance of qualified surgical and medical professionals.

Surgical procedures, techniques, instrument preferences, and clinical protocols vary by institution, surgeon, and patient. Always defer to your institution's policies, your surgical team, and current evidence-based practice.

The author has made every effort to ensure the accuracy of the information presented. However, the author assumes no liability for errors, omissions, or outcomes arising from the use of this material in clinical or educational settings.

docs.title: About the Author
docs.text: David Kovak, CST is a Certified Surgical Technologist with clinical experience across multiple surgical specialties. The ApC's of Surgical Technology was written to give scrub techs a practical, procedure-centered reference built for the OR floor — the kind of resource he wished he had starting out.

docs.title: A Field Guide for the Working Scrub Tech
docs.text:
This book is not a textbook.

It is a reference built for the OR floor — for the tech who wants to get faster, get sharper, and stop getting caught off guard. Whether you are three months out of school or three years into your career, this book is written for the moment you are standing at a back table trying to figure out what comes next.

The framework is simple. Every surgery — every single one — has three phases:

THE APPROACH How do you get in? — Position. Prep. Draping. Gown and gloves. The knife. Retractors.

THE PROCEDURE What happens once you're inside? — Anatomy. Dissection. Steps. What the surgeon needs and when.

THE CLOSURE How do you get back out? — Layer by layer. Specific suture for each layer. Drain or no drain. Dressing.

That structure — Approach, Procedure, Closure — never changes. The instruments change. The specialty changes. The patient changes. But any scrub tech who has internalized this framework can walk into a case they have never seen before, read the procedure, and immediately know what questions to ask.

docs.title: How to Use This Book
docs.text:
Part I covers the foundations — sterile technique, instrumentation, sutures, hemostasis, positioning, anesthesia. Read these when you need to understand the why behind what you do. They are written as practical guides, not lectures.

Part II is where you will spend most of your time. It covers every major surgical specialty, procedure by procedure. Each procedure is structured under the three ApC phases. At the top of every procedure you will find:

  • Quick Facts — wound class, position, average time
  • Before You Scrub — a checklist of what to confirm before the case starts
  • The full Approach → Procedure → Closure breakdown
  • Watch For — what goes wrong, what to anticipate, what the surgeon will call for that you may not expect

Part III covers closure principles, drains, dressings, specimen handling, and post-op transitions — the last phase of every case.

Appendix G has Mayo stand setups for every major procedure. Appendix H has one-page quick-reference cards — everything you need for a case on a single page.

docs.title: The Standard You Are Building Toward
docs.text:
The difference between a good scrub tech and an excellent one is anticipation. The excellent tech never makes the surgeon wait. They know what instrument is needed before it's asked for. They know when to pass and when to hold. They know the anatomy well enough to understand what just happened and what comes next.

That is not a personality trait. It is a skill built through deliberate study of how surgeries actually work — one procedure at a time, in the exact format this book uses.

Start with the cases you scrub most often. Read the procedure the night before. Check your Mayo stand setup against Appendix G. Walk through the Watch For section. Then scrub the case and see what you notice that you would have missed before.

That is how this book is meant to be used.

Begin with Part I — Chapter 1: What Is Surgical Technology? Jump to Part II — Chapter 12: The ApC Framework Quick Reference Cards — Appendix H

docs.title: Chemical Indicator (CI) Classes
docs.text: Class Type Detects Class 1 Process indicator Exposure to sterilization process (not parameters) Class 2 Specific-use indicator Specific test (Bowie-Dick); air removal in pre-vac steam Class 3 Single-parameter indicator One critical parameter Class 4 Multi-parameter indicator Two or more parameters Class 5 Integrating indicator All critical parameters; highest CI performance Class 6 Emulating indicator All critical parameters for a specific cycle

docs.title: Spaulding Classification Quick Reference
docs.text: Category Device Contact Minimum Processing Examples Critical Sterile tissue or vascular system Sterilization Surgical instruments, implants, IV catheters Semi-critical Intact mucous membranes or non-intact skin High-Level Disinfection (sterilization preferred) Flexible endoscopes, laryngoscope blades Non-critical Intact skin only Low- to intermediate-level disinfection BP cuffs, ECG leads, stethoscopes

docs.title: High-Level Disinfectants
docs.text:
Agent Contact Time Notes Glutaraldehyde (Cidex) 20–45 min (HLD); 10 hr (sterilization) Toxic fumes; requires ventilation; must be rinsed Ortho-phthalaldehyde (OPA / Cidex OPA) 12 min at 20°C Less irritating than glutaraldehyde; stains proteins (including skin) Peracetic acid (Steris, Medivator) 12 min (processing system) Fast; effective; clean rinse cycle included Hydrogen peroxide (3.7–35%) Varies Used in automated systems Sodium hypochlorite (bleach) Varies by concentration HLD at high concentration; sporicidal; corrosive to metals

docs.title: Appendix F: Study Questions by Chapter
docs.text: Use these questions for self-assessment, group study, or examination preparation. Answers are found within each respective chapter.

docs.title: Chapter 1 — What Is Surgical Technology?
docs.text:
1. What does CST stand for, and which organization administers the exam?
  2. What is the AST, and what is its purpose?
  3. What year was the Association of Surgical Technologists founded?
  4. Describe the three phases of the surgical experience.
  5. What is the difference between the scrub role and the circulating role?
  6. Define "scope of practice."
  7. What is HIPAA, and how does it affect the surgical technologist?
  8. Define surgical conscience.
  9. How many CE credits are required every four years for CST recertification?
  10. Name three ethical obligations of a CST.

docs.title: Chapter 2 — The Operating Room Environment
docs.text:
1. Describe the three OR traffic zones and the attire required in each.
  2. What does "positive pressure" mean in the context of the OR?
  3. What is the minimum number of air exchanges per hour in an OR?
  4. What is laminar airflow and where is it used?
  5. What is the OR temperature range and why is it maintained there?
  6. Describe the fire triad and give an OR example of each element.
  7. What is the RACE protocol?
  8. Describe the difference between the back table and the Mayo stand.
  9. What are Raney clips used for?
  10. Why should OR doors remain closed during active cases?

docs.title: Chapter 3 — Microbiology and Infection Control
docs.text:
1. Define pathogen and give two examples of gram-positive and two gram-negative bacteria.
  2. What is a spore, and why is it significant to sterilization?
  3. Describe the chain of infection and its six links.
  4. What are the three modes of transmission, and which is most relevant to SSI?
  5. What is the difference between standard precautions and transmission-based precautions?
  6. Why is alcohol-based hand rub ineffective against C. difficile?
  7. Describe the surgical hand scrub procedure.
  8. What is the neutral zone and why is it used?
  9. What is biofilm and why does it matter in instrument processing?
  10. Name three risk factors for SSI in the surgical patient.

docs.title: Chapter 4 — Sterilization and Disinfection
docs.text:
1. Define sterilization and differentiate it from disinfection.
  2. Describe the Spaulding classification and provide examples for each category.
  3. What are the six steps in the instrument reprocessing workflow?
  4. Compare steam sterilization and EtO: indications, advantages, disadvantages.
  5. What is IUSS and when is it appropriate?
  6. Describe the difference between a biological indicator and a chemical indicator.
  7. What organism is used in a BI for steam sterilization?
  8. What action is taken when a BI returns positive?
  9. Explain event-related sterility.
  10. Why must instruments be cleaned before sterilization?

docs.title: Chapter 5 — The Sterile Field
docs.text:
1. What is the sterile field? List five items that are part of it.
  2. What does "strike-through contamination" mean?
  3. List ten principles of aseptic technique.
  4. Describe closed gloving technique.
  5. How should a circulator transfer a peel-pouch item to the sterile field?
  6. What is surgical conscience, and how does it relate to contamination?
  7. When is open gloving technique used?
  8. Why should the sterile field not be left unattended?
  9. Describe how to detect and respond to a contamination event.
  10. What is the rule about repositioning placed drapes?

docs.title: Chapter 6 — Surgical Instrumentation
docs.text:
1. Name the five categories of instruments and give two examples of each.
  2. What is the difference between a Mayo and a Metzenbaum scissor?
  3. How is a scalpel blade safely mounted?
  4. Describe how to pass a loaded needle holder.
  5. When are counts performed?
  6. What is the procedure when a final count does not reconcile?
  7. What is a preference card?
  8. Describe the proper technique for passing a free tie.
  9. What is a Kittner/peanut, and how is it prepared?
  10. What happens if the instrument count is unresolved when the surgeon wants to close?

docs.title: Chapter 7 — Sutures, Needles, and Wound Closure Devices
docs.text:
1. Differentiate absorbable and non-absorbable suture.
  2. Compare monofilament and braided suture.
  3. What does "3-0" mean in suture sizing?
  4. Is 2-0 larger or smaller than 3-0? Explain why.
  5. Name three absorbable sutures and their approximate absorption times.
  6. Describe three needle point types and a use for each.
  7. What position on the needle should suture be loaded in the needle holder?
  8. What is a GIA stapler, and how does it differ from an EEA?
  9. When is tissue adhesive (Dermabond) appropriate?
  10. What is a retention suture, and when is it indicated?

docs.title: Chapter 8 — Hemostasis
docs.text:
1. Describe the three phases of physiologic hemostasis.
  2. How is a free tie passed to the surgeon?
  3. What is the difference between monopolar and bipolar electrosurgery?
  4. Why must the dispersive electrode be applied correctly?
  5. Name three topical hemostatic agents and describe each.
  6. Why must thrombin never be injected IV?
  7. Name three laser-related hazards and their controls.
  8. What is the Argon beam coagulator?
  9. What does ESU stand for, and what does it do?
  10. Describe the scrub tech's proactive role in anticipating hemorrhage.

docs.title: Chapter 9 — Anesthesia
docs.text:
1. What are the four A's of general anesthesia?
  2. Describe the difference between spinal and epidural anesthesia.
  3. Name two IV induction agents and one key characteristic of each.
  4. What is the earliest sign of malignant hyperthermia?
  5. Name the two categories of anesthetic agent that trigger MH.
  6. What is dantrolene, and what is the initial dose?
  7. Why is epinephrine contraindicated in digital anesthesia?
  8. What does CRNA stand for?
  9. Describe the five phases of general anesthesia.
  10. What is the MHAUS hotline number for MH emergencies?

docs.title: Chapter 10 — Patient Positioning
docs.text:
1. What are the five goals of surgical positioning?
  2. Identify pressure points in the supine position and associated risks.
  3. How many people are needed to safely turn a patient prone?
  4. What is the purpose and correct placement of the axillary roll?
  5. What is compartment syndrome, and which position increases its risk?
  6. What is the Fowler's position complication called venous air embolism?
  7. Why must both legs be raised/lowered simultaneously in lithotomy?
  8. Name two positioning considerations specific to obese patients.
  9. What is maximum safe tourniquet inflation time for the lower extremity?
  10. Describe the jackknife position and its surgical indication.

docs.title: Chapter 11 — Skin Preparation and Draping
docs.text:
1. What is the purpose of surgical skin prep?
  2. Compare CHG and povidone-iodine.
  3. Why are razors prohibited for preoperative hair removal?
  4. Where do you start skin prep, and in which direction do you work?
  5. What is the rule about prepping a contaminated area?
  6. What is the rule about repositioning placed drapes?
  7. What is an incise drape, and what must be true before applying it?
  8. Why must alcohol-based preps dry completely before draping?
  9. What is the umbilicus prepped with and why?
  10. Which prep agent is used for vaginal and perineal prep?

docs.title: Chapter 12 — Wound Classification and Tissue Handling
docs.text:
1. Describe the four wound classifications with one example each.
  2. What is the SSI rate for a Class IV wound?
  3. Describe primary, secondary, and tertiary wound healing.
  4. What is traction and countertraction?
  5. Describe the five principles of tissue handling.
  6. What is a Kittner, and how is it used?
  7. List the layers of the anterior abdominal wall from superficial to deep.
  8. What is the linea alba?
  9. What is hydrodissection?
  10. What is blunt dissection and when is it preferred?

(Study questions continue for Chapters 13–26 — one set of 10 per chapter — covering all specialty content in Part II and all closure content in Part III. These questions are distributed throughout the text at the end of each chapter.)

docs.title: Comprehensive Review: The ApC's at a Glance
Quick-Reference Questions for Exam Prep
docs.text:
On the Approach: - What are the three OR zones? - What temperature is maintained in an OR? - Name five principles of aseptic technique. - What is a Class 5 chemical indicator? - What is the gold standard for monitoring sterilization cycles? - Name three types of drapes. - What is the Critical View of Safety?

On the Procedure: - What is the most common SSI pathogen? - What is malignant hyperthermia and its antidote? - Name three types of surgical hemostasis. - What is the difference between TAPP and TEP hernia repair? - Name the three types of bowel anastomosis. - What is the TURP syndrome and how is it prevented? - What is cottonoid management in neurosurgery? - Name the components of a total hip arthroplasty.

On the Closure: - What is dead space, and how is it eliminated? - Name four types of wound drains. - What is event-related sterility? - What is NPWT (wound VAC)? - When must frozen section specimens NOT be placed in formalin? - What documentation is required for implants? - What is the SBAR framework? - What is wound dehiscence, and what does "salmon-colored drainage" indicate?

Back to Table of Contents

docs.title: Appendix G: Instrument Illustrations and Mayo Stand Setups
docs.text:
How to Read These Diagrams

Each Mayo stand is shown as a top-down view. Instruments are arranged left to right in order of use: - Left zone — Approach instruments (incision, exposure) - Center zone — Procedure instruments (dissection, manipulation, hemostasis) - Right zone — Closure instruments (needle drivers, suture, dressings)

Numbered items correspond to the legend below each diagram. Sponges are placed at the far left; loaded needle drivers at the far right. The back table holds everything not on the Mayo — only the actively needed instruments are passed to the Mayo at each phase.

Instrument Legend: | # | Instrument | Purpose | |---|-----------|---------| | ① | #10 blade on #4 handle | Median sternotomy skin incision | | ② | Oscillating sternal saw | Division of the sternum | | ③ | Finochietto rib (sternal) spreader | Sternal retraction — THE key cardiac retractor | | ④ | DeBakey aortic cross-clamp | Occlude ascending aorta for cardioplegic arrest | | ⑤ | Potts scissors (angled) | Coronary arteriotomy to open target vessel | | ⑥ | Castroviejo needle drivers ×2 | Precise anastomosis of graft to coronary artery | | ⑦ | 7-0 or 8-0 Prolene | Coronary anastomosis sutures (under loop/microscope) | | ⑧ | Stainless steel sternal wires | Sternal closure | | ⑨ | #5 Ethibond | Pericardial closure (if closed) | | ⑩ | 1 PDS or 1 Vicryl | Linea alba / deep fascial closure | | ⑪ | 3-0 Vicryl | Subcutaneous layer | | ⑫ | Skin stapler | Skin closure |

Back Table — Key Additional Items: - CPB cannulas (aortic, venous, cardioplegia) - Intracoronary shunts (temporary coronary occlusion) - IMA retractor (for LIMA harvest) - Chest tubes ×2 (mediastinal + pleural) with water seal setup - Hemostatic agents: Gelfoam, thrombin, Surgicel

║  TEMPERATURE: monitor every 15 minutes — neonatal priority            ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

Instrument Legend: | # | Instrument | Purpose | |---|-----------|---------| | ① | #15 blade | Left subcostal incision | | ② | Baby Richardson retractors | Abdominal wall retraction | | ③ | Malleable ribbon retractor | Retract liver and stomach during diaphragm exposure | | ④ | Small Deaver retractor | Reduce herniated organs gently back into abdominal cavity | | ⑤ | Needle drivers ×2 | Diaphragmatic suture placement | | ⑥ | 2-0 Prolene or Ethibond (horizontal mattress) | Primary diaphragm defect closure | | ⑦ | Needle drivers ×2 | Fascial and skin closure | | ⑧ | 2-0 or 3-0 Vicryl interrupted | Musculofascial closure | | ⑨ | 5-0 Monocryl | Subcuticular skin closure |

docs.title: G.24 Posterior Sagittal Anorectoplasty (PSARP)
docs.text:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║           MAYO STAND — PSARP (IMPERFORATE ANUS)                      ║
╠══════════════════════════╦═══════════════════════╦═══════════════════╣
║   APPROACH               ║   PROCEDURE           ║   CLOSURE         ║
║                          ║                       ║                   ║
║  ① Blade #15 / #3 hndl  ║  ④ Nerve stimulator   ║  ⑦ Nd drivers ×2 ║
║  ② Gelpi self-retaining  ║     (Pena stimulator)  ║  ⑧ 3-0 Vicryl    ║
║     retractors           ║  ⑤ DeBakey forceps ×2  ║  ⑨ 5-0 Vicryl   ║
║  ③ Weitlaner (small)     ║  ⑥ Metzenbaum scissors ║     interrupted   ║
╠══════════════════════════╩═══════════════════════╩═══════════════════╣
║  NERVE STIMULATOR: guides every midline cut — never cut without it    ║
║  DRESSING: non-adherent gauze only; must not obstruct the neo-anus    ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

Instrument Legend: | # | Instrument | Purpose | |---|-----------|---------| | ① | #15 blade | Midline posterior sagittal incision (sacrum → perineum) | | ② | Gelpi retractors (self-retaining) | Hold the posterior sagittal wound open throughout | | ③ | Small Weitlaner | Supplemental wound retraction at deeper levels | | ④ | Pena nerve stimulator | Map the sphincter complex — confirms muscle identity before each cut | | ⑤ | DeBakey forceps ×2 | Atraumatic rectal mobilization | | ⑥ | Metzenbaum scissors | Precise midline dissection along mapped planes | | ⑦ | Needle drivers ×2 | Muscle re-approximation and anoplasty | | ⑧ | 3-0 Vicryl interrupted | Levator ani, puborectalis, sphincter re-approximation in midline | | ⑨ | 5-0 Vicryl interrupted | Skin closure at neo-anus (anoplasty sutures) |

docs.title: Quick Reference: Blade Guide
docs.text: Blade Handle Primary Use #10 #3 or #4 Large skin incisions — laparotomy, sternotomy, orthopedic approaches #11 #3 Stab incisions — trocar sites, abscess, arteriotomy entry #15 #3 Fine, controlled skin incisions — neck, pediatric, plastics, ENT #12 #3 Hook-shaped blade — tonsillar pillar, tight-space mucosa incisions Keratome Handled Self-sealing corneal incision (ophthalmology) MVR blade Handled Microvitreoretinal stab incision at pars plana Dermatome Motorized Skin graft harvest — set depth in thousandths of an inch

docs.title: Quick Reference: Retractor Guide
docs.text:
Retractor Type Primary Use Army-Navy Hand-held Superficial retraction — skin, subcutaneous Deaver Hand-held Deep abdominal organ retraction Richardson Hand-held Moderate deep retraction — abdominal walls Weitlaner Self-retaining General wound retraction — neck, spine, hernia Finochietto Self-retaining Sternal or rib spreading — cardiac, thoracic Balfour Self-retaining Abdominal wall — laparotomy O'Connor-O'Sullivan Self-retaining Pelvic surgery — hysterectomy, C-section Greenberg (Leyla bar) Self-retaining Brain retraction — neurosurgery Hohmann Hand-held bone hook Bone/joint retraction — THA, TKA Cobra Hand-held bone hook Anterior acetabular retraction — THA Crowe-Davis mouth gag Self-retaining Oropharynx — T&A Eye speculum (wire) Self-retaining Eyelid retraction — all ophthalmic cases Gelpi Self-retaining Perineal/posterior midline — PSARP, tracheostomy Taylor Self-retaining Paraspinal muscle retraction — spine Denis Browne Hand-held Pediatric small wound retraction

Return to README

docs.title: Appendix H: Quick Reference Cards
docs.text:
One card per major procedure. Everything you need to prep a case, fast.

How to use: Find the procedure. Read it the night before. Walk through the checklist before you scrub. Use the closure table when the surgeon asks what's next.

docs.title: Card H-01 · Laparoscopic Appendectomy
docs.text:
Wound Class II (Clean-Contaminated) Position Supine → slight left tilt Avg Time 30–60 min Blade #11 × 3 (port stabs) Scope 30° laparoscope

Before You Scrub - 30° scope + camera + light cord confirmed - 10 mm clip applier loaded; 2 extra cartridges - Endoloops × 3 (2-0 chromic) - Specimen bag for extraction - Irrigation: warm saline, 1L

Closure

Layer Suture Fascia (10–12 mm ports) 0 Vicryl CTX Skin 4-0 Monocryl or skin glue

Watch For: Clip applier misfire — have loaded backup ready. Retrocecal appendix — longer dissection, more Bovie use.

docs.title: Card H-02 · Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy
docs.text:
Wound Class II (Clean-Contaminated) Position Supine, reverse Trendelenburg + right tilt Avg Time 30–60 min Blade #11 × 4 (port stabs) Fluoroscopy For cholangiogram (if performed)

Before You Scrub - 30° scope + camera + light cord confirmed - 10 mm clip applier loaded; 2 extra cartridges - Cholangiogram catheter + C-arm sterile drape if IOC planned - Maryland dissector + hook electrocautery on field - Specimen bag

Closure

Layer Suture Fascia (10–12 mm) 0 Vicryl CTX Skin 4-0 Monocryl

Watch For: Critical view of safety must be confirmed before clipping. CBD clipping = bile duct injury. Biliary leak: suction ready.

docs.title: Card H-03 · Open Inguinal Hernia (Lichtenstein)
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Supine Avg Time 45–75 min Blade #10

Before You Scrub - Polypropylene mesh (Prolene; confirm size ~6×11 cm) - 0 Prolene for mesh fixation; 2-0 Prolene for cord sling - 1-0 PDS for internal oblique/transversalis repair - Penrose drain as cord retractor (½ inch) - 3-0 and 4-0 Monocryl for subcutaneous/skin

Closure

Layer Suture External oblique 3-0 Vicryl running Scarpa's fascia 3-0 Vicryl Skin 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular

Watch For: Ilioinguinal nerve crossing the field — protect it. Mesh must lie flat, tension-free.

docs.title: Card H-04 · Open Colectomy
docs.text:
Wound Class II (Clean-Contaminated) Position Supine Avg Time 2–4 hrs Blade #10 (midline incision)

Before You Scrub - GIA 75/80 mm stapler + 2 extra loads (blue) - TA 55 or 60 mm stapler for rectal cases - Bowel bag (isolate the specimen) - 3-0 Vicryl for mesenteric vessels; 0 Vicryl for anastomosis - Irrigation: 3L warm saline + bulb syringe - Change gloves after bowel is opened

Closure

Layer Suture Fascia (midline) 0 PDS running loop Subcutaneous 3-0 Vicryl Skin Staples (Class II); or packed if Class III/IV

Watch For: Bowel content spillage → upgrade to Class III. Count all staple loads fired and discarded.

docs.title: Card H-05 · Total Hip Arthroplasty (THA)
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Lateral decubitus (posterior) Avg Time 60–90 min Blade #10 Implants Shell + liner + stem + head

Before You Scrub - Implant sizes: 2 above and below templated size - Oscillating saw + reciprocating saw; reamers in order - Trial components on back table in sequence - Double-glove; replacement outer gloves on field - Hemovac drain + 2-0 nylon for securing

Closure

Layer Suture External rotators #1 Ethibond through drill holes IT band / fascia lata #1 Vicryl Subcutaneous 2-0 Vicryl Skin Staples or 3-0 Monocryl

Watch For: Cement has a working window — track time from mix. Change outer gloves at implant placement.

docs.title: Card H-06 · Total Knee Arthroplasty (TKA)
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Supine, tourniquet Avg Time 75–105 min Blade #10 Implants Femoral component + tibial tray + poly ± patella

Before You Scrub - All trial sizes pulled and ordered - Oscillating saw + extra blades - Cement: mixing supplies ready; do NOT mix until call - Tourniquet pressure confirmed with surgeon (typ. 250–300 mmHg) - Double-glove

Closure

Layer Suture Arthrotomy (quad/medial parapatellar) #1 Vicryl figure-of-eight Subcutaneous 2-0 Vicryl Skin Staples or 3-0 Monocryl

Watch For: Gap imbalance → have thin and thick poly inserts ready. Track tourniquet time; alert surgeon at 90 min.

docs.title: Card H-07 · Craniotomy
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Supine / lateral / prone; Mayfield pins Avg Time 2–6 hrs Blade #10 scalp; #15 dural Implants Titanium fixation plates + screws

Before You Scrub - Mayfield clamp + pins on field - Raney clips × 30–40 pre-loaded in applier - High-speed drill: round burr + craniotome footplate; irrigator running - Cottonoids (1/2", 3/4", 1"): ALL counted; radiopaque thread visible - Microscope sterile drape ready; CUSA if tumor case - Bipolar × 2 pairs; bone wax on field

Closure

Layer Suture Dura 4-0 Nurolon watertight Bone flap Titanium plates + screws Galea 2-0 Vicryl interrupted Skin Staples or 3-0 nylon

Watch For: Cottonoid count — every one in must come out before the dura closes. Raney clips are counted items.

docs.title: Card H-08 · Lumbar Discectomy / Laminectomy
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Prone on Wilson frame or chest rolls Avg Time 45–90 min Blade #10 midline; #15 annulotomy Fluoroscopy Required for level confirmation

Before You Scrub - Wilson frame set up before patient arrives - Kerrison punches: 2, 3, 4 mm - Pituitary rongeurs: straight + angled (up-bite + down-bite) - Cobb elevator + periosteal elevators - Love nerve root retractor - Bipolar for epidural vessels

Closure

Layer Suture Thoracolumbar fascia 1-0 Vicryl figure-of-eight Subcutaneous 2-0 Vicryl Skin Staples or 3-0 Monocryl

Watch For: Level confirmation with fluoroscopy before any bone removal — mandatory. Dural tear → clear fluid in the field = stop.

docs.title: Card H-09 · ACDF (Anterior Cervical Discectomy & Fusion)
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Supine, shoulder roll, neck extended Avg Time 60–120 min Blade #15 skin; #11 annulotomy Fluoroscopy Required Implants PEEK cage + anterior cervical plate + screws

Before You Scrub - Caspar retractor system: all blades + distraction pins + driver - PEEK cage or graft: one size up/down on field - Anterior cervical plate: screw lengths varied - High-speed burr (AM8); pituitary rongeurs (straight + up-bite) - Bipolar; C-arm sterile drape

Closure

Layer Suture Platysma 3-0 Vicryl interrupted Subcutaneous 3-0 Vicryl Skin 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular

Watch For: RLN at risk with medial retraction. Confirm ETT is NIM-equipped if neuromonitoring is used. Fluoroscopy before cage placement.

docs.title: Card H-10 · CABG
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Supine, arms tucked Avg Time 3–5 hrs Blade #10 sternotomy; #15 SVG harvest CPB Yes

Before You Scrub - Sternal saw tested; Finochietto retractor on field - Sutures: 7-0 Prolene × 4 (coronary); 5-0 Prolene × 2 (proximal); 4-0 Prolene × 2 (purse-strings); stainless steel wires #5 or #6 × 8 - Chest tubes × 3–4; 2-0 silk × 4 for securing - Bone wax on field - Double-glove

Closure

Layer Suture Sternum #5 or #6 steel wires × 6–8 Presternal fascia 0 Vicryl running Subcutaneous 2-0 Vicryl Skin Staples or 4-0 Monocryl

Watch For: ACT >480 before cannulation. Track Prolene suture tails — keep moist. Chest tube count = part of the final count.

docs.title: Card H-11 · Carotid Endarterectomy (CEA)
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Supine, head turned away from operative side Avg Time 90–120 min Blade #15 skin; #11 arteriotomy Implants Patch graft (vein, Dacron, or bovine pericardium)

Before You Scrub - Patch graft confirmed; 6-0 Prolene × 2 on field - Silastic vessel loops × 3 (ICA, ECA, CCA) - DeBakey atraumatic clamps (straight + angled) - Freer elevator for plaque dissection - Potts scissors; heparin drawn + ready - Intraluminal shunt (Javid or Pruitt-Inahara) on field

Closure

Layer Suture Carotid sheath 3-0 Vicryl loose approximation Platysma 3-0 Vicryl interrupted Subcutaneous 3-0 Vicryl Skin 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular

Watch For: Clamp time — pass instruments without delay. 6-0 Prolene breaks — have backup loaded. Flush before releasing ICA clamp.

docs.title: Card H-12 · Cesarean Section (C-Section)
docs.text:
Wound Class II (Clean-Contaminated) Position Supine with left lateral tilt (wedge under right hip) Avg Time 30–60 min Blade #10 skin + fascia + uterine stab; bandage scissors for uterine extension

Before You Scrub - Doyen (bladder blade) retractor on Mayo stand - Bandage scissors on Mayo stand (for uterine extension) - Cord clamps × 2 immediately accessible - Warm receiving towel on the lower sterile field for infant - 0 or #1 Vicryl × 2 loaded on needle holders before incision - Oxytocin: circulator has it ready for anesthesia at cord clamping

Closure

Layer Suture Uterus #1 Vicryl running (1–2 layers) Rectus fascia 0 Vicryl running Subcutaneous (if ≥2 cm) 3-0 Vicryl Skin Staples or 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular

Watch For: Doyen retractor slipping = bladder risk. Uterine atony after delivery = call for uterotonics. Placenta to pathology cup.

docs.title: Card H-13 · Total Abdominal Hysterectomy (TAH)
docs.text:
Wound Class II (Clean-Contaminated) Position Supine; Foley catheter in Avg Time 60–120 min Blade #10 Pfannenstiel or midline

Before You Scrub - O'Sullivan-O'Connor retractor on field; appropriate blade sizes - Heaney clamps × 8 on Mayo stand + visible in clamp rack - 0 Vicryl ties pre-cut × 10; suture scissors immediately accessible - Long instruments: long Metzenbaum, long Deaver, long DeBakeys - Specimen cup + pathology slip ready

Closure

Layer Suture Vaginal cuff 0 Vicryl running locking Fascia 0 Vicryl or PDS running Subcutaneous 3-0 Vicryl Skin Staples or 4-0 Monocryl

Watch For: Ureter at risk when clamping uterine vessels — surgeon must identify it first. Clamp slippage = call out immediately.

docs.title: Card H-14 · Cystoscopy with Ureteral Stent
docs.text:
Wound Class II (Clean-Contaminated) Position Lithotomy Avg Time 15–30 min Blade None Fluoroscopy Required

Before You Scrub - Cystoscope sheath + bridge + telescope (30° + 70°) - Double-J stent: confirm size ordered; unopened on field - Guidewires: hydrophilic × 1; stiff × 1 backup - Stent pusher matching the stent system - Irrigation: 1L sterile saline × 2 primed - C-arm sterile drape

Closure No wound closure. Foley catheter may be left per surgeon preference.

Watch For: Irrigation bottle running dry → field collapses instantly. Guidewire resistance → do not force; different wire type needed.

docs.title: Card H-15 · TURP
docs.text:
Wound Class II (Clean-Contaminated) Position Lithotomy Avg Time 45–90 min Blade None Irrigation Glycine 1.5% (monopolar) OR saline (bipolar) — confirm before spiking

Before You Scrub - Resectoscope: sheath (22–26 Fr) + working element + telescope — matching system - Irrigation fluid type confirmed with surgeon; do NOT mix up glycine and saline - Ellik evacuator on field - 3-way Foley (22–24 Fr, 30 mL balloon) ready for end of case - Track irrigation in vs. irrigation out throughout

Closure No wound closure. 3-way Foley placed with continuous bladder irrigation post-op.

Watch For: TURP syndrome (glycine absorption) — deeply negative fluid balance = emergency. Do not resect past the verumontanum.

docs.title: Card H-16 · Laparoscopic Nephrectomy
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Lateral decubitus, operative side up Avg Time 90–150 min Blade #11 ports; #10 extraction site Implants None

Before You Scrub - Endo-GIA: vascular loads (white/gray) × 3 for renal vessels - Endobag: large enough for the kidney - Kidney rest elevated and locked before prepping - 0 Vicryl for extraction site fascia closure

Closure

Layer Suture Extraction site fascia 0 Vicryl interrupted Port sites ≥10 mm 0 Vicryl Skin 4-0 Monocryl

Watch For: Renal artery BEFORE the vein — always. Right side: IVC immediately medial to renal vein. Wrong load = critical error.

docs.title: Card H-17 · Tonsillectomy & Adenoidectomy (T&A)
docs.text:
Wound Class II (Clean-Contaminated) Position Supine, shoulder roll, neck extended Avg Time 20–45 min Blade None (ESU or coblation) Airway Shared (RAE tube)

Before You Scrub - Crowe-Davis mouth gag: correct size (peds vs. adult); suspension arm available - Tonsil clamps (Allis or Hurd) × 2 - ESU pencil or coblation wand — confirm generator settings - Tonsil sponges counted; specimen cups × 2 (left tonsil, right tonsil) - 3-0 chromic on a tonsil needle if suture hemostasis needed

Closure No formal closure. Mucosal beds heal by secondary intention.

Watch For — CRITICAL: Every tonsil sponge placed in the pharynx must come out before gag removal. A retained tonsil sponge = airway obstruction. Count out loud. Confirm before removing the gag.

docs.title: Card H-18 · Thyroidectomy (Total)
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Supine, shoulder roll, neck extended Avg Time 60–120 min Blade #15 (Kocher incision)

Before You Scrub - NIM ETT placed and tested before draping - Nerve stimulator (Prass probe) on field; confirmed functional - Bipolar forceps × 2 pairs: no monopolar near the RLN - Harmonic or LigaSure (confirm which device) - Small vein retractors + right-angle retractors for posterior lobe - Specimen cups × 2

Closure

Layer Suture Strap muscles 3-0 Vicryl running Platysma 3-0 Vicryl interrupted Subcutaneous 3-0 Vicryl Skin 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular

Watch For: NIM silence after a dissection step = RLN may be injured — stop and confirm. Bipolar ONLY near the nerve.

docs.title: Card H-19 · Cataract (Phacoemulsification + IOL)
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Supine, head in donut Avg Time 15–30 min Blade Keratome (2.2–2.8 mm) + paracentesis knife Implants IOL (confirm power + type)

Before You Scrub - IOL loaded in injector per manufacturer instructions — do NOT touch with gloves - OVD (viscoelastic) drawn and on Mayo stand - Phaco machine: tubing primed; BSS bags spiked; footpedal set - Eye speculum: correct size - Dilating drops confirmed given 30–60 min pre-op; confirm full dilation - Every solution labeled; do NOT use CHG near the eye

Closure Self-sealing keratome incision — no sutures. Subconjunctival antibiotic + steroid injection. Rigid eye shield (not a pressure patch).

Watch For: IOL contamination → trash it; open new one. Posterior capsule rupture → case changes; different instruments needed. Label every solution on the back table.

docs.title: Card H-20 · Split-Thickness Skin Graft (STSG)
docs.text:
Wound Class I–II Position Per wound location Avg Time 30–90 min Blade Dermatome (powered or manual) Two sites Donor site + recipient site

Before You Scrub - Dermatome: blade guard depth set per surgeon specification; mineral oil on field - Mesh carrier (if meshing): correct expansion ratio assembled - Damp saline gauze on back table for graft storage - Bolster or NPWT dressing confirmed - Donor site dressing: Adaptic/Xeroform + gauze + ACE - Change outer gloves between donor harvest and recipient site

Closure Donor site: non-adherent dressing only — NOT sutured. Graft secured with staples + bolster or NPWT.

Watch For: Graft desiccation → keep it moist at all times. Hematoma under graft = graft failure. Achieve complete hemostasis in the recipient bed before graft goes down.

docs.title: Card H-21 · Mastectomy with Implant Reconstruction
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Supine, operative arm on arm board Avg Time 2–4 hrs Blade #10 (elliptical periareolar incision) Implants Tissue expander or DTI silicone implant + ADM

Before You Scrub - Implant or expander: confirm type and size; keep sealed until pocket irrigated - Triple antibiotic solution (gentamicin + bacitracin + polymyxin B) for pocket irrigation - Change outer gloves before handling implant — no exceptions - ADM: keep in saline until needed; do not allow it to dry - JP drains × 2; 2-0 nylon for securing - Lighted retractor or fiberoptic headlight for flap elevation

Closure

Layer Suture Pectoralis / ADM 2-0 or 3-0 Vicryl Subcutaneous 3-0 Vicryl Skin 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular

Watch For: No-touch technique — implant cannot contact skin. No compression on the implant after placement.

docs.title: Card H-22 · Pyloromyotomy (Pediatric)
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Supine, small shoulder roll Avg Time 20–40 min Blade #15 × 2 Patient ~2–8 weeks old; handle everything with extra care

Before You Scrub - Baby-scale instruments: Denis Browne retractors, Fredet-Ramstedt spreader or fine hemostat - Warming blanket active; warm irrigation on field; room temp elevated - 3-0 Vicryl fascia; 4-0 Vicryl subcutaneous; 5-0 Monocryl skin - Mucosal integrity test: anesthesiologist injects air through NGT at end; saline in wound; watch for bubbles

Closure

Layer Suture Fascia 3-0 Vicryl interrupted Subcutaneous 4-0 Vicryl Skin 5-0 Monocryl or Dermabond

Watch For: Mucosal perforation — repair + Graham patch if it occurs. Do not cross the pyloric vein distally. Temperature drop in this patient = emergency.

docs.title: Card H-23 · CDH Repair (Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia)
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Supine, slight contralateral rotation Avg Time 60–120 min Blade #15 (subcostal incision) Patient Neonate (days old); left side most common

Before You Scrub - Neonatal instrument set (baby-scaled only) - 2-0 or 3-0 Prolene/Ethibond interrupted for primary repair - Gore-Tex patch on field if large defect expected - Warming blanket active; warm irrigation; overhead warming lamp if available - Check blood loss per sponge — 10 mL = >4% of a 3 kg neonate's blood volume

Closure

Layer Suture Fascia 2-0 or 3-0 Vicryl interrupted Subcutaneous 3-0 Vicryl Skin 5-0 Monocryl or skin glue

Watch For: Temperature every 15 min — a 2°C drop is a physiological emergency. Herniated organs are fragile; handle with extreme gentleness during reduction.

docs.title: Card H-24 · Free Flap (Microvascular)
docs.text:
Wound Class I (Clean) Position Varies by flap and defect Avg Time 4–10 hrs Blade #15 both sites Two teams Donor harvest + recipient site simultaneously

Before You Scrub - Microvascular instruments: Castroviejo needle drivers × 2; jewelers forceps × 2; micro scissors; vessel background material - 9-0 or 10-0 nylon on BV-1 needle × multiple (keep moist) - Venous coupler system (confirm sizes) - Sterile Doppler probe - Operating microscope drape - Communication: both surgeons must confirm readiness before pedicle division

Closure Donor site: layered primary closure or STSG. Recipient: non-compressive window dressing only — no pressure on the flap.

Watch For: Ischemia time begins at pedicle division — have everything ready before that moment. Compression on the flap after anastomosis = thrombosis = flap loss.

docs.title: Card H-25 · da Vinci Robotic Surgery (Bedside Assistant)
docs.text:
Platform da Vinci Xi / X / SP (Intuitive Surgical) Your Station Patient cart (bedside) Surgeon Location Console — non-sterile Your Port 10–12 mm assistant port

Before You Scrub - Robotic arm drapes × 4: applied before docking; no wrinkles; confirm locked - EndoWrist instruments: check use counter — expired instruments cannot be loaded - Assistant port instruments: laparoscopic clip applier, suction-irrigator, grasper, endobag - Pre-load anastomosis sutures on laparoscopic needle holders 20 min before the suturing step - Instrument exchange protocol memorized: release button → pull straight out → seat new instrument → confirm on monitor

Watch For: - Pre-load every suture before it is called — the console surgeon cannot wait - Never grab a robotic arm while it is under power — significant torque risk - Arm drape breach → stop; re-drape the affected arm before continuing - Instrument counter expiration → open replacement immediately

docs.title: Card H-26 · Mako SmartRobotics (Stryker)
docs.text:
Platform Mako SmartRobotics (Stryker) Procedures THA, TKA, UKA Requires Pre-op CT plan loaded before case Cutting Tool Burr (not a saw) Implant System Stryker (Triathlon knee / Accolade hip)

Before You Scrub - Pre-op CT plan confirmed loaded on Mako system - Tibial + femoral tracker arrays (sterile; pin-mounted) on field - Registration probe: sterile; immediately available when surgeon calls - Mako burr loaded and sterile; backup burr on field - Irrigation running during burr resection (heat generation) - All trial components in order; cement supplies ready (Mako guides cuts; cementing is still manual)

Watch For: - Array disturbance = registration lost → re-register required - Burr requires continuous irrigation — confirm before activation - Arrays are counted; note their position to the whole team before draping

docs.title: Card H-27 · ROSA (Zimmer Biomet)
docs.text:
Platform ROSA Knee / ROSA Hip (Zimmer Biomet) ROSA Knee Imageless — no pre-op CT required ROSA Hip Image-based — pre-op CT required Cutting Tool Saw (ROSA positions the cutting jig) Implant System Zimmer Biomet (Persona, NexGen)

Before You Scrub - ROSA system powered on; sterile arm drape applied - Bone pins + tracker arrays: sterile; on field - Registration probe: sterile - ROSA saw jig adapter confirmed compatible with implant system - Standard TKA instruments still required for manual steps

Watch For: - Surgeon will spend time at the ROSA screen building the intraoperative plan — do not pass instruments during this phase - Saw blade must be fully seated in the jig before activation - ROSA arm drape breach = arm exits the sterile field

docs.title: Card H-28 · Mazor X (Medtronic)
docs.text:
Platform Mazor X (Medtronic) Procedure Spine pedicle screw placement Requires Pre-op CT plan + O-arm spin for registration Robot Does Positions drill guide at planned trajectory Surgeon Does Drills, taps, and places screws manually Implant System Medtronic (CD Horizon, Solera)

Before You Scrub - Pre-op CT plan confirmed loaded - Spine reference array (sterile; clamp-mounted on spinous process or iliac crest) - Mazor arm sterile drape applied before arm enters field - Mazor-specific drill guide cannulas on field - O-arm sterile drape ready - Medtronic screw set: confirm sizes; set screws in their rack (counted items)

Watch For: - Reference array movement = invalidated registration → alert surgeon immediately - O-arm spin = hands off patient, everyone step back, call it loudly - Drill guide must contact cortical bone before drilling — not soft tissue - Set screws are tiny counted items; never leave unsupported on the field

docs.title: Card H-29 · ExcelsiusGPS (Globus Medical)
docs.text:
Platform ExcelsiusGPS (Globus Medical) Procedure Spine pedicle screw placement Unique Feature Integrated navigation + robotics (one platform) Can work with or without pre-op CT Intraoperative O-arm registration available Implant System Globus Medical (CREO, REVOLVE) — NOT interchangeable with Medtronic

Before You Scrub - Workflow confirmed: pre-op CT plan OR intraoperative O-arm registration - ExcelsiusGPS arm sterile drape applied - Globus-specific reference array and drill cannulas on field - Globus screw set confirmed (not Medtronic, not DePuy) - O-arm or C-arm sterile drape ready

Watch For: - ExcelsiusGPS arm locks automatically at planned trajectory — confirm lock indicator displayed before drilling - Reference array = same rules as Mazor X; any movement requires re-registration - O-arm spin protocol: full room clear, hands off patient

docs.title: Card H-30 · Stealth Navigation (Medtronic)
docs.text:
Platform Stealth Navigation (Medtronic) Procedure Spine, cranial, ENT navigation guidance Robot None — navigation only (no robotic arm) Tracking Optical (camera must have line of sight to trackers)

Before You Scrub - Stealth reference array: sterile; clamp-mounted; placed before O-arm spin - Instrument trackers (sterile): attach to drill, pedicle probe, tap — confirm all are on the field - Stealth-compatible instruments only: non-tracked instruments won't appear on the screen - O-arm sterile drape if spin is planned - Tracker ball cleaning routine: wipe balls with a dry sponge every time the instrument returns to the back table

Watch For: - Tracker ball contamination = instrument disappears from navigation screen silently - Line-of-sight blocking: do not cross between the Stealth camera and the tracker arrays - O-arm spin = full room clear; hands off field; call it every time - If navigation fails intraoperatively, case continues with fluoroscopy — have C-arm sterile drape in the room

docs.title: Quick Reference: Robotic Platform at a Glance
docs.text: System Manufacturer What the Robot Does Implant System da Vinci Xi Intuitive Surgical Holds/moves instruments (telemanipulation) N/A Mako Stryker Haptic boundary; guides burr Stryker ROSA Zimmer Biomet Positions cutting jig Zimmer Biomet Mazor X Medtronic Positions drill guide Medtronic ExcelsiusGPS Globus Medical Locks drill guide; integrated navigation Globus Medical Stealth Medtronic Navigation tracking only (no robot arm) Any

docs.title: Quick Reference: Blade Selection

docs.title: Quick Reference: Closure Suture by Layer
docs.text:
Layer Standard Suture Common Alternatives Fascia (abdomen) 0 PDS running loop 0 Vicryl running Fascia (orthopedic) #1 Vicryl 0 Ethibond Peritoneum 3-0 Vicryl running Often left open Muscle (deep) 2-0 Vicryl interrupted 0 Vicryl Subcutaneous 3-0 Vicryl 2-0 Vicryl (thick tissue) Skin (cosmetic) 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular 5-0 nylon interrupted Skin (functional) Staples 3-0 nylon interrupted Vascular anastomosis 6-0 to 9-0 Prolene Venous coupler (vein) Dura 4-0 Nurolon or Ti-Cron Duraplasty graft Tympanic membrane Gelfoam packing (no suture) 7-0 Vicryl conjunctiva Cornea (keratome) Self-sealing (no suture) 10-0 nylon if leak

Return to Introduction · Appendix G — Mayo Stand Setups

docs.title: 1.1 Defining the Profession
docs.text:
Surgical technology is the allied health profession that focuses on providing surgical care to patients in the perioperative setting. The surgical technologist works under the supervision of a registered nurse (RN) and the direction of a licensed surgeon to ensure that the operating room environment is safe, that equipment functions properly, and that the operative procedure is conducted under conditions that maximize safety and efficiency for the patient.

The practice of surgical technology spans three phases of the surgical experience: preoperative, intraoperative, and postoperative. Within each phase, the surgical technologist performs specific, defined responsibilities — from setting up the sterile back table before the first patient enters the room, to maintaining the sterile field during the most complex procedures, to assisting with specimen handling and wound closure as the case concludes.

Surgical technologists are not surgeons, and they are not nurses — they are a specialized hybrid, trained in the technical and aseptic demands of the operating room with a depth of procedural knowledge that few other healthcare roles require.

docs.title: 1.2 History of the Profession
docs.text:
The modern surgical technologist did not emerge from a classroom. The profession was born in necessity.

During World War II, the United States military faced an acute shortage of trained surgical personnel. Medics and corpsmen were trained to assist surgeons in battlefield and field hospital settings, performing tasks that had previously been reserved for nurses. Their effectiveness — under extreme conditions, with minimal resources — demonstrated that focused technical training could produce highly capable OR assistants.

After the war, veterans with surgical training entered civilian hospitals. Hospital administrators, already wrestling with nursing shortages, recognized the value of having dedicated OR technical staff. Throughout the 1950s and 1960s, ad hoc programs began to appear at hospitals across the country, training "operating room technicians" in the skills of the scrub role.

In 1969, a group of OR technicians and their nursing supporters formed the Association of Surgical Technologists (AST) to establish professional standards, develop educational curricula, and advocate for the profession. The organization created a code of ethics, practice standards, and — critically — a pathway toward credentialing.

The National Board of Surgical Technology and Surgical Assisting (NBSTSA) was established to administer a national certification examination, culminating in the Certified Surgical Technologist (CST) credential. Today, graduation from a CAAHEP-accredited program and passage of the CST exam are the gold standard of entry into the profession.

docs.title: Program Accreditation
docs.text:
Surgical technology programs must be accredited by CAAHEP (Commission on Accreditation of Allied Health Education Programs), through its reviewing arm ARC/STSA (Accreditation Review Council on Education in Surgical Technology and Surgical Assisting). Accreditation ensures that programs meet established competency standards and that their graduates are eligible to sit for the CST exam.

Programs are offered at: - Community colleges (most common — typically Associates of Applied Science degree) - Technical/vocational schools (certificate programs — typically 12–24 months) - Universities (Bachelor of Science programs available at select institutions)

docs.title: Curriculum Content
docs.text:
Accredited programs must include: - Basic sciences (anatomy, physiology, microbiology, pharmacology) - Surgical technology core (aseptic technique, instrumentation, sterilization) - Surgical specialties (organized by body system) - Clinical practicum (hands-on hours in actual operating rooms)

The AST recommends a minimum of 120 surgical procedures performed in the scrub role during clinical education, across a range of specialties.

docs.title: The CST Examination
docs.text:
The NBSTSA CST examination is a computer-based, multiple-choice exam covering: - Perioperative care (preoperative, intraoperative, postoperative) - Ancillary duties (sterilization, equipment, pharmacology) - Fundamental knowledge (anatomy, physiology, microbiology) - Specialty-specific content

Certification must be maintained through continuing education (CE) — 60 CE credits are required every four years to maintain CST status.

docs.title: 1.4 The Surgical Team
docs.text:
The surgical technologist works within a multidisciplinary team. Understanding each member's role — and how the surgical technologist interfaces with each — is essential to functioning effectively in the OR.

Team Member Role Surgeon Performs the operative procedure; directs the scrub tech regarding instrument and supply needs Surgical Technologist (scrub) Maintains the sterile field; passes instruments; manages the back table and Mayo stand Circulating Nurse (RN) Manages the nonsterile OR environment; documents, retrieves supplies, communicates Anesthesiologist / CRNA Manages the patient's anesthetic state and airway Surgical First Assistant Provides direct surgical assistance; may be CST-SFA, PA, RNFA, or resident Radiologic Technologist Provides intraoperative imaging when needed (fluoroscopy, C-arm) Perfusionist Manages cardiopulmonary bypass during cardiac procedures Pathologist Evaluates and interprets surgical specimens

docs.title: Team Dynamics
docs.text: Effective surgical teams are built on closed-loop communication — messages are sent, received, and confirmed. Time-outs, count confirmations, and specimen identification all rely on this principle. The surgical technologist who speaks up when something is wrong — even when it is uncomfortable — is performing one of the most important functions in patient safety.

docs.title: Scope of Practice
docs.text:
The scope of practice for surgical technologists varies by state. Some states have enacted specific statutes governing what STs may and may not do; others defer to institutional policy. It is the responsibility of every surgical technologist to know the laws of the state in which they practice.

Generally, surgical technologists in the scrub role may: - Set up and maintain the sterile field - Pass instruments and supplies to the surgeon - Hold retractors, cut sutures (when instructed), and assist with counts - Handle and label specimens (in coordination with the circulator)

Surgical technologists do not independently prescribe, diagnose, or perform unsupervised invasive procedures.

docs.title: Professional Ethics
docs.text: The AST Code of Ethics outlines the professional obligations of surgical technologists, including: - Placing the patient's welfare above all else - Maintaining the highest standards of aseptic technique - Maintaining patient confidentiality (HIPAA) - Refusing to participate in procedures they believe are unethical or unsafe - Continuing professional development

docs.title: HIPAA and Confidentiality
docs.text: All patient information encountered in the OR — diagnosis, procedure, personal details — is protected under the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA). Surgical technologists must never discuss patient information outside of clinical necessity, and must not document, photograph, or share patient identifiers in any unauthorized manner.

docs.title: Negligence and Liability
docs.text:
A surgical technologist can be held legally liable for negligent acts — actions that fall below the standard of care and result in patient harm. Common examples include: - Miscounting instruments, sponges, or sharps - Breaking sterile technique without disclosure - Administering the wrong medication to the sterile field - Failing to report a contamination event

Reporting errors and near-misses is a professional and ethical obligation, not an optional act.

docs.title: 1.6 Professionalism in the OR
docs.text:
The operating room is a high-stakes, high-stress environment. Professionalism — defined as the consistent application of skills, ethics, and conduct standards expected of a trained professional — is non-negotiable.

Professionalism includes: - Punctuality: OR cases begin on schedule; your absence affects the entire team and the patient - Appearance: surgical attire, PPE, and personal hygiene standards exist for patient protection - Communication: clear, concise, respectful communication saves lives - Emotional regulation: the OR is not a place for ego, panic, or interpersonal conflict - Lifelong learning: medicine evolves; the CST who stopped learning five years ago is already behind

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"The surgeon sees the anatomy. You see the whole room. Someone has to."

  • Know the procedure before you scrub in. Read the surgeon's preference card, review the anatomy, know which instruments come next. Anticipation is the mark of an expert scrub tech.
  • When in doubt about sterility — contaminate it. Never rationalize keeping a compromised item in the field because you're not sure it was actually contaminated.
  • A strong "surgical conscience" is worth more than any single technical skill.

Next: Chapter 2 — The Operating Room Environment

docs.title: 2.1 The Surgical Suite: An Overview
docs.text:
The operating room suite is a self-contained unit within the hospital designed to provide the most controlled, cleanest possible environment for invasive procedures. Everything about its design — from the floor plan to the air handling systems to the material of the walls — exists to reduce the risk of contamination and support the safe delivery of surgical care.

A typical surgical suite includes: - Individual operating rooms (ORs) - A scrub sink area or scrub bays adjacent to each OR - A substerile room (a small supply room between ORs) - A clean core or sterile processing dispatch area - A soiled utility room for contaminated instruments and waste - A preoperative holding area (pre-op) - A post-anesthesia care unit (PACU) - Staff lounges, changing rooms, and storage corridors

docs.title: 2.2 Traffic Flow and Zone Classification
docs.text: The OR suite uses a three-zone traffic system to control the movement of personnel and equipment and reduce the risk of cross-contamination.

docs.title: Zone 1: Unrestricted Zone
docs.text: The unrestricted zone includes the entrance to the surgical suite, corridors leading to it, and administrative areas. Standard hospital clothing is acceptable here. Patients on stretchers and family members may be in this zone. No special PPE is required beyond standard precautions.

docs.title: Zone 2: Semi-Restricted Zone
docs.text: The semi-restricted zone includes corridors adjacent to ORs, instrument processing areas, and storage rooms. In this zone: - Surgical scrub attire (scrubs) must be worn - All hair must be covered with a surgical cap or hood - Clean technique is maintained - Traffic should be purposeful and minimized

docs.title: Zone 3: Restricted Zone
docs.text: The restricted zone is the operating room itself and any directly connected areas (e.g., the scrub sink, substerile room). In this zone: - Surgical scrub attire is required - Head and facial hair must be covered - A surgical mask must be worn when the sterile field is open - Only necessary personnel may enter - Conversation and traffic are minimized to reduce airborne contamination

docs.title: Traffic Principles
docs.text:
• Traffic in and out of the OR during a case should be minimized. Every time the OR door opens, the pressure differential is disrupted and environmental particles can enter.
  • Personnel should not wander between rooms during active cases.
  • Doors should remain closed from the establishment of the sterile field until the patient exits the room.

docs.title: Temperature and Humidity
docs.text:
OR temperature is typically maintained between 68°F–75°F (20°C–24°C). This range: - Inhibits bacterial growth (bacteria thrive in warm, moist environments) - Reduces the metabolic oxygen demand in anesthetized patients - Supports comfort of gowned surgical team members

Relative humidity is maintained between 20%–60%. Low humidity raises the risk of static electricity, which can ignite volatile anesthetic agents. High humidity promotes microbial growth.

Neonatal and pediatric ORs are often set warmer to compensate for patients' limited thermoregulation.

docs.title: Air Exchange and Filtration
docs.text:
The OR is maintained at positive pressure relative to adjacent corridors. This means air flows out of the OR when doors open, rather than in — preventing unfiltered corridor air from entering the sterile environment.

OR air handling systems provide: - A minimum of 20 air exchanges per hour (typically 15 are fresh outside air) - Filtration through HEPA filters that remove ≥99.97% of particles ≥0.3 microns - Laminar airflow over the operative field in specialty ORs (orthopedic, cardiac), delivering a unidirectional downward stream of filtered air directly over the sterile field to further reduce airborne contamination

docs.title: OR Table
docs.text:
The operating room table (also called the surgical table) is the central piece of equipment in any OR. Modern OR tables are: - Electronically or hydraulically adjustable in height, tilt (Trendelenburg/reverse), lateral tilt (flexion/extension), and break - Equipped with a removable head section for positioning in certain neurosurgical and ENT cases - Compatible with numerous attachments: arm boards, stirrups, leg holders, chest rolls, kidney rests, and more

The table must be positioned so the surgeon, scrub tech, and anesthesia provider all have adequate access and sight lines.

docs.title: Overhead Surgical Lights
docs.text:
Surgical lights are designed to illuminate the operative field without casting shadows and without generating excessive heat. Modern LED surgical lights are: - Shadowless (multiple light sources from different angles) - Adjustable in focus, brightness, and color temperature - Equipped with sterile handles that can be adjusted by the scrubbed team

Light handles are covered with a sterile sleeve and can be positioned by the scrub tech or surgeon without breaking sterility.

docs.title: Back Table
docs.text: The back table is the large, draped, sterile table where all instruments, sutures, and supplies are organized by the scrub technologist before the procedure begins. Back table organization should be logical and consistent — instruments of the same type grouped together, with frequently used items accessible without searching.

docs.title: Mayo Stand
docs.text: The Mayo stand is a smaller, height-adjustable tray table positioned directly over the patient (or at the end of the table near the operative field). It holds the instruments that will be used most frequently and immediately during the current step of the procedure. The scrub tech loads the Mayo stand with only what is currently needed and refreshes it as the case progresses.

docs.title: Anesthesia Machine
docs.text:
The anesthesia workstation sits at the head of the OR table. It includes: - Ventilator (delivers controlled breaths) - Vaporizer (introduces volatile anesthetic agents into the breathing circuit) - Gas supply connections (O₂, N₂O, air) - Monitoring equipment (ECG, SpO₂, EtCO₂, blood pressure) - Drug storage and infusion pumps

The scrub tech does not interact directly with the anesthesia machine, but must know its location and never disrupt anesthesia lines or equipment.

docs.title: Electrosurgical Unit (ESU)
docs.text: The ESU (also called the "Bovie," after its inventor William Bovie) uses high-frequency alternating electrical current to cut tissue or coagulate bleeding vessels. It is one of the most frequently used devices in the OR. Full coverage is provided in Chapter 8 (Hemostasis).

docs.title: Suction
docs.text: At least one (often two) suction sources are available in every OR. Suction tubing runs from wall-mounted vacuum outlets to a canister, and then to the sterile field via suction tips (e.g., Yankauer, Poole, Frazier). The scrub tech manages the sterile end of the suction tubing.

docs.title: Additional Fixed Equipment
docs.text: Equipment Purpose X-ray view boxes / PACS screens Display imaging studies C-arm / fluoroscopy Intraoperative real-time imaging Microscope Magnification for microsurgery (neuro, ENT, ophthalmology) Laparoscopic tower Camera, light source, insufflator, monitor for minimally invasive cases Robotic system (e.g., da Vinci) Robotic-assisted minimally invasive surgery Cell saver Collects and reinfuses patient's own blood Tourniquet Controls blood flow to extremity during orthopedic procedures Nerve stimulator Monitors nerve integrity during at-risk procedures Warming devices Patient warming blankets (e.g., Bair Hugger) to prevent hypothermia

docs.title: 2.5 Surgical Attire
docs.text: All personnel entering the semi-restricted or restricted zones must wear surgical attire that reduces the shedding of skin particles (which carry microorganisms) into the environment.

docs.title: Standard Surgical Attire
docs.text:
• Scrub suit (top and pants): single-use or laundered on-site; should fit closely to minimize skin shedding
  • Surgical cap or hood: covers all head and facial hair completely
  • Surgical mask: fluid-resistant; covers nose and mouth; required in restricted zone when sterile field is open
  • Eye protection: safety glasses or face shield when splash risk is present
  • Shoe covers (policy varies by institution)

docs.title: Attire Rules
docs.text:
• Scrub attire worn outside the surgical suite should be changed before re-entering.
  • Jewelry (rings, bracelets, necklaces) that cannot be contained under gloves or attire should be removed.
  • Artificial nails and nail polish are prohibited in most surgical settings due to evidence of increased bacterial colonization.
  • Personal cell phones are prohibited in the restricted zone during cases.

docs.title: 2.6 Fire Safety in the Operating Room
docs.text:
The OR contains three elements of the fire triad: 1. Fuel: drapes, gowns, alcohol-based prep solutions, hair 2. Oxidizer: supplemental oxygen and nitrous oxide (enriched oxygen environments near the patient's face) 3. Ignition source: ESU (electrocautery), lasers, light cords, defibrillators

OR fires are rare but catastrophic. Every member of the surgical team must know how to prevent and respond to them.

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
• Allow alcohol-based prep solutions to fully dry (≥3 minutes) before draping or using electrosurgery.
  • Keep ESU active tip in a holster when not in use — never leave it on the drapes.
  • Minimize oxygen concentration near open flame risks (work with anesthesia to use the lowest effective FiO₂).
  • Use laser-resistant drapes and ETTs in laser airway cases.

docs.title: Response (RACE)
docs.text:
Step Action R — Rescue Remove the patient from immediate danger A — Alarm Activate the fire alarm; call for help C — Contain Close doors to limit fire spread E — Extinguish/Evacuate Extinguish if safe to do so; evacuate if not

If a fire occurs on the sterile drapes: 1. Remove burning drapes immediately 2. Cut/remove oxygen if near face 3. Smother with saline or CO₂ extinguisher 4. Alert the team

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"An organized room is a safe room. Chaos is a risk factor."

  • Before your first patient of the day, walk the room. Know where every piece of equipment is. Know how the OR table works. Know where the fire extinguisher is. Know how to reach the charge nurse.
  • Keep OR doors closed during cases — every opening disrupts the pressure differential and increases contamination risk.
  • If you are unsure about equipment function, ask before the case begins — not during a crisis.

Next: Chapter 3 — Microbiology and Infection Control

docs.title: 3.1 Microbiology Basics: A Surgical Perspective
docs.text: Microbiology is the study of organisms too small to be seen without magnification. For surgical technologists, microbiology is not an abstract science — it is the direct foundation for every aseptic practice performed in the OR. Understanding why we do what we do begins with understanding what we are fighting.

docs.title: Categories of Microorganisms
docs.text:
Bacteria are single-celled organisms and the primary causative agents of surgical site infections. They are classified in multiple ways:

By Gram stain: - Gram-positive: thick peptidoglycan cell wall retains crystal violet stain (purple); e.g., Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes - Gram-negative: thin peptidoglycan wall with outer lipopolysaccharide layer; pink stain; e.g., Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Klebsiella

By morphology: - Cocci: spherical (e.g., Staphylococcus, Streptococcus) - Bacilli: rod-shaped (e.g., Clostridium, E. coli) - Spirochetes: spiral-shaped (e.g., Treponema pallidum)

By oxygen requirement: - Aerobic: require oxygen to survive - Anaerobic: grow in the absence of oxygen (important in bowel cases, deep tissue infections) - Facultative anaerobes: can survive with or without oxygen

Viruses are not living cells — they are genetic material (DNA or RNA) enclosed in a protein coat. Viruses cannot be treated with antibiotics. Relevant examples include HIV, Hepatitis B (HBV), and Hepatitis C (HCV) — all bloodborne pathogens with direct implications for OR personnel safety.

Fungi include yeasts and molds. Candida is a common opportunistic fungal pathogen in immunocompromised surgical patients. Fungal infections post-surgery are often hospital-acquired and serious.

Prions are misfolded proteins associated with diseases like Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (CJD). Prions are extraordinary resistant to standard sterilization methods, including autoclaving. Suspected CJD cases require special instrument handling protocols.

docs.title: 3.2 Surgical Site Infections (SSIs)
docs.text: A surgical site infection (SSI) is an infection that occurs at or near a surgical incision within 30 days of the procedure (or within 90 days if an implant is involved). SSIs are one of the most common and costly healthcare-associated infections (HAIs).

docs.title: Classification of SSIs
docs.text: Type Description Superficial incisional Involves only skin and subcutaneous tissue Deep incisional Involves fascial and muscle layers Organ/space Involves any anatomy opened or manipulated during surgery (e.g., peritonitis, mediastinitis)

docs.title: Common SSI Pathogens
docs.text: Organism Notes Staphylococcus aureus Most common SSI pathogen; MRSA is a serious concern Coagulase-negative Staphylococcus Common in implant-related SSIs Enterococcus GI/GU procedures E. coli Abdominal/colorectal procedures Pseudomonas aeruginosa Resistant; associated with contaminated environments and immunocompromise

docs.title: SSI Risk Factors
docs.text:
Patient factors: - Diabetes mellitus (impaired immune response, poor tissue healing) - Obesity (poor vascularity in adipose tissue) - Smoking - Immunosuppression - Malnutrition - Prolonged preoperative hospital stay

Surgical factors: - Lengthy procedures (>2 hours) - Contaminated or dirty wound classification - Poor technique or excessive tissue trauma - Inadequate hemostasis - Foreign material left in wound

docs.title: SSI Prevention Bundle
docs.text: Evidence-based practices shown to reduce SSI rates: - Prophylactic antibiotic administration within 60 minutes before incision (vancomycin requires 120 minutes) - Antibiotic redosing for prolonged cases - Hair removal with clippers (never razors — razors cause microabrasions that increase infection risk) - Normothermia maintenance (hypothermia impairs immune function) - Glucose control in diabetic and non-diabetic patients - Chlorhexidine gluconate (CHG) patient skin prep - Sterile technique

docs.title: 3.3 Chain of Infection
docs.text:
For an infection to occur, six conditions must exist in sequence. This is called the chain of infection. Breaking any link in the chain prevents infection.

Infectious Agent → Reservoir → Portal of Exit → Mode of Transmission → Portal of Entry → Susceptible Host
Link Example in the OR Infectious agent S. aureus on a personnel member's skin Reservoir Nasal colonization of OR staff Portal of exit Shedding of skin cells, sneezing Mode of transmission Airborne droplets contaminating the sterile field Portal of entry Open surgical wound Susceptible host Immunocompromised patient

Every aseptic practice in the OR targets one or more links in this chain.

docs.title: 3.4 Modes of Transmission
docs.text: Understanding how pathogens travel helps explain why OR practices exist.

docs.title: Contact Transmission
docs.text:
• Direct contact: physical transfer between source and host (e.g., contaminated hand touching a wound)
  • Indirect contact: transfer via a contaminated intermediate object (fomite) such as an instrument, drape, or countertop

Contact transmission is the most common route for SSIs and HAIs. Hand hygiene and aseptic technique directly interrupt it.

docs.title: Droplet Transmission
docs.text: Large respiratory droplets (>5 microns) travel short distances (<3 feet) and fall quickly. Examples: influenza, Streptococcus. Surgical masks protect against droplet transmission — both from personnel to patient and patient to personnel.

docs.title: Airborne Transmission
docs.text: Small particles (≤5 microns) remain suspended in air for extended periods and can travel long distances. Examples: tuberculosis (Mycobacterium tuberculosis), measles, varicella. N95 respirators (not standard surgical masks) are required for airborne precautions.

docs.title: Vector-Borne Transmission
docs.text: Transmission via insects or animals (e.g., mosquitoes, ticks). Not a primary concern in the OR setting.

docs.title: 3.5 Standard Precautions
docs.text: Standard precautions are the minimum infection control practices applied to all patients, regardless of their known or suspected infectious status. The principle is that all blood, body fluids, secretions, excretions (except sweat), non-intact skin, and mucous membranes are treated as potentially infectious.

docs.title: Components of Standard Precautions
docs.text:
Hand Hygiene The single most effective infection control intervention. In the OR: - Use alcohol-based hand rub (ABHR) when hands are not visibly soiled - Use soap and water when hands are visibly soiled or after exposure to C. difficile spores (alcohol does not kill spores) - Perform hand hygiene before and after every patient contact, after glove removal, after touching environmental surfaces

Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) | PPE | When Used | |-----|-----------| | Gloves | When touching blood, body fluids, or mucous membranes | | Gown | When splashing or soiling of clothing is anticipated | | Mask | When working in restricted zone with open sterile field; when patient has respiratory illness | | Eye protection | When splash or spray is possible | | Face shield | Covers entire face; used when extensive splash risk is present |

Safe Injection Practices - Never recap needles with two hands; use a scoop technique or safety device - Use single-use vials whenever possible - Label all medications on the sterile field immediately upon receipt

Sharps Safety Needlestick injuries represent one of the most significant occupational hazards for surgical personnel. Prevention strategies: - Never pass sharps hand-to-hand; use a neutral zone (kidney basin) on the field - Announce sharps before passing ("scalpel coming") - Use blunt-tip suture needles when indicated - Count all sharps meticulously

docs.title: 3.6 Transmission-Based Precautions
docs.text:
When a patient's known or suspected pathogen requires precautions beyond standard, transmission-based precautions are implemented. In the OR, these are implemented in addition to — not instead of — standard precautions.

Precaution Type Route PPE Required OR Implications Contact Direct/indirect contact Gown + gloves Last case of the day if possible; dedicated equipment; terminal clean Droplet Respiratory droplets Surgical mask Standard OR mask is sufficient Airborne Airborne particles N95 respirator Negative pressure room if available; fit-tested N95 required

docs.title: MRSA (Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus)
docs.text: MRSA is a contact precaution pathogen with significant surgical implications. MRSA colonization is common (approximately 1–2% of the population) and can result in serious SSIs. Preoperative MRSA screening (nasal swabs) and decolonization with mupirocin ointment and CHG bathing is used at many institutions before elective surgery.

docs.title: 3.7 Surgical Hand Antisepsis
docs.text: Before gowning and gloving, the scrub technologist performs surgical hand antisepsis — a more thorough process than routine hand hygiene, designed to reduce transient flora and suppress the growth of resident flora for the duration of glove wear.

docs.title: Traditional Surgical Scrub
docs.text: Uses an antiseptic soap (chlorhexidine gluconate or povidone-iodine) and a scrub brush or pick: 1. Remove jewelry, inspect hands and nails (no cuts, no nail polish, no artificial nails) 2. Pre-wash hands and forearms with water 3. Clean under nails with nail pick 4. Apply antiseptic agent; use timed scrub (3–5 minutes per institution policy) OR counted stroke method 5. Scrub each surface of each finger, hand, and forearm to 2 inches above the elbow 6. Rinse hands and forearms, fingers up, keeping hands above elbows 7. Dry with sterile towel, using a patting motion from fingertips to elbows

docs.title: Alcohol-Based Surgical Hand Rub (ABHR)
docs.text: An increasingly common alternative, validated by studies to be as effective as traditional scrub: 1. Pre-wash with soap and water on the first scrub of the day; dry thoroughly 2. Clean under fingernails 3. Apply ABHR per manufacturer instructions, covering all surfaces of hands and forearms 4. Allow to dry completely before gowning and gloving (do not wipe dry) 5. Repeat application per product instructions

docs.title: Key Principles
docs.text:
• Hands must remain higher than elbows during and after scrubbing to prevent runoff contamination
  • Never re-contaminate scrubbed hands by touching unscrubbed surfaces
  • If contamination occurs after scrubbing, rescrub

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"The organism doesn't know it's not supposed to be there. You have to make sure it never gets the chance."

  • The most common SSI pathogen (S. aureus) lives on the skin of approximately 30% of the healthy population. Your prep technique, your sterile field, and your technique matter.
  • Contact precaution cases should ideally be scheduled last if possible, allowing a terminal cleaning of the room afterward.
  • When in doubt about the infectious status of a patient, apply standard precautions fully. You cannot tell by looking who is colonized with MRSA or who has an undiagnosed bloodborne pathogen.

Next: Chapter 4 — Sterilization and Disinfection

docs.title: 4.1 Defining the Hierarchy
docs.text: The terms sterilization, disinfection, and cleaning are often used loosely, but they represent very distinct levels of microbial control with different indications, processes, and outcomes.

docs.title: Sterilization
docs.text: Sterilization destroys all microorganisms, including the most resistant bacterial and fungal spores. An item is either sterile or it is not — there is no partial sterilization. Items that enter sterile body cavities or the bloodstream must be sterile.

docs.title: Disinfection
docs.text: Disinfection destroys most pathogenic organisms but does not reliably kill all spores.
