Disinfection is classified by level: - High-level disinfection (HLD): kills all organisms except high numbers of spores; used for semi-critical items - Intermediate-level disinfection: kills mycobacteria, most viruses, bacteria, some fungi; not sporicidal - Low-level disinfection: kills most bacteria and some viruses; used for non-critical surfaces

docs.title: Cleaning (Sanitization)
docs.text: Cleaning removes visible debris, organic material, and reduces the microbial load. Cleaning must always precede sterilization or disinfection — organic material (blood, protein) can shield microorganisms from sterilants and disinfectants. A dirty instrument cannot be reliably sterilized.

docs.title: 4.2 The Spaulding Classification System
docs.text:
In 1972, Earle Spaulding developed a rational framework for determining the level of reprocessing required for medical devices and equipment. The system classifies items based on the risk of infection if the item is contaminated.

Category Definition Risk Required Processing Examples Critical Enters sterile tissue or the vascular system Highest Sterilization Scalpels, scissors, bone instruments, IV catheters, implants Semi-critical Contacts intact mucous membranes or non-intact skin Intermediate High-Level Disinfection (sterilization preferred) Laryngoscope blades, flexible endoscopes, ET tubes Non-critical Contacts intact skin only Low Low- to intermediate-level disinfection Blood pressure cuffs, ECG leads, bed rails, stethoscopes

The Spaulding system remains the foundation of U.S. instrument reprocessing guidelines from the CDC and professional organizations.

docs.title: 4.3 Instrument Reprocessing Workflow
docs.text: All surgical instruments follow a defined reprocessing pathway from contamination to sterile storage. This process is managed by sterile processing (central processing) technicians, but surgical technologists must understand it to ensure proper instrument care at point of use.

docs.title: Step 1: Point-of-Use Treatment
docs.text: At the end of the case: - Remove gross soil from instruments using sterile water or saline (do not use normal saline for prolonged soaking — it is corrosive) - Keep instruments moist to prevent biofilm formation (use a moist towel or enzymatic foam spray) - Transport instruments in a closed, leak-proof container to sterile processing

docs.title: Step 2: Decontamination
docs.text: In sterile processing: - Instruments are manually or mechanically cleaned - Automated washer-disinfectors clean and thermally disinfect instruments using high-temperature water - Ultrasonic cleaners use high-frequency sound waves to remove debris from complex surfaces and lumens - Manual scrubbing under water (never above water, to prevent aerosolization of contaminants)

docs.title: Step 3: Inspection and Assembly
docs.text: After cleaning: - Instruments are inspected for functionality, cracks, corrosion, and debris - Instruments are lubricated as needed ("milk") - Sets are assembled per standardized instrument count sheets

docs.title: Step 4: Packaging
docs.text:
Instruments are wrapped or pouched for sterilization: - Woven/non-woven wraps: wrapped in layers using a sequential wrap technique - Peel pouches: single instruments or small sets; transparent for identification - Rigid containers: reusable systems with filter-equipped lids; common for complex instrument sets

All packaging must be labeled with: contents, lot/load number, sterilizer number, expiration or process date, and technician identifier.

docs.title: Step 5: Sterilization
docs.text: See Section 4.4.

docs.title: Step 6: Storage and Distribution
docs.text: Sterile items are stored in a clean, dry, closed area away from traffic and moisture. Items are retrieved and delivered to ORs based on case schedules.

docs.title: Steam Sterilization (Autoclaving)
docs.text:
The most common, cost-effective, and well-validated method of sterilization. Uses saturated steam under pressure to kill microorganisms by denaturating proteins.

Parameters for standard gravity cycle: - Temperature: 250°F (121°C) - Pressure: 15 psi - Time: 15–30 minutes

Parameters for pre-vacuum (dynamic air removal) cycle: - Temperature: 270°F (132°C) - Time: 4–10 minutes (faster due to superior steam penetration)

Advantages: fast, inexpensive, non-toxic residue, highly reliable Disadvantages: not compatible with heat-sensitive instruments (some plastics, electronics, delicate optics)

Immediate Use Steam Sterilization (IUSS) — formerly called "flash sterilization": - Used for unwrapped instruments needed urgently (e.g., a dropped instrument) - IUSS items are delivered immediately to the field — they cannot be stored - Should not be used routinely as a substitute for proper sterilization - Must be documented and justified

docs.title: Ethylene Oxide (EtO) Gas
docs.text:
Low-temperature sterilization using ethylene oxide gas. Penetrates packaging and sterilizes at room temperature or slightly above.

Parameters: 55°C–60°C, 30–50% relative humidity, several hours (plus a lengthy aeration period to off-gas toxic residue)

Advantages: compatible with heat-sensitive items, excellent penetration Disadvantages: carcinogenic gas; long cycle times; items require aeration before use (up to 8–12 hours); environmental and personnel safety concerns

Indications: flexible endoscopes (some types), cameras, power equipment, and other heat-sensitive items.

docs.title: Hydrogen Peroxide Gas Plasma (Sterrad)
docs.text:
Low-temperature sterilization using vaporized hydrogen peroxide energized into a plasma state.

Cycle time: 24–75 minutes Temperature: 45°C–55°C

Advantages: no toxic residuals (breaks down to water and oxygen), fast turnaround, safe for most heat-sensitive items Disadvantages: not compatible with cellulose (paper, linen), liquids, or items with very long lumens; more expensive per cycle

Indications: cameras, powered instruments, flexible scopes (select types), electronic equipment

docs.title: Peracetic Acid (Steris System)
docs.text:
Liquid chemical sterilization using peracetic acid (diluted acetic acid with hydrogen peroxide). Used for immediate use sterilization of heat-sensitive items submerged in the liquid.

Cycle time: ~30 minutes Temperature: 50°C–56°C

Note: Items processed by peracetic acid cannot be stored; they must be used immediately. Items must be processed without packaging.

docs.title: Dry Heat
docs.text:
Uses hot air (160°C–180°C) for extended periods. Used for items that cannot be exposed to moisture (e.g., anhydrous oils, powders, glassware).

Disadvantages: very long cycle times, damages many plastics; limited use in modern surgical settings

docs.title: 4.5 Sterilization Monitoring
docs.text: No sterilization process is infallible. Monitoring systems verify that sterilization parameters were met.

docs.title: Mechanical/Physical Indicators
docs.text: Time, temperature, and pressure recordings from the sterilizer itself (printouts, digital logs). Reviewed with each cycle to confirm parameters were achieved.

docs.title: Chemical Indicators (CIs)
docs.text:
Chemical indicators change color or configuration when exposed to sterilization parameters. They are placed inside and outside every package.

Class Description Examples Class 1 Process indicators Autoclave tape (stripes appear when exposed to steam) Class 2 Specific test indicators Bowie-Dick test (pre-vacuum steam) Class 3 Single-parameter React to one parameter only Class 4 Multi-parameter React to multiple parameters Class 5 Integrating indicators Respond to all critical parameters; most reliable CIs Class 6 Emulating indicators Verified for specific cycles; very high performance

Important: A chemical indicator confirms exposure to sterilization conditions — it does NOT confirm sterility. It is a process indicator, not a sterility guarantee.

docs.title: Biological Indicators (BIs)
docs.text:
The gold standard of sterilization monitoring. Contains live, resistant spores: - Steam sterilization: Geobacillus stearothermophilus spores - EtO sterilization: Bacillus atrophaeus spores

BIs are incubated after cycling. If spores survive, the sterilization cycle failed and all items in that load must be recalled.

Frequency of BI use: - At least weekly for each sterilizer - With every load containing implants - After any sterilizer malfunction or repair

docs.title: Load Recall
docs.text: If a BI returns positive (indicating sterilization failure): - The sterilizer is taken out of service - All items from potentially affected loads are recalled - Items recalled must be reprocessed before use - The event is documented and investigated

docs.title: 4.6 Event-Related vs. Time-Related Sterility
docs.text:
Historically, sterile packages were given expiration dates based on time (e.g., "good for 30 days"). Current evidence supports event-related sterility — the concept that a properly packaged, intact sterile item remains sterile indefinitely, assuming: - The package is intact (no tears, moisture, holes) - It was stored in appropriate conditions (clean, dry, closed area) - The package was not improperly handled

Under this system, expiration dates are still applied as an internal audit mechanism, but package integrity inspection is the primary determinant of sterility on any given day.

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"Clean before you sterilize. A dirty instrument wrapped in a sterilization pouch is still a dirty instrument."

  • Always inspect packaging before opening. Wet packages, torn packaging, or damaged containers must be considered non-sterile and returned for reprocessing.
  • Never use a chemical indicator result alone to release items from quarantine after a suspected sterilization failure — wait for the BI result.
  • If an IUSS item is processed for an emergency, document the reason. Routine IUSS bypasses important quality controls and should be a last resort.

Next: Chapter 5 — The Sterile Field

docs.title: 5.1 What Is the Sterile Field?
docs.text:
The sterile field is the designated area of the operating room that has been established as free of microorganisms. It includes: - The draped patient - The draped back table - The draped Mayo stand - All sterile instruments, supplies, and equipment within the field - The gowns and gloves of scrubbed personnel from the chest to table level and from cuffs to elbows

The sterile field exists to protect the patient from exogenous sources of infection during surgery — it is the physical barrier between the patient's open tissues and the non-sterile world.

docs.title: 5.2 Principles of Aseptic Technique
docs.text: The following principles govern behavior in and around the sterile field. They are not optional guidelines — they are the rules by which every action in the OR is judged. Every member of the surgical team is responsible for upholding them.

docs.title: Principle 1: Only Sterile Items May Be Placed on the Sterile Field
docs.text: Items must be confirmed sterile before being placed on or transferred to the sterile field. Package integrity must be inspected. Any item of questionable sterility must be removed.

docs.title: Principle 2: Sterile Persons Touch Only Sterile Items
docs.text: A scrubbed and gowned surgical technologist or surgeon may only contact sterile surfaces. Reaching across the non-sterile field, touching the back of the gown, or contacting the circulator's hands breaks this rule.

docs.title: Principle 3: Unsterile Persons Avoid the Sterile Field
docs.text: Non-scrubbed personnel must not reach over or lean across the sterile field. The circulating nurse passes items to the field from a distance — never standing over or touching sterile surfaces.

docs.title: Principle 4: The Sterile Field Is Continuously Monitored
docs.text: Once established, the sterile field must be observed and not left unattended. If a sterile field is unattended and its sterility cannot be confirmed, it is considered contaminated.

docs.title: Principle 5: Sterile Drapes Define the Field
docs.text: Only the top surface of a sterile drape is considered sterile. Anything that falls below table level is considered contaminated. Items that drop below the table edge must not be retrieved and returned to the field.

docs.title: Principle 6: The Edges of Sterile Packaging Are Not Sterile
docs.text: When opening sterile packages, the outer edge (the peel flap, the fold) is considered the boundary between sterile and non-sterile. Contents are placed onto the field without allowing the package to touch the field, or by allowing the contents to drop without contact with non-sterile surfaces.

docs.title: Principle 7: Moisture Is the Enemy of Sterility (Strike-Through)
docs.text: A sterile barrier wet with any liquid allows microorganisms to wick through from non-sterile surfaces — this is called strike-through contamination. Wet sterile drapes, wet packaging materials, or wet gown surfaces over non-sterile areas are considered contaminated.

docs.title: Principle 8: Movement Around the Sterile Field Is Controlled
docs.text: Scrubbed personnel face the sterile field and move past each other front-to-front or back-to-back. Non-scrubbed personnel pass behind scrubbed personnel. Turning your back to the sterile field is a contamination risk.

docs.title: Principle 9: Talking, Coughing, and Sneezing Near the Field Are Risks
docs.text: Respiratory droplets can contaminate the sterile field. Minimizing unnecessary conversation, using proper masking, and directing airflow away from the field are important habits.

docs.title: Principle 10: When in Doubt — Contaminate It
docs.text: If any question exists about whether an item, surface, or person has been contaminated, it must be treated as contaminated. There is no "probably fine" in aseptic technique.

docs.title: Timing
docs.text: The sterile field should be established as close to the time of patient skin incision as practical. Long delays between setup and incision increase the time available for contamination. Most facilities allow sterile fields to stand for a case day if continuously monitored and uncovered; institution policy governs maximum allowable times.

docs.title: Back Table Setup
docs.text:
The scrub technologist dons sterile gown and gloves, then sets up the back table in a systematic, organized manner:

  1. Open the back table pack and drape the table with a sterile barrier
  2. Open instrument sets onto the table (by the circulator or by pulling from peel packs)
  3. Organize instruments by category: cutting/dissecting, grasping/clamping, retraction, suction, specialty items
  4. Count sponges, sharps, and instruments with the circulator (initial count)
  5. Set up suture, electrosurgery equipment, and specialty supplies
  6. Load the Mayo stand with instruments for the beginning of the case

docs.title: Opening Sterile Supplies
docs.text:
The circulating nurse opens sterile supplies and transfers them to the sterile field using one of two methods:

Method 1 — Drop technique (peel pouches) The circulator peels the package open and "presents" the opening toward the scrub tech, who lifts the item out without touching the package edges. Or, the item is allowed to drop directly onto the field from above.

Method 2 — Flip technique (wrapped packages) The circulator unfolds the outer wrap, holding the corners away from the sterile interior, and either presents or drops the inner-wrapped item to the sterile field.

Critical rules: - The outer 1-inch border of any wrapping is considered non-sterile - The circulator never reaches across or contacts the sterile field - Items are never dropped from excessive height (risk of bouncing or uncontrolled landing) - Pouring sterile liquids into containers on the sterile field requires the circulator to pour without touching the sterile container; pour from a close height to prevent splash

docs.title: 5.4 Gowning and Gloving
docs.text: Donning a sterile gown and gloves is one of the fundamental skills of surgical technology. It must be performed correctly, without contamination, every time.

docs.title: Gowning Procedure
docs.text:
1. Complete surgical hand antisepsis (scrub or ABHR)
  2. Enter the OR with hands above the waist, not touching any surface
  3. Approach the sterile back table; the gown is folded inside-out and presented by the circulator or pre-placed on the back table
  4. Grasp the gown at the inside neckline; step back from the table
  5. Allow the gown to unfold without touching the outside surfaces
  6. Slide both arms into the sleeves simultaneously, keeping hands inside the gown cuffs (for closed gloving)
  7. The circulator ties the back of the gown

docs.title: Closed Gloving Technique (Preferred)
docs.text:
Hands remain inside the gown sleeves throughout the gloving process — the gown cuff is never breached.

  1. Using the sleeve-covered right hand, pick up the left glove from the inner glove wrapper
  2. Place the glove palm-down on the left sleeve cuff, fingers pointing toward the elbow
  3. Using sleeve-covered fingers, fold the glove cuff over the gown cuff
  4. Push the left hand through the gown cuff and into the glove simultaneously
  5. Repeat with the right hand, using the gloved left hand to assist

docs.title: Open Gloving Technique
docs.text:
Used when gloving without a sterile gown (e.g., changing a glove during a case) or when adding gloves at certain junctures.

  1. Pick up the first glove by the cuff with the opposite hand
  2. Slide the dominant hand into the glove without touching the outside
  3. With the gloved hand, slide fingers under the cuff of the second glove
  4. Slide the second hand into the second glove
  5. Adjust both gloves for fit without contaminating the glove surfaces (touch only the sterile outer surface)

docs.title: Assisted Gloving
docs.text: An already-gowned and gloved team member can assist another in gloving: 1. The assisting tech holds the glove open, thumb tucked away 2. The recipient slides their hand into the glove 3. The assisting tech rolls the glove cuff up over the gown sleeve

docs.title: Removing Contaminated Gloves (Gown and Glove Removal)
docs.text: At the end of a case: 1. Remove gown first (circulator pulls from behind), allowing gown to roll inward 2. Remove gloves by grasping the outside of one glove near the wrist, peeling it off inside out 3. Slide finger of ungloved hand under the remaining glove cuff (touching only the inside) 4. Peel off the second glove inside out, enclosing the first

docs.title: 5.5 Maintaining the Sterile Field During the Case
docs.text:
Once the case begins, the scrub technologist's primary role is to maintain the sterile field and pass instruments efficiently. Key ongoing responsibilities:

Instrument management: - Keep Mayo stand organized and current to the step of the procedure - Remove used instruments from the field promptly to reduce clutter - Anticipate the surgeon's next move and pre-load suture or set up the next instrument before being asked

Handling sharps: - Maintain a neutral zone (e.g., kidney basin or emesis basin) for passing scalpels and needles - Never pass sharps hand-to-hand - Announce sharps: "Scalpel" or "Needle back" - Keep used needles on a magnetic needle mat or in a needle box; count at intervals

Suction and cord management: - Secure suction tubing, electrosurgery cords, and light cables to the drapes using clamps - Ensure cords are not draped over sterile surfaces in ways that risk contamination - Maintain suction tip in a clean location when not in active use

Sponge discipline: - Sponges on the field must always be accounted for - Tally sponge counts in groups of five (standard packaging) - Bloody sponges should be collected in a defined area and counted before discarding the field count

docs.title: 5.6 Recognizing and Responding to Contamination
docs.text: Contamination events will occur in every surgical career. The mark of a professional is not whether contamination happens — it's whether it is recognized, announced, and corrected.

docs.title: Common Contamination Events
docs.text: Event Correct Response Scrubbed person touches non-sterile surface Announce contamination; change gown and/or gloves Sterile item falls below table level Do not retrieve; discard; open replacement Package found wet or torn Consider non-sterile; remove from field; replace Hole found in glove Change glove immediately; inform surgeon Unknown person touches sterile drape Consider contaminated; redress field Circulator leans over field Call out; assess extent; treat as contaminated

docs.title: Announcing Contamination
docs.text:
When contamination occurs, the person who observes it must speak up — immediately and clearly:

"The field is contaminated — the back table was brushed. I need to redress."

This requires surgical conscience: the willingness to prioritize patient safety over the convenience of the moment, even when it slows the case, even when you are the one who caused the contamination.

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"Your sterile field is only as good as the weakest moment in which you failed to speak up."

  • When setting up the back table, organize instruments the same way every time. Consistency becomes muscle memory, and muscle memory frees your mind to anticipate the case.
  • If you are unsure whether you touched something non-sterile, change your glove. The patient cannot speak for themselves — you must speak for them.
  • Maintain visual contact with your sterile field whenever possible. An unmonitored sterile field is a question mark.

Next: Chapter 6 — Surgical Instrumentation

docs.title: 6.1 Instrument Classification by Function
docs.text: Surgical instruments are grouped by what they do. A thorough understanding of these categories allows the scrub technologist to anticipate surgical needs and organize the sterile field logically.

docs.title: Category 1: Cutting and Dissecting Instruments
docs.text:
These instruments divide tissue. They are among the sharpest items on the back table and demand careful handling.

Scalpel (Knife) The scalpel is the primary cutting instrument. It consists of a handle and a disposable blade.

Common handles: - #3 handle: standard; accepts blades 10, 11, 12, 15 - #4 handle: longer/heavier; accepts blades 20–25 - #7 handle: thin/long; accepts blades 10–15

Common blades: | Blade | Shape | Common Use | |-------|-------|-----------| | #10 | Large, curved | General skin incision | | #11 | Pointed/triangular | Stab incisions, abscess drainage | | #12 | Hook-shaped | ENT, specialized dissection | | #15 | Small, curved | Delicate dissection, pediatric | | #20–25 | Large curved | Abdominal, orthopedic |

Mounting and passing blades: Always mount and remove blades with a needle driver or hemostat — never with bare fingers. Pass the loaded scalpel handle in the neutral zone.

Scissors Scissors are used for cutting tissue, suture, and mesh.

Scissors Description Use Mayo scissors Heavy; straight or curved Cutting fascia, heavy suture Metzenbaum scissors Fine, curved or straight Delicate tissue dissection Iris scissors Tiny, straight or curved Ophthalmic, microsurgery Bandage scissors Angled blade to protect skin Cutting dressings (non-sterile) Suture scissors Short-blade with blunt tips Cutting suture on the field

Rule: Never use tissue scissors to cut suture and never use suture scissors on tissue. Cutting suture dulls the blades quickly, degrading their performance on delicate tissue.

Electrosurgical Instruments While technically energized devices (covered in Chapter 8), active electrode tips — pencil tips, ball electrodes — are cutting/coagulating instruments managed on the sterile field.

docs.title: Category 2: Grasping and Holding Instruments
docs.text:
These instruments hold or manipulate tissue and other materials.

Thumb (Tissue) Forceps Held like a pencil; spring-loaded; no locking mechanism. Available in multiple jaw configurations: | Forceps | Jaw Type | Use | |---------|----------|-----| | Adson | Toothed | Skin grasping | | Tissue (Standard) | 1×2 teeth | General tissue | | Russian | Rounded, serrated | Tissue grasping | | DeBakey | Fine serrations, atraumatic | Vascular; bowel | | Smooth (dressing) | No teeth | Dressings, packing |

Clamps (Hemostats and Occlusion Clamps) Ring-handled instruments with a ratchet lock; used to clamp vessels, pedicles, or tissue.

Clamp Description Use Mosquito hemostat Very small; curved or straight Small vessels, small tissue pedicles Kelly hemostat Medium; straight or curved Medium vessels Crile hemostat Similar to Kelly; slightly longer jaw General hemostasis Kocher clamp Heavy; toothed tip Dense tissue, fascia Allis clamp Multiple interlocking teeth Grasping tissue to be excised Babcock clamp Smooth, fenestrated jaw Atraumatic bowel/tube grasping Pennington clamp Triangular fenestrated jaw Rectal, bowel tissue Sponge forceps (ring forceps) Long ring handles, ring jaws Holding sponges; skin prep

Towel Clamps (Perforating and Non-Perforating) Secure drapes to each other or (if perforating) to the patient's skin. Non-perforating (Backhaus) clamps are standard for most draping.

Needle Holders Specifically designed to grip suture needles: | Holder | Description | Use | |--------|-------------|-----| | Mayo-Hegar | Heavy, standard length | General closure | | Crile-Wood | Similar to Mayo-Hegar | General use | | Webster | Fine; shorter | Delicate/plastic surgery | | Castroviejo | Spring-action; very fine | Ophthalmology, microsurgery |

docs.title: Category 3: Retraction Instruments
docs.text:
Retractors expose the operative field by holding tissue out of the surgeon's line of view.

Hand-held Retractors | Retractor | Description | Use | |-----------|-------------|-----| | Army-Navy | Double-ended, S-curved | Skin and subcutaneous tissue | | Richardson | Hooked blade, various sizes | Abdominal wall, fascia | | Deaver | Curved, narrow blade | Deep abdominal retraction | | Ribbon (Malleable) | Flat, flexible, adjustable | Custom-shaped retraction | | Rake (Volkmann) | Multiple prong tips | Skin edges | | Vein retractor | Small right-angle blade | Vascular exposure | | Senn retractor | Double-ended: curved blade + rake | Superficial tissue |

Self-Retaining Retractors Hold themselves open without a hand-holder, freeing assistants for other tasks. | Retractor | Description | Use | |-----------|-------------|-----| | Weitlaner | Spring-loaded, sharp-tipped | Small incisions; orthopedic | | Gelpi | Spring-loaded; sharp self-retaining | Orthopedic, spinal | | Balfour | Frame with interchangeable blades | Abdominal retraction | | Bookwalter | Modular ring and blade system | Large abdominal/pelvic cases | | O'Sullivan-O'Connor | Ring retractor for pelvic cases | OB/GYN | | Cerebellar (Meyerding) | Spine retractor | Spinal surgery |

docs.title: Category 4: Suction Instruments
docs.text: Remove blood, fluid, and debris from the operative field. | Tip | Shape | Use | |-----|-------|-----| | Yankauer | Rigid, bulb tip | Oropharynx, general suctioning | | Frazier | Angled, narrow | Neurosurgery, ENT, small spaces | | Poole | Large barrel with perforated sleeve | Abdominal flooding | | Baron | Short, angled | Ear surgery |

docs.title: Category 5: Visualization Instruments
docs.text: Specula and Scopes | Instrument | Use | |-----------|-----| | Nasal speculum | Dilates nares for nasal examination/surgery | | Vaginal speculum | Dilates vaginal vault | | Ear speculum | Canal visualization | | Laparoscope | Camera inserted through trocar for MIS | | Arthroscope | Joint visualization | | Cystoscope | Bladder visualization |

docs.title: 6.2 Instrument Naming Conventions
docs.text:
Most instrument names follow one of these patterns: - Eponym: named after the person who designed it (e.g., Kelly, DeBakey, Allis, Deaver, Babcock) - Descriptive: describes the instrument's appearance or function (e.g., right-angle clamp, ribbon retractor, sponge forceps) - Combined: eponym + description (e.g., Crile hemostat, Mayo scissors)

Learning eponyms is a matter of repetition and context. Focus on function first — know what the instrument does — then match the name to the tool.

docs.title: 6.3 Passing Instruments
docs.text: Passing instruments efficiently and safely is one of the most important practical skills of the scrub technologist.

docs.title: Principles of Instrument Passing
docs.text:
• Anticipate: know the procedure; have the next instrument ready before it's requested
  • Firm hand-off: place the instrument firmly in the surgeon's palm in a position of function — they should be able to use it immediately without repositioning
  • Safe: sharps in the neutral zone; never palm-to-palm for knives or needles
  • Silent communication: a well-timed hand-off without prompting builds trust and flow

docs.title: Passing Specific Instrument Types
docs.text:
Clamps and scissors: Handle first, into the ring finger and thumb of the surgeon's dominant hand. Curved instruments should curve away from the surgeon.

Tissue forceps: Grasp the forceps near the box (pivot), place handles into surgeon's palm.

Scalpel: Place in neutral zone or, if neutral zone is not in use, handle-first with blade down and away.

Suture on needle holder: Needle loaded in needle holder at 2/3 down the needle; pass needle holder with suture tails draped away from the field; control the needle's free tip.

Retractors: Pass with the blade oriented toward the tissue to be retracted, handle toward the surgeon.

docs.title: 6.4 Surgical Counts
docs.text: Surgical counts are a critical patient safety mechanism. A retained surgical item (RSI) — any instrument, sponge, or sharp left inside a patient — represents a serious preventable harm.

docs.title: What Is Counted
docs.text:
• Soft goods: surgical sponges (laps, raytecs, tonsil balls, cottonoids/neuro patties)
  • Sharps: hypodermic needles, suture needles, scalpel blades, electrosurgery tips, knife handles
  • Instruments: all instruments on the back table and Mayo stand at the start of the case; any added during the case

docs.title: When Counts Are Performed
docs.text:
1. Initial count: before the procedure begins; establishes baseline for all items
  2. Closing count: when closure of a body cavity begins
  3. Final count: when skin closure begins
  4. Any time a count discrepancy is suspected
  5. Before and after adding any items to the field mid-case

docs.title: Count Procedure
docs.text: The scrub technologist and circulating nurse count together, simultaneously viewing each item: - Items are separated and counted one by one (not in stacks) - Sponges are opened and inspected for integrity - Counts are called out loud and confirmed by both parties - The circulator records the count on the count board or in the electronic record

docs.title: Count Discrepancy
docs.text:
If the final count does not reconcile: 1. Recount all items on the field and in the room 2. Search the wound, under the drapes, in the suction canisters, and in the trash 3. If still unreconciled, obtain an intraoperative X-ray to rule out a retained item 4. Do not close until the discrepancy is resolved or an X-ray clears the cavity

All discrepancies must be documented and reported per institutional policy, regardless of whether the item is found.

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"Know what's on your field. At all times. If you can't account for it, find it."

  • Keep your back table organized consistently. Moving things around between cases causes hesitation — hesitation in a crisis costs seconds.
  • When handing a needle holder, always be aware of where the free needle tip is pointing.
  • If a surgeon asks for an instrument you don't recognize, do not guess — ask for clarification. A wrong guess handed confidently can go somewhere it shouldn't.
  • Read the preference card before every case. Surgeons have preferences because they matter.

Next: Chapter 7 — Sutures, Needles, and Wound Closure Devices

docs.title: Chapter 7: Sutures, Needles, and Wound Closure Devices
7.1 Suture Classification
docs.text: Sutures are classified along three axes: absorbability, origin, and structure.

docs.title: By Absorbability
docs.text:
Absorbable sutures are digested by body enzymes or hydrolysis over days to months. They are used in tissue that will heal to the point of not requiring permanent support, or in locations where suture removal is not practical.

Non-absorbable sutures resist enzymatic digestion and remain indefinitely unless physically removed. They are used for permanent tissue approximation, vessel ligation, or in tissues that heal slowly.

docs.title: By Origin
docs.text:
Natural (biological) sutures are derived from organic sources: - Plain gut: derived from sheep submucosa or beef serosa; rapidly absorbed (7–10 days) - Chromic gut: plain gut treated with chromium salts to delay absorption (10–21 days); less reactive than plain gut

Natural sutures trigger more inflammatory response than synthetic materials and have variable absorption rates.

Synthetic sutures are manufactured from polymers. They are more predictable, have lower tissue reactivity, and offer better tensile strength retention: - Polyglycolic acid, polyglactin 910, poliglecaprone, polydioxanone, nylon, polypropylene, polyester, etc.

docs.title: By Structure
docs.text:
Monofilament: A single strand. Advantages: less capillarity (reduces bacterial wicking), smooth passage through tissue. Disadvantages: higher memory (stiff, springy), less knot security — requires more throws.

Multifilament (braided or twisted): Multiple strands woven together. Advantages: easier handling, excellent knot security, pliable. Disadvantages: higher capillarity, slightly more tissue reaction.

docs.title: Absorbable Sutures
docs.text: Suture Type Absorption Common Uses Plain Gut Natural mono/multi 7–10 days Mucosa, ligation Chromic Gut Natural mono 10–21 days GI, OB/GYN, mucosa Polyglycolic acid (Dexon) Synthetic braided 60–90 days Fascial closure, GI Polyglactin 910 (Vicryl) Synthetic braided 56–70 days Fascia, subcutaneous, GI Vicryl Rapide Synthetic braided 10–14 days Skin, mucosa Poliglecaprone (Monocryl) Synthetic mono 91–119 days Subcuticular skin closure Polydioxanone (PDS) Synthetic mono 180+ days Fascial closure, pediatric cardiac Glycomer 631 (Biosyn) Synthetic mono 90–110 days General soft tissue

docs.title: Non-Absorbable Sutures
docs.text: Suture Type Characteristics Common Uses Silk Natural braided Excellent handling, high capillarity Vessel ties, cardiovascular (decreasing in favor of synthetics) Nylon (Ethilon, Dermalon) Synthetic mono High memory, strong Skin closure, ophthalmology, neurosurgery Polypropylene (Prolene, Surgilene) Synthetic mono Very smooth, minimal tissue reaction Vascular anastomosis, hernia, skin Polyester (Mersilene, Ethibond) Synthetic braided High tensile strength Cardiac, valve repair, orthopedic Polybutester (Novafil) Synthetic mono Slight elasticity Skin closure Stainless steel wire Metal mono or multi Maximum tensile strength, non-absorbable Sternal closure, orthopedic

docs.title: 7.3 Suture Sizing
docs.text:
Suture size refers to the diameter of the strand. The sizing system is counter-intuitive: - Increasing numbers (e.g., #0, #1, #2): larger diameter, heavier suture - Increasing zeros (e.g., 2-0, 3-0, 4-0, 5-0): smaller diameter, finer suture - #1 is larger than 2-0 (2-0 is two zeros; larger number of zeros = finer)

Size Relative Diameter Common Use #5 Very heavy Sternal wire #2 Heavy Tendon repair, fascial retention #1 Heavy Mass closure of abdomen #0 Standard Fascial closure 2-0 Moderate Deep fascial layer 3-0 Medium Subcutaneous, muscle 4-0 Fine Skin, vascular 5-0 Very fine Vascular anastomosis, microsurgery 6-0 to 9-0 Extremely fine Ophthalmology, microsurgery

docs.title: 7.4 Reading a Suture Package
docs.text:
Every suture package contains critical information the scrub technologist must be able to read quickly:

  1. Suture material name (generic and brand)
  2. Size (e.g., 3-0, 2-0)
  3. Length (e.g., 18 inches, 27 inches)
  4. Needle type and size (e.g., SH — small half-circle taper; FS-2 — free swaged cutting)
  5. Absorbable or non-absorbable
  6. Lot number and expiration date
  7. Number of sutures in packet (often a pack of 12 with individual foil packets inside)

docs.title: On the Sterile Field
docs.text:
• Open suture package with the opening pointing toward your sterile field; allow the inner foil packet to drop onto the field
  • Open the inner packet and remove the suture carefully, preserving the needle
  • Load the needle into the needle holder at the upper 2/3 (between the tip and midpoint), perpendicular to the jaws
  • Present the needle holder to the surgeon with the needle pointing toward the tissue; control the free tail

docs.title: 7.5 Surgical Needles
docs.text: A needle's design directly affects its performance. Understanding needle anatomy allows the scrub tech to select and load correctly.

docs.title: Needle Anatomy
docs.text:
• Eye (swaged or closed): where suture is attached; swaged (eyeless) needles are standard
  • Body: the structural section; may be round, half-round, or flat
  • Point: the tip that penetrates tissue

docs.title: Needle Curvature
docs.text: Curvature Degrees Use Straight 0° Skin closure with fingers 1/4 circle 90° Ophthalmic, superficial 3/8 circle 135° Most general tissue; most common 1/2 circle 180° Deep, confined spaces 5/8 circle 225° Very deep; GI, GU J-needle J-shape Laparoscopic port closure

docs.title: Needle Point Types
docs.text: Point Type Description Use Taper (round) Tapers to a point; no cutting edge Fascia, bowel, vessel Cutting Two or three sharp edges; triangular cross-section Skin, dense tissue Reverse cutting Cutting edge on outer curve; reduces tissue tear-through Skin, fibrous tissue (preferred over conventional cutting) Tapercut Taper body with small cutting tip Fascia, calcified tissue Blunt Rounded tip; does not cut Liver, kidney, blunt dissection Spatula Flat body with cutting edges Ophthalmic; superficial layers

docs.title: 7.6 Wound Closure Devices
docs.text: Suture is not the only method of wound closure. The scrub tech must be familiar with all available options.

docs.title: Surgical Staples
docs.text:
Skin staplers: Disposable devices that fire individual stainless steel staples into skin edges, holding them in approximation. Faster than suture for long straight incisions. Common in abdominal, orthopedic, and spinal procedures. - Pass the stapler to the surgeon with the firing end toward the tissue - Skin staples are removed with a dedicated staple remover (not a hemostat)

Internal staplers: Used for bowel anastomosis, lung resection, and vessel division. - Linear stapler (GIA — Gastrointestinal Anastomosis): fires two parallel rows of staples and simultaneously cuts between them; used for bowel division and lung stapling - Circular stapler (EEA — End-to-End Anastomosis): creates a circular staple line for end-to-end bowel anastomosis - Linear non-cutting (TA — Thoracoabdominal): fires two rows of staples without cutting; used to close a stump

Loading units: Many staplers use replaceable cartridges (loads). Cartridges are color-coded by staple height (tissue thickness). The scrub tech must identify the correct load per surgeon instruction.

docs.title: Vascular and Hemostatic Clips
docs.text:
• Ligaclips (titanium or absorbable polymer clips): Applied with a clip applier to ligate vessels; permanent or absorbable versions available
  • Hemoclips: Small metal clips for vessel control; remain in patient permanently

docs.title: Skin Closure Strips (Steri-Strips)
docs.text: Adhesive-backed paper or nylon strips applied across a wound for superficial reinforcement. Used alone for small wounds or after subcuticular closure to take tension off the suture line.

docs.title: Tissue Adhesive (Dermabond, Histoacryl)
docs.text: A cyanoacrylate-based liquid adhesive applied topically to skin edges held in approximation. Polymerizes on contact and creates a flexible, waterproof bond. - Used for small, low-tension wounds in cooperative patients (and in pediatrics to avoid suture removal) - Not suitable for high-tension or infected wounds - On the sterile field: handle with care — adhesive sets immediately on gloves

docs.title: Retention Sutures (Stay Sutures)
docs.text: Heavy, non-absorbable sutures placed through all layers of the abdominal wall to distribute tension in high-risk closures (obesity, malnourishment, multiple re-operations). Protective bolsters are placed under the suture bridges to prevent skin cutting.

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"The right suture for the right tissue — it's not one size fits all."

  • When loading suture on a needle holder, load it at 2/3 down the needle body — not at the tip (where it can break), not at the swage (where it weakens the suture attachment).
  • Monofilament sutures (Prolene, nylon, PDS) have memory. Pre-stretch them slightly by pulling through a moist gauze to reduce springback.
  • When passing suture to the surgeon, keep the tail organized — let it fall to one side and don't let it tangle with other supplies on the field.
  • Know the difference between the Vicryl the surgeon uses for fascia (#1), subcutaneous (3-0), and skin (4-0). All three may be open at once; keeping them distinct matters.

Next: Chapter 8 — Hemostasis

docs.title: 8.1 Physiologic Hemostasis
docs.text:
Before examining surgical hemostasis, it is useful to understand the body's own mechanisms. When a vessel is injured:

  1. Vascular spasm: the injured vessel constricts immediately, reducing blood flow
  2. Platelet plug formation: platelets adhere to the damaged vessel wall and aggregate, forming a temporary plug
  3. Coagulation cascade: a series of clotting factor reactions (intrinsic and extrinsic pathways) that culminate in the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin, forming a stable clot

Surgical hemostasis supports and supplements these mechanisms when bleeding exceeds the body's capacity or speed to respond.

docs.title: 8.2 Mechanical Hemostasis
docs.text: Mechanical methods physically control bleeding by compression, ligation, or occlusion.

docs.title: Direct Pressure
docs.text:
The simplest hemostatic technique: apply a sponge (laparotomy pad, raytec) with pressure to the bleeding site. Effective for diffuse oozing, venous bleeding, and as a temporizing measure while a more definitive technique is prepared.

The scrub tech maintains a ready supply of damp sponges and anticipates requests for pressure application.

docs.title: Ligatures and Ties
docs.text:
A tie is a length of suture used to ligate (encircle and close off) a vessel or tissue pedicle. The surgeon clamps the vessel with a hemostat, the scrub tech passes a free tie (suture without a needle), and the surgeon ties it around the clamped tissue before releasing the clamp.

Types of ties: - Free tie: suture passed on the field and tied by the surgeon - Tie on a passer (stick tie): suture loaded onto a needle holder or clamp and passed to the surgeon for suture ligation of a specific vessel; used when a free tie might slip

Passing a free tie: Hold the suture between both hands, pull it taut, and present it to the surgeon's dominant hand over the clamped vessel. Avoid letting the suture tail trail in the wound.

docs.title: Hemostatic Clips
docs.text: Metallic or absorbable polymer clips applied with a clip applier to mechanically occlude vessels without heat or suture. Rapid and precise. Titanium clips remain permanently; polymer clips (e.g., Hem-o-lok) absorb over months.

docs.title: Bone Wax
docs.text:
A mixture of beeswax and isopropyl palmitate applied directly to cut bone surfaces to mechanically tamponade bleeding from the Haversian canals. Used in craniotomies, sternotomies, and orthopedic cases. Bone wax is a foreign body and should be used sparingly; it inhibits bone healing and may increase infection risk.

On the sterile field: bone wax is soft and may be rolled into a small ball for easy application. Passed on a periosteal elevator or directly.

docs.title: Tourniquet
docs.text: A pneumatic cuff applied to an extremity to create a bloodless operative field. Used in orthopedic and vascular extremity cases. - Applied over cast padding above the operative site - Inflated to a pressure above systolic BP (typically 50–75 mmHg above) - Maximum safe inflation time is typically 90–120 minutes for upper extremities, up to 2 hours for lower extremities (institution and surgeon dependent) - Deflation is documented; reperfusion injury and tourniquet paralysis are potential complications

docs.title: 8.3 Thermal Hemostasis: Electrosurgery
docs.text: Electrosurgical units (ESUs) use high-frequency alternating electrical current (300 kHz–3 MHz — well above the frequency range that causes cardiac arrhythmia) to thermally coagulate or vaporize tissue.

docs.title: Monopolar Electrosurgery
docs.text:
In monopolar electrosurgery, current flows: Generator → Active electrode (pencil tip) → Patient tissue → Dispersive electrode (grounding pad) → Generator

The current is concentrated at the active electrode tip (creating heat) and dispersed over the large surface area of the grounding pad (preventing burns there).

Modes: - Cut mode: continuous waveform; heats cells rapidly, causing vaporization — smooth cutting - Coag mode: pulsed waveform; heats more slowly, causing desiccation/coagulation without vaporization — more char, less cutting - Blend mode: alternating cut and coag waveforms; simultaneous cutting and coagulation

Dispersive electrode (grounding pad): - Must be placed on a fleshy, well-vascularized, hair-free area - Placed as close to the operative site as practical - Must have complete, uniform skin contact — any gap concentrates current and causes a burn - Never place over bony prominences, scar tissue, metal implants, or ECG electrodes

ESU safety: - Active pencil tip must be placed in a safety holster when not in use - Never activate the ESU when not in contact with or near tissue — arcing can cause fires - Smoke generated by ESU (surgical plume) contains toxic byproducts — use smoke evacuation systems - Be cautious near oxygen-enriched environments (see fire safety, Chapter 2)

On the sterile field: The scrub tech receives the ESU pencil and cord, secures the cord to the drape, and places the pencil in the holster on the field. Extra tips may be opened during the case. Clean the tip as needed with a scratch pad (not a blade).

docs.title: Bipolar Electrosurgery
docs.text:
In bipolar electrosurgery, current flows only between the two tines of the bipolar forceps. There is no dispersive electrode needed, and current does not travel through the patient's body beyond the immediate tissue in the forceps.

Advantages over monopolar: - More precise; less collateral thermal damage - Safe for use near critical structures (nerves, vessels, brain) - No dispersive electrode required

Disadvantages: - No cutting capability; only coagulation - Less effective on large vessels

Common uses: neurosurgery, microsurgery, ophthalmic surgery, cases involving nerves or delicate structures

docs.title: Argon Beam Coagulator (ABC)
docs.text:
An ESU variant in which a stream of argon gas is used to deliver the coagulation current. The ionized argon conducts the current to the tissue surface, improving hemostasis while reducing char and tissue depth.

Useful for liver, spleen, and other highly vascular solid organs where diffuse surface bleeding is the problem.

docs.title: 8.4 Chemical and Topical Hemostatic Agents
docs.text:
When mechanical and thermal methods are insufficient or inappropriate, topical hemostatic agents provide additional coagulation support.

Agent Type Mechanism Examples Notes Gelatin sponge (Gelfoam) Absorbable gelatin Provides scaffold for clot formation Gelfoam Absorbed in 4–6 weeks; may be soaked in thrombin Oxidized cellulose (Surgicel) Absorbable cellulose Lowers local pH; mechanical and biochemical hemostasis Surgicel, Surgicel Nu-Knit Absorbed in 7–14 days Collagen sponge/pad (Helistat, Instat) Absorbable collagen Activates platelet aggregation Helistat Effective for diffuse oozing Thrombin (topical) Enzyme Converts fibrinogen to fibrin Thrombin-JMI, Evithrom Applied alone or combined with gelatin sponge (FloSeal, Surgiflo) Fibrin sealant (Tisseel, Evicel) Two-component glue Fibrin clot formation mimicking final coagulation step Tisseel Two syringes mix at tip; used for anastomoses, surface bleeding Bone wax Mechanical Physical tamponade of bone canals — See Section 8.2 Epinephrine (local) Vasoconstrictive Arteriolar constriction In local anesthetics Added to local anesthetic for control of tissue bleeding

Handling hemostatic agents on the sterile field: - Know what is open on your field and where it is - Label all medications (including thrombin) immediately when received from the circulator - Thrombin must never be injected intravenously — it causes systemic coagulation and is potentially fatal. Keep thrombin clearly separated from injectable medications. - Prepare Gelfoam soaked in thrombin in a small basin, not on the open instrument field

docs.title: 8.5 Laser Surgery
docs.text: LASER stands for Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation. Lasers produce a coherent, monochromatic, highly focused beam of light that can cut, vaporize, coagulate, or ablate tissue depending on wavelength and power settings.

docs.title: Common Surgical Lasers
docs.text: Laser Wavelength Color Target Common Uses CO₂ laser 10,600 nm Infrared Water (soft tissue) ENT, gynecology, dermatology, microsurgery Nd:YAG laser 1,064 nm Near-infrared Pigmented tissue GI, urology, ophthalmology Argon laser 488–514 nm Visible blue-green Hemoglobin/melanin Ophthalmology (retinal), dermatology KTP laser 532 nm Green Hemoglobin ENT, urology Holmium:YAG (Ho:YAG) 2,100 nm Near-infrared Water Urology (lithotripsy), arthroscopy, spine Diode laser 800–980 nm Near-infrared Various ENT, ophthalmology Excimer laser 193–351 nm Ultraviolet Tissue surface Ophthalmology (LASIK)

docs.title: Laser Hazards
docs.text:
Hazard Risk Control Eye injury Laser energy can cause retinal burns or corneal damage, even from reflected beams Wavelength-appropriate protective eyewear required for all personnel and patient; signs posted outside OR Skin/tissue burn Misdirected beam can burn skin, drapes, or deep tissue Cover non-target tissue with moist towels; use laser-specific drapes Fire Oxygen-enriched environment + laser beam Wet sponges around operative site; use laser-safe ETTs in airway cases; minimize FiO₂ Laser plume Vaporized tissue particles contain carcinogens and viable virus particles Laser plume evacuators required; all personnel wear laser-specific N95 masks Electrical hazard High-voltage equipment Follow manufacturer guidelines; trained personnel only

The scrub technologist must: - Don appropriate wavelength-specific eye protection before laser activation - Ensure the patient's eyes are protected - Have moist towels/sponges prepared to surround the operative site - Know the laser safety officer and the facility laser safety program - Never look directly at the beam

docs.title: 8.6 The Scrub Technologist's Role in Hemostasis
docs.text:
The scrub tech anticipates hemorrhage and manages the response at the level of the sterile field.

Proactive: - Keep suture ties ready during dissection near major vessels - Have hemostatic clips loaded in the applier - Keep a supply of appropriate sponges (damp laparotomy pads, raytec) accessible - Know which topical hemostatic agents are on the field and their locations

Reactive: - When unexpected bleeding occurs, respond without being told: place a sponge, hand a sucker, pass a clamp - In major hemorrhage, maintain awareness of sponge counts despite the urgency — a massive bleed is the scenario where retention is most likely - Communicate any shortage of critical supplies to the circulator immediately

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"In a bleed, your hands should be moving before the surgeon finishes the sentence."

  • ESU pencil tip builds char over time. Clean it proactively with the scratch pad — a dirty tip requires more power, which causes more collateral damage.
  • Never leave the ESU pencil resting on a drape unholstered. The device can be inadvertently activated, and it is a fire starter.
  • Thrombin is topical only. If it lands on an instrument that then goes back into the wound, it is not harmful — but injecting it is deadly. Know what is in each labeled basin.

Next: Chapter 9 — Anesthesia

docs.title: 9.1 Types of Anesthesia
docs.text: Anesthesia is not a single intervention — it is a spectrum of techniques calibrated to the patient, the procedure, and the goals of the operative team.

docs.title: General Anesthesia
docs.text:
The patient is rendered completely unconscious, unaware, and non-responsive to surgical stimulation. All protective airway reflexes are abolished, requiring assisted ventilation.

General anesthesia provides the "four A's": - Analgesia: freedom from pain - Amnesia: no memory of the procedure - Areflexia: absence of protective reflexes - Akinesia: muscular relaxation and immobility

Achieved through a combination of IV induction agents, inhalation agents, neuromuscular blocking agents, and analgesics.

docs.title: Regional Anesthesia
docs.text:
A region of the body is desensitized by blocking specific nerves. The patient may remain awake, sedated, or have monitored anesthesia care added.

Types: - Spinal (subarachnoid block): local anesthetic injected into the subarachnoid space (L3–L4 or L4–L5); rapid, dense block; used for lower extremity, perineal, and lower abdominal surgery - Epidural: local anesthetic injected into the epidural space; slower onset; allows continuous infusion via catheter; used in OB, post-op pain management, extended cases - Peripheral nerve block: targeted injection around a specific nerve or plexus (e.g., brachial plexus, femoral nerve, popliteal block); used for upper and lower extremity surgery; excellent post-op analgesia

docs.title: Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC)
docs.text:
The patient receives IV sedation (typically midazolam, fentanyl, propofol) while the surgeon provides local anesthetic to the operative site. The patient is sedated but not fully unconscious and may be arousable.

Used for: minor procedures, endoscopy, ophthalmology, biopsies, superficial surgery

The scrub tech should be aware that MAC patients can hear and feel discomfort — communicate carefully.

docs.title: Local Anesthesia Only
docs.text:
A local anesthetic agent is injected by the surgeon directly into the operative site without any systemic sedation. The patient is fully awake.

Used for: very minor procedures (skin biopsy, lipoma excision), ophthalmology (cataract under topical), dental procedures

docs.title: Induction Agents (IV)
docs.text: Agent Class Key Characteristics Notes Propofol (Diprivan) Alkylphenol Rapid onset/offset, antiemetic "Milk of amnesia"; white emulsion Ketamine NMDA antagonist Dissociative; analgesic; preserves airway reflexes Used in pediatrics, trauma; may cause emergence delirium Etomidate Imidazole Minimal cardiovascular depression Preferred for hemodynamically unstable patients; brief adrenal suppression Methohexital Barbiturate Rarely used today Occasionally for ECT

docs.title: Inhalation (Volatile) Agents
docs.text:
Administered as gases via the anesthesia circuit; responsible for maintenance of general anesthesia.

Agent Notes Sevoflurane Most common; non-pungent; good for inhalation induction Desflurane Rapid onset/offset; pungent; airway irritant Isoflurane Older; cost-effective; pungent Nitrous oxide (N₂O) Adjunct; weak analgesic; limited when high O₂ required

MH trigger alert: All volatile agents and succinylcholine are triggers for malignant hyperthermia in susceptible individuals (see Section 9.5).

docs.title: Neuromuscular Blocking Agents (NMBAs)
docs.text:
Produce skeletal muscle relaxation for intubation and surgical exposure.

Agent Type Duration Notes Succinylcholine (Sux, Anectine) Depolarizing Ultra-short (~8 min) Fastest onset; MH trigger; use restricted in burn/crush/neuromuscular disease Rocuronium Non-depolarizing Intermediate Reversible with Sugammadex Vecuronium Non-depolarizing Intermediate Reversible with neostigmine/glycopyrrolate Cisatracurium Non-depolarizing Intermediate Hoffman elimination; useful in organ failure Pancuronium Non-depolarizing Long Tachycardia side effect

Reversal agents: - Neostigmine + glycopyrrolate: reverses non-depolarizing NMBAs - Sugammadex (Bridion): selective reversal of rocuronium and vecuronium; faster and more complete

docs.title: Local Anesthetic Agents
docs.text:
Agent Duration Notes Lidocaine (Xylocaine) 1–2 hours Most common; available in various concentrations; with or without epinephrine Bupivacaine (Marcaine) 4–8 hours Long-acting; cardiac toxicity risk at high doses Ropivacaine (Naropin) 4–8 hours Less cardiotoxic than bupivacaine Mepivacaine (Carbocaine) 2–3 hours Intermediate duration Tetracaine Long Spinal anesthesia Cocaine Short ENT only; vasoconstrictor in the airway; controlled substance

Epinephrine addition: When added to local anesthetic, epinephrine causes vasoconstriction at the injection site, prolonging the block duration and reducing systemic absorption. Never use epinephrine-containing local anesthetic in areas without collateral circulation: digits, penis, nose, ear flaps.

On the sterile field: All medications received on the sterile field must be labeled immediately with the drug name and concentration. Use pre-labeled medication cups; confirm with the circulator before labeling.

docs.title: Phase 1: Preoperative Preparation
docs.text:
• IV access established
  • Monitors applied (ECG, SpO₂, NIBP, EtCO₂, temperature)
  • Pre-oxygenation: 100% O₂ via facemask for 3–5 minutes to denitrogenate lungs
  • Pre-medication given if indicated (anxiolytic, antisialagogue, antiemetic)

docs.title: Phase 2: Induction
docs.text:
• IV induction agent administered (rapid loss of consciousness within 30–60 seconds)
  • Succinylcholine or rocuronium given for intubation
  • Laryngoscopy and endotracheal intubation (or LMA placement)
  • Confirmation of placement: bilateral breath sounds, chest rise, EtCO₂ waveform
  • The scrub tech should be silent and still during induction — this is the most critical moment for the anesthesia provider

docs.title: Phase 3: Maintenance
docs.text:
• Volatile agent +/- nitrous oxide delivered via ventilator
  • IV analgesics (fentanyl, remifentanil), adjunct agents as needed
  • Surgical procedure is performed during this phase
  • Monitors track depth of anesthesia and physiologic stability

docs.title: Phase 4: Emergence
docs.text:
• Volatile agents discontinued; patient begins to wake
  • Neuromuscular blockade reversed
  • Patient must be able to breathe spontaneously, follow commands, and maintain airway before extubation
  • The scrub tech should be aware that emergence can be a period of patient agitation — keep instruments away from the patient's reach; do not lean on the patient

docs.title: Phase 5: Recovery (PACU)
docs.text:
• Patient transferred to post-anesthesia care unit
  • Monitored until stable, awake, and meeting discharge criteria

docs.title: Endotracheal Tube (ETT)
docs.text:
A cuffed tube inserted through the mouth (orotracheal) or nose (nasotracheal) into the trachea, past the vocal cords. Provides definitive airway protection.

  • Sizes: typically 7.0–8.0 mm for adult males, 6.5–7.5 mm for adult females
  • Cuff inflated to 20–30 cmH₂O to create a seal
  • Position confirmed by EtCO₂ and chest auscultation
  • Secured with tape or a commercial tube holder

docs.title: Laryngeal Mask Airway (LMA)
docs.text:
A supraglottic device (sits above the glottis, not in the trachea). Easier to insert than an ETT; does not protect against aspiration. Used for shorter procedures, patients with difficult airways, or when intubation is not required.

Types: standard, intubating (Fastrach), proseal (with gastric drain channel)

docs.title: Oral/Nasopharyngeal Airways
docs.text: Used to maintain airway patency in sedated patients or during mask ventilation; not definitive airways.

docs.title: Surgical Airways
docs.text: In emergency airway situations: - Cricothyrotomy: emergency incision through the cricothyroid membrane; rapid but temporary - Tracheostomy: surgical airway through the trachea (planned procedure)

docs.title: 9.5 Malignant Hyperthermia (MH)
docs.text: Malignant hyperthermia is a pharmacogenetic crisis — a rare but potentially fatal hypermetabolic reaction to certain anesthetic agents in genetically susceptible individuals. It is a true operating room emergency.

docs.title: Triggering Agents
docs.text:
• All volatile anesthetic agents (sevoflurane, desflurane, isoflurane, halothane)
  • Succinylcholine (the only neuromuscular blocking agent that triggers MH)

Non-triggering anesthetics (propofol, ketamine, opioids, non-depolarizing NMBAs) are used in MH-susceptible patients.

docs.title: Pathophysiology
docs.text: In susceptible individuals, a mutation in the ryanodine receptor (RYR1) causes an uncontrolled release of calcium from muscle sarcoplasmic reticulum in response to triggers. Uncontrolled muscle metabolism ensues: - Massive CO₂ production - Hyperthermia (temperature may rise >1°C every 5 minutes) - Muscle rigidity - Rhabdomyolysis - Acidosis - Hyperkalemia → cardiac arrhythmia → cardiac arrest

docs.title: Signs and Symptoms (in approximate order of appearance)
docs.text:
1. Unexplained increase in end-tidal CO₂ (earliest and most sensitive sign)
  2. Tachycardia
  3. Hyperthermia (may be late sign)
  4. Masseter spasm (jaw rigidity after succinylcholine)
  5. Generalized muscle rigidity
  6. Dark urine (myoglobinuria from muscle breakdown)
  7. Metabolic and respiratory acidosis

docs.title: Treatment (MH Emergency Protocol)
docs.text:
The Malignant Hyperthermia Association of the United States (MHAUS) maintains an emergency hotline and protocol: MHAUS Hotline: 1-800-644-9737

  1. Call for help — this is an all-hands emergency
  2. Discontinue triggering agents immediately
  3. Switch to non-triggering anesthetic (total IV anesthesia)
  4. Hyperventilate with 100% O₂ at maximum flow
  5. Administer Dantrolene sodium IV: 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus; repeat every 5 minutes until symptoms resolve (maximum ~10 mg/kg, often more required)
  6. Treat hyperthermia: ice packs, cold IV fluids, gastric lavage with iced saline
  7. Treat acidosis: sodium bicarbonate
  8. Treat hyperkalemia: calcium chloride, insulin/glucose, sodium bicarbonate
  9. Continue monitoring: ICU admission; MH can recrudesce
  10. Dantrolene IV for 24–48 hours post-event

docs.title: The Scrub Tech's Role During an MH Crisis
docs.text:
• Do not abandon the sterile field unless directed — wounds may need to be closed rapidly
  • Assist with closing if directed
  • Remain calm and follow instructions from the anesthesia team
  • Prepare ice-cold saline if asked
  • Be prepared for rapid case termination

docs.title: Dantrolene Preparation
docs.text: Dantrolene is provided as a lyophilized powder (20 mg per vial) requiring 60 mL of sterile water for reconstitution. Each vial requires vigorous mixing. In an MH crisis, many vials must be reconstituted simultaneously — all available personnel will be recruited to help.

docs.title: 9.6 Medications on the Sterile Field: Safety Principles
docs.text:
1. Label all medications immediately upon transfer to the sterile field
  2. Confirm verbally with the circulator: "I'm labeling this as lidocaine 1% with epinephrine"
  3. Use pre-printed labels or a permanent marker on medication cups/syringes
  4. Never have two unlabeled medication containers open simultaneously
  5. If a medication's identity is questioned, discard it — never guess
  6. Thrombin must be labeled and kept separate from injectable medications

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"During induction and emergence, the anesthesia provider needs silence and space. Give both without being asked."

  • If a surgeon is asking for epinephrine in a local anesthetic for a digit procedure, speak up — epinephrine is contraindicated in end-arterial tissues. Patient safety is everyone's responsibility.
  • Rising EtCO₂ during a case is not normal. If you notice it on the monitor, inform the team.
  • Know where the MH cart is before every case. Every OR should have one stocked and immediately accessible.

Next: Chapter 10 — Patient Positioning

docs.title: 10.1 Principles of Surgical Positioning
docs.text:
Positioning the patient for surgery is a team effort requiring careful planning before the patient arrives in the OR. The goals of surgical positioning are to:

  1. Provide optimal access to the operative site for the surgeon
  2. Maintain patient safety throughout the procedure
  3. Protect neurovascular structures from stretch, compression, or ischemia
  4. Preserve respiratory and cardiovascular function
  5. Maintain patient dignity

Improper positioning can result in catastrophic, permanent injury: peripheral nerve damage, compartment syndrome, pressure necrosis, vision loss, or cardiovascular compromise. Every member of the surgical team shares responsibility for safe positioning.

docs.title: The Safety Check Before Positioning
docs.text:
• Confirm the procedure and position on the surgical schedule
  • Identify patient-specific risk factors: obesity, diabetes, vascular disease, prior surgeries, joint limitations, history of DVT
  • Verify all positioning equipment is available and functional
  • Review the surgeon's preference card for any specific positioning requirements
  • Plan the position before the patient is anesthetized — repositioning after induction is more difficult and riskier

docs.title: 10.2 Positioning Accessories and Equipment
docs.text:
Equipment Purpose Arm board Supports the extended arm; must be padded; abduction limited to ≤90° Padded stirrups Support legs in lithotomy position (candy cane, Allen stirrups, knee crutch) Bean bag positioner Vacuum-hardened foam that conforms to body contours; secures lateral position Chest rolls (bolsters) Placed under the chest in prone position; allow chest expansion Axillary roll Padded roll placed under the axilla in lateral position to offload the dependent shoulder Kidney rest (flank rest) Elevates the flank in lateral position for kidney procedures Headrest Supports the head; multiple designs (donut, Mayfield pins, horseshoe) Padded footboard Prevents foot drop and plantarflexion pressure in prolonged supine cases Safety strap (leg strap) Secures patient to the OR table; applied 2 inches above the knees Wilson frame Convex frame that flexes the spine; used in prone spinal procedures Laminectomy frame (Relton-Hall) Four-post frame supporting chest and iliac crests in prone spinal cases

All bony prominences and pressure points must be padded prior to draping.

docs.title: Supine (Dorsal Recumbent)
docs.text:
Description: Patient lies flat on their back; arms may be at the side or extended on arm boards.

Used for: General abdominal surgery, gynecologic surgery (with modification), open heart surgery, lower extremity surgery, most head and neck procedures.

Pressure points to pad: - Occiput (back of head) - Scapulae - Olecranon (elbow) - Sacrum/coccyx - Heels

Common complications: - Brachial plexus injury from arm hyperabduction (>90°) - Ulnar nerve injury from pressure on medial elbow - Heel pressure injuries in prolonged cases - Lumbar back pain (loss of normal lordosis)

Physiological effects: Most physiologically neutral position; baseline for comparison.

docs.title: Prone
docs.text:
Description: Patient lies face-down; chest and iliac crests supported on rolls or frames; head turned or in neutral position on padded headrest.

Used for: Posterior spinal surgery, posterior cranial fossa surgery, rectal surgery, posterior extremity procedures.

Pressure points to pad: - Forehead and chin (if face-down) - Breasts/nipples (female patients) - Male genitalia (must hang free) - Iliac crests - Knees - Dorsum of feet

Turning the patient prone: A coordinated team effort. - Anesthesia manages the airway throughout the turn - At minimum 4 persons for turning (one per extremity/body segment + anesthesia at the head) - Log-roll or Jackson table transfer - Always confirm ETT position after turning

Complications: - Airway compromise/accidental extubation during turn - Pressure on the eyes → ischemic optic neuropathy (devastating) - Abdominal compression → impaired ventilation - Brachial plexus injury (arms tucked at sides or extended overhead) - Penile/genital compression (males)

docs.title: Lateral (Lateral Decubitus)
docs.text:
Description: Patient lies on either side; lower arm extended on arm board; upper arm supported on a padded arm sled or pillow; axillary roll placed under lower chest wall.

Used for: Thoracic surgery (lung), kidney surgery (nephrectomy), hip arthroplasty (lateral approach), lateral spine procedures.

Pressure points to pad: - Down-side ear - Shoulder of dependent arm - Down-side ribs - Iliac crest (dependent) - Medial knee (bones of opposing knees contact each other) - Down-side ankle

Critical detail — axillary roll: The axillary roll is placed just below (caudal to) the axilla, not in it. Its purpose is to protect the neurovascular bundle of the dependent arm from compression against the table. If placed incorrectly in the axilla, it will cause injury.

Stability: Use a beanbag positioner, kidney rests, or a lateral positioner/rail system. Confirm stability by gently rocking the table before draping.

docs.title: Lithotomy
docs.text:
Description: Patient lies supine; hips are flexed; legs are raised and held in stirrups with knees slightly flexed and hips abducted. Buttocks are at or just past the table break.

Used for: Vaginal, perineal, rectal, and combined abdominal/perineal procedures; urologic endoscopy; some laparoscopic gynecologic cases.

Positions legs simultaneously to avoid lumbar injury — two persons raise and lower both legs at the same time, in synchrony.

Stirrup types: - Candy cane: simple hook; puts more stress on ankle and hip - Allen/knee-in-arm (Yellow Fin): padded knee support; distributes leg weight along thigh; preferred for most cases - Knee crutch: supports knee; allows some leg extension

Pressure points to pad: - Sacrum and coccyx (bottom of pelvis on table) - Popliteal fossa (back of knee — peroneal nerve) - Heels and ankles

Complications: - Compartment syndrome of the lower legs — the most feared complication; caused by sustained elevation of legs above heart level → venous engorgement → tissue pressure; risk increases with case duration >3–4 hours - Common peroneal nerve injury from pressure at the fibular head (foot drop) - Femoral nerve injury from excessive hip flexion - Hip dislocation in patients with prior hip replacement

Duration limit: Many surgeons aim to stay under 3 hours in full lithotomy. If anticipated duration is longer, discuss with the team and consider intermittent repositioning.

Lowering legs after lithotomy: Lower both legs simultaneously and slowly to prevent sudden blood pressure drop from venous redistribution.

docs.title: Trendelenburg
docs.text:
Description: Supine with the head tilted down; feet elevated above the head.

Used for: Lower abdominal/pelvic surgery (gravity displaces bowel out of pelvis), laparoscopic procedures (improves pelvic exposure), central venous catheter placement, some vascular procedures.

Physiological effects: - Increases venous return → increased cardiac preload - Increases intracranial and intraocular pressure - Displaces diaphragm cephalad → reduces FRC, increases work of breathing

Complications: - Increased ICP (contraindicated in elevated ICP) - Brachial plexus stretch (shoulder braces should not be used; anti-slip mattress preferred) - Retinal detachment in susceptible patients with prolonged steep Trendelenburg - Edema of head/neck

docs.title: Reverse Trendelenburg
docs.text:
Description: Supine with the head elevated; feet below the head.

Used for: Head and neck surgery, laparoscopic upper GI procedures (displaces bowel inferiorly), thyroid surgery, bariatric surgery.

Physiological effects: Improves respiratory mechanics; reduces intracranial pressure; decreases venous return → may cause hypotension.

Complications: - Hypotension (venous pooling in lower extremities) - Footboard pressure (pad thoroughly)

docs.title: Fowler's / Semi-Fowler's (Beach Chair)
docs.text:
Description: Head of the OR table elevated to 30°–90°; patient essentially sitting upright.

Used for: Shoulder surgery (beach chair position for arthroscopy), craniotomies, some ear and neck procedures.

Complications: - Venous air embolism (VAE) — surgical site is above the heart; negative venous pressure can pull air into open veins → air lock in the right heart → cardiac arrest. Monitor with Doppler; treat by flooding the field with saline, positioning in left lateral Trendelenburg. - Hypotension from venous pooling - Cervical hyperextension (protect the cervical spine)

docs.title: Jackknife (Kraske)
docs.text:
Description: Prone with the table flexed at the middle ("broken" like a jackknife); buttocks are elevated as the highest point.

Used for: Anal and rectal procedures (hemorrhoidectomy, pilonidal cyst excision, anal fistula repair).

Pressure points: same as prone position; additional padding for knees and abdomen at the flexion point.

docs.title: Obese Patients
docs.text:
• Longer extremities and heavier limbs increase torque at joints → greater risk of positioning injuries
  • May require extra arm board extensions, additional padding
  • Increased risk of respiratory compromise in Trendelenburg → steep angles may not be tolerated
  • Pressure injury risk higher due to poor tissue perfusion in adipose tissue

docs.title: Pediatric Patients
docs.text:
• Less able to maintain normothermia (higher surface area-to-volume ratio) → add warming measures
  • Smaller pressure points still at risk — pad diligently
  • Fragile bones and underdeveloped joints → gentle, careful positioning

docs.title: Elderly Patients
docs.text:
• Reduced skin turgor and poor perfusion → pressure injuries develop faster
  • Joint stiffness and limited range of motion → do not force positions; know the patient's limitations
  • Osteoporosis → fracture risk during repositioning

docs.title: Patients with Implants
docs.text:
• Existing metal implants may contraindicate certain positions or imaging modalities
  • Hip/knee prostheses limit lithotomy positioning — confirm with the surgeon and the patient's history before positioning

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"A position that causes nerve damage takes seconds to create and months to recover from — if ever."

  • Before draping, do a final visual sweep: is everything padded? Arms in neutral? No pressure on eyes? ETT still secure?
  • In lithotomy position, slide your fingers behind the patient's knee to ensure the popliteal fossa is not resting on the stirrup edge.
  • Document position, padding, duration, and any noted concerns at the end of every case.

Next: Chapter 11 — Skin Preparation and Draping

docs.title: 11.1 Purpose of Skin Preparation
docs.text:
Even with intact skin, the human body harbors millions of bacteria. Skin prep cannot sterilize the skin — it reduces the total bacterial count to a level unlikely to cause infection in the context of the surgical wound. The goal is to decrease the risk of endogenous SSIs (those caused by the patient's own flora).

Skin prep: - Reduces transient flora (surface-level contamination) - Suppresses resident flora (deeper skin flora) - Creates an antiseptic barrier that persists into the procedure

Skin prep is typically performed by the circulating nurse or surgical technologist (circulating), in coordination with the scrub technologist who will later drape.

docs.title: 11.2 Hair Removal
docs.text:
If hair must be removed from the surgical site, use electric clippers with a single-use or disinfectable head — never a razor.

Rationale: Razors cause microscopic skin abrasions that become colonized with bacteria and increase SSI risk. Studies consistently show razor shaving results in higher SSI rates than clipping.

Timing: Hair removal should be performed as close to surgery as practical, ideally immediately before prep in the holding area or OR.

Policy: Many facilities have abandoned routine hair removal entirely; it is only performed if hair physically interferes with the surgical procedure.

docs.title: Chlorhexidine Gluconate (CHG)
docs.text:
• Mechanism: Disrupts bacterial cell membrane integrity; bactericidal
  • Spectrum: Broad-spectrum gram-positive and gram-negative, some fungi
  • Onset: Rapid
  • Persistence: Excellent — continues to work after application; residual activity persists even after rinsing with water
  • Available forms: Aqueous (water-based) or alcohol-based (CHG + isopropyl alcohol — more effective)
  • Precautions: Do not use in ears (ototoxic), eyes, or on mucous membranes; do not use in premature infants

CHG + 70% isopropyl alcohol (e.g., ChloraPrep) is the current evidence-based standard for most surgical skin antisepsis.

docs.title: Povidone-Iodine (Betadine)
docs.text:
• Mechanism: Free iodine denatures proteins and nucleic acids
  • Spectrum: Broad-spectrum (bacteria, fungi, viruses, spores)
  • Persistence: Limited — reduced when exposed to blood, serum, or other organics
  • Available forms: Aqueous scrub, aqueous paint, alcohol-based
  • Precautions: May cause skin sensitivity; avoid eyes; drying takes time — do not wipe off

Povidone-iodine is preferred for mucous membrane prep (vaginal, anorectal, oral) and for patients with CHG allergy.

docs.title: Isopropyl Alcohol
docs.text:
• Fast onset, excellent broad-spectrum activity
  • Short persistence (evaporates rapidly)
  • Highly flammable — must dry completely before draping or using electrosurgery
  • Used primarily as a component of combination products (CHG/alcohol)

docs.title: Iodine in Alcohol (Io-Scrub, Duraprep)
docs.text: Combination of iodine povacrylex and isopropyl alcohol. Strong, rapid antiseptic activity. Must dry completely before draping (≥3 minutes). Do not pool in skin folds.

docs.title: General Principles
docs.text:
1. Start at the incision site and work outward in a circular (concentric) motion
  2. Never return to the center after moving outward — the periphery is dirtier than the center
  3. Prep the full area that may be needed for the incision or for field extension
  4. Use sufficient coverage: extend beyond the proposed incision to allow for variation and wound extension
  5. Allow the prep to dry completely before draping — wet prep under drapes increases strike-through risk and fire risk if alcohol-based
  6. Remove excess pooling solution from the skin to prevent chemical burns

docs.title: Special Technique Situations
docs.text:
Contaminated area (stoma, open wound, anus): - Prep the contaminated area last, using a separate prep sponge - Work from clean area inward toward the contaminated site — never from contaminated to clean

Umbilicus: - Prep the umbilicus separately with a separate applicator; it harbors higher concentrations of bacteria - Use a swab or small applicator to reach into the umbilical recess

Genital/perineal prep: - Use povidone-iodine (aqueous) for vaginal, scrotal, or perianal prep — not CHG on mucous membranes - For female vaginal cases: prep vaginal vault with long-handled prep sponges; prep cervix if applicable
