⚠ WATCH FOR - Uterine clamp slippage: if a Heaney clamp slips off a pedicle, tell the surgeon immediately — do not assume they saw it; re-clamping protects against hemorrhage and ureteral injury - Ureter identification: the ureter must be confirmed before clamping the uterine vessels; if the surgeon is working in a scarred or distorted pelvis, slow down and confirm anatomy before passing any clamp - Running out of Heaney clamps: this case uses 6–8 Heaneys in rapid succession; track their location; never leave a clamp on a pedicle without telling the surgeon what is still on the field - Pedicle bleeding after ligature: if a tie slips, have a suture on a needle holder immediately available — 0 Vicryl on a CT-1 or SH needle - Vaginal cuff closure: once the uterus is removed, the surgeon works in the vagina from above; keep your field organized and ready to pass the 0 Vicryl for cuff closure without delay

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Layer Suture Notes Vaginal cuff 0 Vicryl running locking or interrupted figure-of-eight Critical layer — hemostasis Peritoneum 3-0 Vicryl running May be left open Fascia 0 Vicryl or PDS running Subcutaneous 3-0 Vicryl Skin Staples or 4-0 Monocryl Drain JP in pelvis Rarely needed; used if significant dissection

Dressing: Gauze 4×4 + ABD pad.

docs.title: Laparoscopic Hysterectomy (TLH)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: II (Clean-Contaminated) · Position: Low lithotomy → steep Trendelenburg · Avg time: 75–120 min · Team: surgeon + 1 assistant · Energy device: LigaSure or EnSeal · Uterine manipulator: required

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text: BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Uterine manipulator (RUMI or Koh system) placed vaginally before the abdominal approach — confirm it's been placed before prepping - [ ] Laparoscopic energy device (LigaSure or EnSeal) — confirm correct instrument and generator are in the room - [ ] Laparoscope: 10 mm 0° or 30°; camera white-balanced; light cord confirmed - [ ] 5 mm trocars × 3 + 10–12 mm umbilical trocar - [ ] 0 Vicryl × 2 for port site fascial closure; 4-0 Monocryl for skin - [ ] Vaginal cuff closure: 0 Vicryl on a CT-1 needle; may be sutured laparoscopically or vaginally

docs.title: Instruments
docs.text:
Instrument Notes #11 blade on #3 handle × 4 All port incisions 10–12 mm umbilical trocar × 1 Camera port 5 mm trocars × 3 Working ports 10 mm 0° or 30° laparoscope + camera + light cord Confirm white balance and light before draping Laparoscopic energy device (LigaSure or EnSeal) Pedicle ligation — confirm correct generator is connected Laparoscopic graspers × 2 One toothed; tissue manipulation and retraction Laparoscopic scissors (hook or curved) Tissue cutting and colpotomy Laparoscopic Maryland dissector Blunt dissection and vessel exposure Laparoscopic suction/irrigation Field irrigation and fluid removal Laparoscopic needle drivers × 2 Vaginal cuff closure through scope Carter-Thomason fascial closure device Fascial closure at all ports ≥10 mm Uterine manipulator (RUMI or Koh system) Placed vaginally pre-op — confirm in place before draping Specimen retrieval bag (endobag) For adnexa if removed 0 Vicryl on CT-1 needle Vaginal cuff closure ESU pencil Backup energy Yankauer suction Backup suction

Position: Supine → modified low lithotomy (Allen stirrups) after abdominal access established; steep Trendelenburg (bowel displaces out of the pelvis by gravity).

Prep: CHG/alcohol abdomen; betadine vaginal prep.

Draping: Fenestrated laparoscopic sheet over the trocar sites; perineal drape with vaginal access incorporated.

The Knife: #11 blade for all port incisions. Umbilical: 10–12 mm. Three additional 5 mm ports in lower quadrants and suprapubic.

Retractors: Trocars + pneumoperitoneum + steep Trendelenburg. The uterine manipulator antireflexes and retroflexes the uterus from below to expose each vascular pedicle.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
Mirrors TAH steps executed laparoscopically. Energy device (LigaSure/EnSeal) replaces the clamp-cut-tie technique for ligating pedicles. The vaginal cuff is cut with laparoscopic scissors; the uterus is delivered vaginally through the colpotomy.

⚠ WATCH FOR - Energy device to the ureter: the ureter runs close to the uterine artery; if the energy device fires too close to the ureter without direct visualization, thermal injury causes a delayed ureteral leak — confirm the surgeon has visualized the ureter before each energy device activation at the parametrium - Uterine manipulator movement: if the manipulator is not moving correctly (the scrub tech or assistant is managing it from below), the surgeon cannot expose the correct pedicle — communicate; respond to verbal cues - Pneumoperitoneum loss: if a trocar is dislodged, CO₂ escapes rapidly; the surgeon must repuncture — have an extra trocar of the same size on the field - Port site fascial closure: ports ≥10 mm must have fascial closure to prevent port-site hernia; do not skip this step

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Layer Suture Notes Vaginal cuff 0 Vicryl Laparoscopic or vaginal Port sites ≥10 mm fascia 0 Vicryl Required Skin (all ports) 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular

Dressing: Small adhesive dressings over port sites.

docs.title: Dilation and Curettage (D&C)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: II (Clean-Contaminated) · Position: Lithotomy · Avg time: 10–20 min · Team: surgeon only · No incision · Specimen: endometrial curettings to pathology

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text: BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Hegar or Pratt dilators: lay out in ascending order on the Mayo stand before the case starts - [ ] Weighted speculum: confirm size (standard vs. large); on the Mayo stand - [ ] Anterior Sims retractor on the Mayo stand - [ ] Single-tooth tenaculum (cervical): loaded in hand before passing - [ ] Uterine sound: confirm it's on the Mayo stand - [ ] Sharp curettes (small, medium, large) in order - [ ] Specimen cup labeled and ready to receive curettings immediately

docs.title: Instruments
docs.text:
Instrument Notes Weighted (Auvard) vaginal speculum Provides constant posterior vaginal retraction via its own weight Anterior Sims retractor Anterior vaginal wall retraction Single-tooth tenaculum Grasps and stabilizes the anterior cervix Hegar dilators — full set (#1–14) Cervical dilation in ascending order — lay out in sequence before the case Pratt dilators — full set Alternative tapered dilator set Uterine sound Measures uterine cavity depth before dilation Sharp uterine curette, small Endometrial curettage Sharp uterine curette, medium Endometrial curettage Sharp uterine curette, large Endometrial curettage Serrated ring forceps / sponge forceps Removing larger tissue fragments Suction curettage cannula + syringe or vacuum system If suction curettage planned Betadine-soaked sponge stick Cervical prep Specimen cup Labeled and ready before the first curette pass

Position: Lithotomy (Allen stirrups).

Prep: Betadine vaginal prep.

Draping: Lithotomy/perineal drape — splits around both legs; perineum and vagina exposed.

The Knife: No incision. Access is through the natural orifice (vagina/cervix).

Retractors: Weighted speculum (posterior vaginal wall retraction — its own weight provides constant retraction) + anterior Sims retractor (anterior vaginal wall) = complete cervical exposure.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Bimanual examination under anesthesia; determine uterine size and position
  2. Grasp the cervix with a single-tooth tenaculum on the anterior lip
  3. Sound the uterus; note depth (normal 6–8 cm)
  4. Dilate the cervix: pass Hegar (or Pratt) dilators in ascending sequence from small to desired size
  5. Pass sharp curette into the uterine cavity; scrape the endometrial lining systematically; collect curettings
  6. Suction curettage (if indicated): vacuum aspiration cannula passed; suction applied; contents removed

⚠ WATCH FOR - Uterine perforation: if the sound or curette passes further than the noted uterine depth, the uterus may have been perforated — call it out; the surgeon will assess with laparoscopy - Cervical laceration from tenaculum: if the tenaculum tears through the cervix, bleeding occurs at the site; silver nitrate or a figure-of-eight suture (0 Vicryl) is needed — have it ready - Dilators passed out of order: always hand the next size up in sequence; if a size is skipped, the cervix can tear rather than dilate - Incomplete specimen: if the pathology specimen is very small or absent, the surgeon will want to confirm the cavity was fully curetted; have an additional curette of a different size ready

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text: No formal wound closure. Remove the tenaculum; confirm cervical hemostasis; remove retractors. No sutures. No dressing.

docs.title: Robotic-Assisted Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy (da Vinci TLH)
docs.text:
QUICK FACTS Wound Class: II (Clean-Contaminated) · Position: Low lithotomy → steep Trendelenburg · Avg time: 90–150 min · Team: console surgeon + bedside scrub tech + circulator + perineal assistant · Robot: da Vinci

What changes at the back table: You are stationed at the patient cart. The console surgeon controls all robotic arm movements remotely and cannot directly pass anything to themselves. Every suture — including the vaginal cuff closure — comes through the assistant port from your hands. A separate perineal assistant manages the uterine manipulator from below throughout the entire abdominal phase; the console surgeon directs manipulator positioning verbally.

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text: BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Uterine manipulator (RUMI or Koh system) placed vaginally before any abdominal approach — confirm it is in place and the perineal assistant is positioned before prepping; the case cannot begin without it - [ ] Robot arm drapes × 4: assist the circulator before docking - [ ] Ports: 12 mm camera port (umbilical); 2–3 × 8 mm robotic arm ports; 1 × 8–12 mm assistant port for the bedside scrub tech - [ ] Robotic instruments: ProGrasp forceps + monopolar curved scissors + bipolar Maryland forceps + large needle driver — confirm all seated - [ ] Energy device for pedicle ligation: da Vinci Vessel Sealer Extend or bipolar Maryland — confirm per preference card; thermal spread from the Vessel Sealer is 2–4 mm - [ ] Vaginal cuff closure suture: 0 Vicryl on CT-1 needle × 2; load on a laparoscopic needle holder; have immediately available when the uterus is delivered — passed through the assistant port; surgeon closes the cuff robotically - [ ] Specimen delivery: uterus exits vaginally through the colpotomy; no endobag required for the uterus (have one available if adnexa are removed) - [ ] Endobag for adnexa: have one unsealed on the back table if oophorectomy is planned

docs.title: Instruments
docs.text:
Instrument Notes EndoWrist ProGrasp forceps Tissue grasping and retraction via robotic arm EndoWrist monopolar curved scissors Dissection and cutting via robotic arm EndoWrist bipolar Maryland forceps Hemostasis via robotic arm EndoWrist large needle driver Suturing via robotic arm da Vinci Vessel Sealer Extend Pedicle ligation — thermal spread 2–4 mm; confirm per preference card Laparoscopic needle holder × 2 Passing 0 Vicryl through assistant port for vaginal cuff closure Laparoscopic grasper (5 mm) Assistant port retraction Laparoscopic suction/irrigation cannula For assistant port Endobag For adnexa if removed Robotic arm drapes × 4 Applied before docking 12 mm trocar × 1 Camera port 8 mm robotic trocars × 2–3 Robotic arm ports 8–12 mm assistant port trocar × 1 Bedside scrub tech working port

Position: Low lithotomy in Allen stirrups → steep Trendelenburg (30–45°) once ports are placed; gravity displaces the bowel cephalad and exposes the pelvic structures.

Prep: CHG/alcohol abdomen; betadine vaginal prep; perineal area prepped for the uterine manipulator.

Draping: Laparoscopic fenestrated sheet + perineal drape with vaginal access incorporated; robotic arm drapes applied before docking.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
Robotic da Vinci TLH mirrors the conventional TLH sequence (see Section 17.3) executed through robotic arms:

  1. Round ligaments divided bilaterally with the energy device
  2. Ureter identification: the surgeon identifies the ureter on each side before any clamp or energy device approaches the parametrium; do not pass instruments during this step
  3. IP ligaments (ovaries removed) or utero-ovarian ligaments (ovaries preserved): divided with energy device
  4. Uterine vessels: bladder reflected downward; energy device divides the uterine artery pedicles bilaterally — confirm the surgeon has visualized the ureter before each activation near the uterine vessels; delayed ureteral thermal injury from a missed ureter presents 5–10 days post-op
  5. Cardinal and uterosacral ligaments divided sequentially
  6. Vagina incised at the colpotomy cup of the uterine manipulator; uterus delivered vaginally by the perineal assistant
  7. Vaginal cuff closure: 0 Vicryl on CT-1 passed through the assistant port; console surgeon sutures the cuff laparoscopically; have the suture loaded and in hand before the uterus is out

⚠ WATCH FOR - Uterine manipulator response: the console surgeon controls the arms but not the manipulator; the perineal assistant must respond promptly to exposure requests; if the correct pedicle is not exposed, the surgeon will wait — do not pass the energy device until anatomy is exposed - Ureter thermal proximity: the Vessel Sealer and bipolar both spread heat 2–4 mm; a ureter not visualized before each activation is a ureter at risk; if the surgeon pauses near the parametrium, they are looking for the ureter — wait for them to call the instrument - Vaginal cuff suture timing: have the 0 Vicryl on CT-1 loaded and in your hand before the uterus is delivered; the surgeon will call for it immediately after the colpotomy is complete - CO₂ loss through the colpotomy: when the vagina is entered, pneumoperitoneum can escape around the manipulator; the perineal assistant should hold the manipulator firmly during the colpotomy step - Ovarian specimen extraction: if the ovaries are removed, place them in an endobag before extraction; tumor seeding of the port site is a risk if specimen is delivered loose

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Layer Suture Notes Vaginal cuff 0 Vicryl CT-1 running Passed through assistant port; sutured robotically Port sites ≥10 mm fascia 0 Vicryl Required Skin (all ports) 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular

Dressing: Small adhesive dressings over port sites.

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"In pelvic surgery, your Approach determines whether the ureter is safe. Your retractors determine whether the surgeon can see it."

  • In a C-section, the Doyen retractor is your most important tool. If it slips, the bladder enters the field. Hold it steady and do not let your grip fatigue.
  • In hysterectomy, keep your Heaney clamp supply visible and accessible. You will pass them in rapid sequence. Running out of clamps mid-case is not an option.
  • For a D&C, pre-arrange dilators in ascending order on the Mayo stand before the speculum goes in. You will not have time to sort them once the tenaculum is on the cervix.
  • The ureter in pelvic surgery is always at risk. If the surgeon stops and asks for better exposure near the parametrium, they are looking for the ureter. Do not rush them.

Next: Chapter 18 — Urology

docs.title: Anatomy You Need at the Table
docs.text:
Kidneys: Retroperitoneal; renal pelvis → ureter → bladder. Three narrowings where stones lodge: ureteropelvic junction (UPJ), pelvic brim, ureterovesical junction (UVJ).

Bladder: Muscular; trigone (posterior base) contains the two ureteral orifices and the internal urethral orifice.

Prostate: Surrounds the prostatic urethra. Peripheral zone (most cancers), transitional zone (BPH), central zone. The verumontanum is the distal safety landmark during TURP — do not resect past it; the external sphincter lies just beyond.

Male urethra: Prostatic → membranous (external sphincter — critical to preserve for continence) → penile/bulbar.

Why it matters at the back table: Urologic cases run heavily on scopes, irrigation, and energy devices through natural orifices. Your job is knowing which scope goes on which sheath, keeping irrigation running, and tracking fluid balance.

docs.title: Cystoscopy with Ureteral Stent Placement
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: II (Clean-Contaminated) · Position: Lithotomy · Avg time: 15–30 min · Team: surgeon only or + 1 · Fluoroscopy: required for stent placement · No incision

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text: BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Cystoscope sheath (16–22 Fr) + bridge + telescope (30° standard; 70° for lateral walls) — confirm all components are together and functional - [ ] C-arm sterile drape ready; fluoroscopy required for stent wire placement - [ ] Guidewires: hydrophilic guidewire (Glidewire) × 1; stiff guidewire × 1 backup - [ ] Double-J ureteral stent: confirm size (length and diameter) ordered; confirm it's on the field unopened - [ ] Stent pusher (inserter) compatible with the stent system - [ ] Irrigation: 1L sterile saline bags × 2 primed and running through the cystoscope irrigation tubing - [ ] Lubricating jelly: sterile; for urethral introduction - [ ] Cystoscopy drape with integrated collection pouch — fluid runs constantly; the pouch catches the runoff

docs.title: Instruments
docs.text:
Instrument Notes Cystoscope sheath (16–22 Fr) + obturator Urethral entry; obturator removed once in bladder Cystoscope bridge (working channel bridge) Attaches telescope to sheath; provides the working channel 30° telescope Standard bladder visualization 70° telescope Lateral wall and ureteral orifice visualization Irrigation tubing + 1 L sterile saline bags × 2 Spiked and primed; bladder distension and visualization medium Hydrophilic guidewire (Glidewire, 0.035") Primary wire for ureteral access Stiff guidewire (0.035") Backup wire if primary meets resistance Double-J ureteral stent Confirm length and diameter ordered; do not open until confirmed Stent pusher System-specific — confirm compatibility with stent before opening Foley catheter May be left post-procedure Cystoscopy drape with integrated collection pouch Fluid runoff collection Sterile lubricating jelly Urethral introduction C-arm sterile drape Fluoroscopic guidance during wire and stent placement

Position: Lithotomy (Allen or candy-cane stirrups).

Prep: Betadine; perineum, external genitalia, and inner thighs. Males: prep the glans and urethral meatus.

Draping: Lithotomy/cystoscopy drape with collection pouch; fenestrated aperture over the urethral meatus.

The Knife: No skin incision. The approach is through the natural orifice (urethra). The obturator tip of the cystoscope sheath is the entry instrument.

Retractors: The cystoscope sheath holds the urethra open. Irrigation fluid distends the bladder and creates the working space.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Lubricate the urethra; introduce the cystoscope sheath with obturator
  2. Remove obturator; attach the bridge and telescope; connect irrigation; distend the bladder
  3. Systematic inspection: all walls, trigone, both ureteral orifices, dome
  4. Stent placement: under fluoroscopy, pass a guidewire through the working channel up the ipsilateral ureter to the renal pelvis; pass the double-J stent over the guidewire with the pusher; withdraw the guidewire; proximal coil in the renal pelvis, distal coil in the bladder

⚠ WATCH FOR - Guidewire resistance: if the guidewire will not advance past a narrowing (UPJ, stone, stricture), do not force it — additional guidewire types (stiffer, angled) may be needed; have a second guidewire on the field - Stent mismatch: the pusher must match the stent system; a generic pusher on a specific stent will not seat correctly — confirm before opening - Irrigation bottle running dry: a cystoscope without irrigation deflates the bladder and collapses the visual field; maintain at least one backup bag primed and ready to switch without stopping - Fluoroscopy exposure: when the C-arm fires, step clear; do not hold instruments in the beam

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text: No wound closure. Remove all instruments; drain the bladder if needed. Foley catheter may be left in place post-procedure at the surgeon's discretion.

docs.title: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: II (Clean-Contaminated) · Position: Lithotomy · Avg time: 45–90 min · Team: surgeon only · No incision · Irrigation fluid: glycine 1.5% (monopolar) or saline (bipolar) — confirm with surgeon

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text: BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Resectoscope: confirm monopolar vs. bipolar system; gather the matching sheath (22–26 Fr), working element (cutting loop), and telescope - [ ] Irrigation fluid: glycine 1.5% (monopolar) or normal saline (bipolar) — do NOT mix these up; confirm with surgeon before spiking bags - [ ] Ellik evacuator on the field for evacuating tissue chips; use with saline (not glycine) - [ ] Three-way Foley catheter (22–24 Fr) with 30 mL balloon — needed at the end; have it ready - [ ] Track irrigation in vs. irrigation out throughout the case; document

docs.title: Instruments
docs.text:
Instrument Notes Resectoscope sheath (22–26 Fr) Confirm monopolar vs. bipolar system with surgeon before opening Resectoscope working element (cutting loop electrode) The active resection instrument 30° telescope for the resectoscope Visualization through the resectoscope Resectoscope irrigation tubing Connects irrigation fluid to the sheath Glycine 1.5% irrigation fluid Monopolar system only — do NOT use with bipolar Normal saline irrigation fluid Bipolar system only — confirm type before spiking bags Ellik evacuator Removes prostatic chips — use with saline only, never glycine Ball electrode Hemostasis; confirm it fits the working element Three-way Foley catheter (22–24 Fr, 30 mL balloon) Placed at case end for continuous bladder irrigation (CBI) CBI tubing + normal saline bags Set up at end of case for post-resection bladder irrigation Extra working element with fresh cutting loop Backup if primary loop breaks mid-case Fluid measurement / tracking log Circulator documents in vs. out throughout the entire case

Position: Lithotomy (Allen stirrups; legs slightly lower than standard — reduces prostatic venous pressure and bleeding).

Prep: Betadine; perineum and genitalia.

Draping: Cystoscopy drape with collection pouch.

The Knife: No skin incision. The resectoscope sheath passes through the urethra. The cutting element is the loop electrode (uses electrical current in cut mode to shave prostatic tissue).

Retractors: The resectoscope sheath (22–26 Fr) holds the urethra open. Irrigation distends the bladder and carries resected tissue chips away from the field.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Insert resectoscope sheath; attach working element (cutting loop), telescope, and irrigation
  2. Inspect the bladder; identify the verumontanum — the distal safety landmark; mark its position visually; resection must not extend past this point
  3. Systematically resect obstructing prostatic tissue in chips; work from the bladder neck toward the verumontanum; coagulate bleeding vessels with the ball electrode
  4. Evacuate prostatic chips periodically using the Ellik evacuator
  5. Final hemostasis; confirm no active bleeding before withdrawing the scope

⚠ WATCH FOR - TURP syndrome: absorption of hypotonic glycine irrigation into the circulation causes hyponatremia, hypertension, bradycardia, and visual changes in the patient; if the circulator or anesthesia reports that the fluid balance is deeply negative (>1 L absorbed), alert the team — this is an emergency - Resection past the verumontanum: crossing this landmark damages the external sphincter → permanent incontinence; if the surgeon is approaching the verumontanum, confirm their awareness - Loop electrode failure: if the loop breaks or the current stops cutting, have a backup working element on the field; know how to switch the loop - Bleeding obscuring the field: aggressive prostatic bleeding drops visibility suddenly; have the ball electrode ready for immediate coagulation

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text: No wound closure. A three-way Foley catheter (22–24 Fr, 30 mL balloon) is placed into the bladder; balloon positioned at the bladder neck; continuous bladder irrigation (CBI) initiated to prevent clot formation. Brief catheter traction may tamponade the prostatic fossa.

docs.title: Laparoscopic Radical Nephrectomy
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: I (Clean) · Position: Lateral decubitus, operative side up · Avg time: 90–150 min · Team: surgeon + 1 assistant · Implants: none · Specimen: kidney to pathology

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text: BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Laparoscopic tower: camera, light source, insufflator — all on and functioning before draping - [ ] Endo-GIA with vascular loads (white/gray) × 2–3 for renal artery and vein; confirm with surgeon - [ ] Medium and large titanium clips + clip applier for smaller vessels - [ ] Specimen extraction bag (endobag) — large enough for the kidney - [ ] Extraction port incision planning: surgeon will extend one port site; have #10 blade and longer instruments ready for that step - [ ] Kidney rest: confirm it is elevated and locked before prepping - [ ] 0 Vicryl for fascial closure at extraction site; 4-0 Monocryl for skin

docs.title: Instruments
docs.text:
Instrument Notes #11 blade on #3 handle × 4 All port incisions #10 blade on #3 handle Extraction port incision extension 10–12 mm camera trocar × 1 Camera port 5 mm trocars × 2–3 Working ports 10 mm 0° or 30° laparoscope + camera + light cord Confirm white balance and light before draping Laparoscopic graspers × 2 Tissue manipulation and retraction Laparoscopic Maryland dissector Hilar dissection and vessel exposure Laparoscopic scissors Tissue cutting and ureter division Laparoscopic L-hook electrocautery or Harmonic/LigaSure Energy dissection throughout Laparoscopic clip applier with medium and large titanium clips Smaller vessel control Endo-GIA stapler + vascular loads (white/gray) × 2–3 Renal artery and vein division — confirm load type before every fire Large specimen endobag Must be large enough for the kidney; do not force extraction Laparoscopic fan retractor Colon medial retraction to expose the retroperitoneum Laparoscopic suction/irrigation Field irrigation and fluid removal 0 Vicryl Fascial closure at extraction site 4-0 Monocryl Skin closure at all port sites Yankauer suction Backup suction

Position: Lateral decubitus, operative side up; beanbag positioner. Kidney rest elevated under the flank; table slightly flexed at the waist to open the flank space. Confirm stability before draping.

Prep: CHG/alcohol; entire flank circumferentially from lower rib margin to iliac crest, midline anteriorly to the spine posteriorly.

Draping: Split sheet under the patient → operative flank exposed → Ioban incise drape may seal drape edges.

The Knife: #11 blade for all port incisions. 10–12 mm camera port at the umbilicus or lateral; two or three 5 mm working ports in the flank; hand-assist port (Pfannenstiel or lower quadrant) if hand-assisted technique.

Retractors: Pneumoperitoneum provides the working space. Laparoscopic fan retractor or grasper retracts the colon medially. Hand-assist port provides direct tactile retraction (hand-assisted cases).

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Access the peritoneal cavity; place trocars under direct vision
  2. Reflect the colon medially along the white line of Toldt; expose the retroperitoneum and kidney
  3. Identify the renal hilum: dissect and divide the renal artery first (Endo-GIA vascular load or clips + divided); then the renal vein
  4. Free the kidney from Gerota's fascia; preserve the adrenal gland if not involved by tumor
  5. Divide the ureter distally with clips; specimen completely freed
  6. Place specimen in endobag; extend one port incision to extract the kidney

⚠ WATCH FOR - Artery before vein, always: if the renal vein is divided before the artery, the kidney engorges with blood and bleeds dramatically during the remainder of the dissection — this is an unrecoverable mistake during the case; confirm the sequence with the surgeon before handing any stapler or clip near the hilum - Endo-GIA load selection: vascular loads only at the hilum; confirm the load type with the surgeon before every fire - Inferior vena cava proximity: the right renal vein is very short; on the right side, the IVC is immediately medial; if the stapler misfires or the vein tears, this bleeds catastrophically — have a second vascular load immediately at hand during right-side nephrectomy - Endobag integrity: if the bag tears during extraction, tumor cells can seed the port site; handle the specimen carefully; do not force extraction

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Layer Suture Notes Extraction site fascia 0 Vicryl interrupted or running Close securely Port sites ≥10 mm fascia 0 Vicryl Carter-Thomason or direct suture Skin (all sites) 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular

Dressing: Small adhesive dressings.

docs.title: Robotic-Assisted Radical Prostatectomy (RARP)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: I (Clean) · Position: Supine, steep Trendelenburg · Avg time: 2.5–4 hrs · Team: console surgeon + bedside scrub tech + circulator · Robot: da Vinci (all robotic arm instruments passed through the assistant port by the scrub tech)

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text: BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Robot drapes: sterile robotic arm drapes × 3–4; assist the circulator draping each arm before docking - [ ] Robotic instruments: ProGrasp forceps, monopolar scissors, needle drivers × 2 — confirm all in the room and seated correctly in their cannulas - [ ] 3-0 Monocryl on a UR-6 needle × 2 for the Van Velthoven vesicourethral anastomosis; load BEFORE the case reaches that step - [ ] 2-0 Vicryl on a large CT needle for the DVC suture ligature — this is passed through the assistant port; have it on a loaded needle holder in advance - [ ] Specimen extraction bag (endobag) passed through the assistant port - [ ] JP drain (10 mm Blake or round): suture (2-0 nylon) for securing; placed through an assistant port site at the end - [ ] Foley catheter (18 or 20 Fr) on the back table — placed through the urethra before tying the anastomosis

docs.title: Instruments
docs.text:
Instrument Notes EndoWrist ProGrasp forceps Tissue grasping and retraction via robotic arm EndoWrist monopolar curved scissors Dissection — cold scissors only during nerve-sparing steps EndoWrist bipolar Maryland forceps Hemostasis via robotic arm EndoWrist large needle drivers × 2 Vesicourethral anastomosis suturing Laparoscopic needle holder × 2 Passing sutures through assistant port — DVC 2-0 Vicryl and anastomosis 3-0 Monocryl Laparoscopic clip applier (Hem-o-lok clips) Vessel clipping through assistant port Laparoscopic suction/irrigation For assistant port Specimen endobag Prostate extraction through assistant port Robotic arm drapes × 4 Applied before docking 12 mm trocar × 1 Camera port 8 mm robotic trocars × 3–4 Robotic arm ports 10–12 mm assistant port trocar × 1 Bedside scrub tech working port Foley catheter (18 or 20 Fr) On back table visible throughout — placed before final anastomosis throw JP drain (10 mm Blake drain) Placed near anastomosis at case end 2-0 nylon JP drain securing suture 3-0 Monocryl on UR-6 × 2 Van Velthoven anastomosis — pre-load 20 minutes before needed

Position: Supine; steep Trendelenburg (30–45°). Arms tucked bilaterally. Padded shoulder supports to prevent sliding cephalad. The robot is docked over the patient's lower body after port placement.

Prep: CHG/alcohol; umbilicus to mid-thigh, full width; include the perineum.

Draping: Laparoscopic lower abdominal draping; sterile robotic arm drapes (applied before docking).

The Knife: #11 blade for all port incisions. 12 mm camera port just above the umbilicus; three or four 8 mm robotic arm ports in a fan configuration across the lower abdomen; one 10–12 mm assistant port for the bedside scrub tech.

Retractors: Pneumoperitoneum + steep Trendelenburg displace bowel cephalad. Robotic ProGrasp forceps provide intraoperative retraction. Bedside scrub tech provides additional retraction through the assistant port with a laparoscopic grasper.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Ports placed under direct vision; robot docked to the ports
  2. Incise peritoneum to enter the space of Retzius; bladder falls posteriorly
  3. Divide the endopelvic fascia; ligate the dorsal venous complex (DVC) with 2-0 Vicryl — passed through the assistant port by the scrub tech
  4. Dissect the bladder neck; identify and divide the urethra anteriorly
  5. Dissect the posterior prostate off the rectum; divide posterior urethra and seminal vesicles
  6. Nerve-sparing dissection (if planned): preserve neurovascular bundles laterally using cold scissors only — no energy
  7. Divide the urethra at the apex; remove specimen in an endobag through the assistant port
  8. Vesicourethral anastomosis: 3-0 Monocryl running suture (Van Velthoven — two sutures from each side); Foley catheter placed before the final throw is tied

⚠ WATCH FOR - Instrument handoff timing: the console surgeon operates continuously; they will call for sutures, clips, and instruments without pausing — have the 3-0 Monocryl for the anastomosis fully loaded and ready 20 minutes before it will be needed; do not make the surgeon wait - DVC suture passing: the 2-0 Vicryl for the DVC ligature must pass through the assistant port on a loaded needle holder; practice the motion for passing through a 12 mm port before the case — the angle matters - Nerve-sparing designation: if the surgeon calls "nerve spare," cold scissors only on that side — no bipolar, no Bovie; hand only what is asked for; do not guess - Anastomosis suture twist: if the 3-0 Monocryl twists during the running anastomosis, the suture must be cut and reloaded — have a backup suture loaded and immediately available - Foley catheter timing: the catheter must be placed into the urethra before the last throw of the anastomosis is tied; if it is not on the field, the case stops — keep it on the back table visible throughout the case

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Layer Suture Notes Pelvic peritoneum 3-0 Vicryl running Close over the anastomosis 12 mm port site fascia 0 Vicryl Required All port site skin 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular JP drain 2-0 nylon Placed near the anastomosis; exits through an assistant port site

Dressing: Small adhesive dressings. Foley catheter remains 5–7 days post-op.

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"In urology, the Approach is often through a natural orifice. The irrigation fluid is your medium, your light, and your retractor all at once — manage it carefully."

  • In TURP, track every liter in and out. A deeply negative fluid balance is a medical emergency, not a notation on the OR record.
  • In nephrectomy, the renal artery is always divided before the vein. This is not a preference — it is the sequence that prevents catastrophic blood loss.
  • In RARP, your job at the bedside is anticipation. The console surgeon cannot stop mid-dissection. Pre-load the anastomosis suture before the specimen is out of the field.
  • Cystoscopy runs on irrigation. An empty bladder collapses the visual field instantly. Keep a backup bag primed and ready to switch without pausing.

Next: Chapter 19 — Otolaryngology (ENT)

docs.title: Anatomy You Need at the Table
docs.text:
Ear: External canal → tympanic membrane → ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes) → cochlea (hearing) and semicircular canals (balance). Facial nerve (CN VII) runs through the mastoid bone — at risk in any mastoid surgery.

Nose/Sinuses: Paranasal sinuses drain through the ostiomeatal complex. Lateral wall of the ethmoid is the lamina papyracea (paper-thin; separates sinus from orbit). Roof is the skull base/cribriform plate.

Thyroid/Parathyroid: Thyroid lobes connected by isthmus at the 2nd–4th tracheal rings. The recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN) runs in the tracheoesophageal groove and enters the larynx just posterior to the ligament of Berry — the most dangerous millimeter in thyroid surgery.

Why it matters at the back table: ENT cases are narrow fields with microscopic anatomy. Your passing must be controlled, quiet, and precise. One wrong instrument handed at the wrong angle can cause a nerve injury or orbital entry.

docs.title: Tonsillectomy and Adenoidectomy (T&A)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: II (Clean-Contaminated) — oropharynx is not sterile · Position: Supine, shoulder roll, head extended · Avg time: 20–45 min · Team: surgeon only or + 1 · Airway: shared with anesthesia (RAE tube)

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text: BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Crowe-Davis (or Jennings/McIvor) mouth gag: confirm size appropriate for the patient (pediatric vs. adult); confirm the suspension arm is available (hooks from Mayo stand or ceiling mount) - [ ] Tonsil clamps (Allis or Hurd): × 2 minimum on the Mayo stand - [ ] ESU pencil or coblation wand: confirm the correct energy device per surgeon preference; generator settings confirmed - [ ] Sponge count: tonsil sponges (tonsil balls) are small, heavily used in the oropharynx, and life-threatening if retained — count vigilantly before AND after - [ ] Specimen cups × 2 labeled: left tonsil, right tonsil (pathology) - [ ] Adenoid curette (La Force adenotome) if adenoidectomy included - [ ] Suction: Yankauer for secretions and blood; Frazier for the narrow adenoid field

docs.title: Instruments
docs.text:
Instrument Notes Crowe-Davis mouth gag + tongue blade Self-retaining mouth retractor; suspends from Mayo stand arm or ceiling mount; most critical exposure instrument McIvor gag (alternate) Alternative mouth gag; surgeon preference Allis clamps × 2 Grasp and provide traction on tonsil during dissection Hurd tonsil dissector and pillar retractor Blunt dissection and pillar retraction Yankauer suction Primary suction for blood and secretions in the oropharynx Frazier suction tip (#5 or #7) Narrow-field suction for adenoid space and tight recesses Tonsil sponges (tonsil balls) Small gauze squares for packing and hemostasis; counted as retained foreign body risk Adenoid curettes × 3 (LaForce or Barnhill, graduated sizes) Remove adenoid tissue from the nasopharynx Adenoid punch / basket forceps Basket-style punch for trimming residual adenoid tissue Tydings tonsil snare (wire snare) Divides the tonsillar pedicle at the base Bipolar electrosurgical forceps Precise hemostasis; preferred over monopolar in the oropharynx ESU pencil with tonsil tip (monopolar) Electrosurgical dissection if electrocautery technique; confirm surgeon preference Coblation wand Coblation technique alternative; confirm energy device type with surgeon preoperatively Tonsil needle (curved needle on large handle) For suture ligation of tonsillar pillar bleeding; load 3-0 chromic before the case #12 blade on #3 handle (or curved tonsil scissors) Cold dissection technique; fine curved blade for submucosal dissection Periosteal elevator (small) Blunt dissection of tonsil from its capsule in cold dissection Nasopharyngoscope or laryngeal mirror Visualization of nasopharynx for adenoidectomy assessment Specimen cups × 2 (labeled L/R tonsil) Pathology specimens; label immediately after each tonsil is removed

Position: Supine; shoulder roll under the shoulder blades to extend the neck; head in a foam donut ring.

Prep: No traditional skin prep — the oropharynx is a non-sterile field. The oral mucosa is not prepped with antiseptic. Eyes are taped closed and protected.

Draping: Head drape across the top of the head; chest drape over the patient's body.

The Knife: No skin incision. The monopolar ESU pencil (or coblation wand, or cold scissors) makes the first cut through the anterior tonsillar pillar mucosa. For cold dissection: #12 blade or curved scissors.

Retractors: Crowe-Davis mouth gag placed between the teeth/gums; tongue blade depresses and exposes the posterior oropharynx; gag suspended from a Mayo stand arm or ceiling hook above the patient. This is the entire retractor system for the case.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Place Crowe-Davis gag and suspend it; surgeon works from the head of the table looking into the open mouth
  2. Grasp the tonsil with Allis or Hurd clamp for traction
  3. Incise mucosa of the anterior tonsillar pillar with ESU; develop the dissection plane between tonsil and its fibrous bed
  4. Dissect to the base; divide the tonsillar pedicle; place tonsil in specimen cup
  5. Achieve hemostasis in the tonsillar fossa (ESU; suture ligation if needed with 3-0 chromic on a tonsil needle)
  6. Repeat on the opposite side
  7. Adenoidectomy: visualize the nasopharynx with a mirror or nasopharyngoscope; remove adenoid tissue with a suction coagulator, La Force adenotome, or coblation wand
  8. Pack the nasopharynx with a moist sponge while achieving hemostasis; remove the sponge before removing the gag

⚠ WATCH FOR - Sponge retention: a sponge left in the oropharynx after gag removal obstructs the airway — this is immediately life-threatening; before removing the gag, confirm every tonsil sponge is accounted for; call your count clearly - Gag slippage during the case: if the Crowe-Davis gag loses suspension, the tongue falls forward and the field collapses; reset the suspension before the surgeon proceeds - Tonsillar fossa bleeding: active arterial bleeding in the fossa may require a suture ligature (3-0 chromic on a tonsil needle); have it loaded and on the Mayo stand before the case begins - Coblation wand tip clogging: the coblation wand tip clogs with eschar; have a clean wet sponge on the Mayo stand for wiping it; a clogged tip stops cutting entirely

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
No formal wound closure. Mucosal beds heal by secondary intention.

Before removing the gag: confirm all sponges are out; confirm the mouth is clear of all packing. Remove the gag carefully.

Dressing: None at the operative site.

docs.title: Thyroidectomy (Total)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: I (Clean) · Position: Supine, shoulder roll, neck extended, slight reverse Trendelenburg · Avg time: 60–120 min · Team: surgeon + 1 assistant · Nerve monitoring: NIM ETT required · Specimen: thyroid lobe(s) to pathology

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text: BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] NIM (nerve integrity monitor) ETT: confirm it is placed correctly by anesthesia before draping; test the system with the nerve stimulator before dissection begins - [ ] Nerve stimulator (Prass probe or equivalent): on the field; confirm it produces an audible NIM tone before use - [ ] Bipolar forceps: essential for hemostasis near the RLN — do not use monopolar near the nerve - [ ] Harmonic scalpel or LigaSure (surgeon preference) for vessel sealing; confirm which device is requested - [ ] Small vein retractors and right-angle retractors for posterior thyroid lobe exposure - [ ] Parathyroid autotransplant supplies: if a parathyroid is accidentally devascularized, it is minced and implanted into the SCM — have a small basin and saline ready - [ ] Specimen cups × 2 (left lobe, right lobe); intraoperative PTH test tube if parathyroid testing is anticipated

docs.title: Instruments
docs.text:
Instrument Notes #15 blade on #3 handle × 2 Kocher collar incision and subcutaneous dissection; fine control Metzenbaum scissors (curved) Dissection of subplatysmal flaps and strap muscle separation Fine Metzenbaum scissors Delicate dissection around parathyroids and near the RLN DeBakey tissue forceps Atraumatic tissue handling throughout Adson forceps (1×2 teeth) Skin edge and subcutaneous tissue handling Kelly hemostats × 6 Vessel control and tissue clamping in superficial layers Crile hemostats × 6 Hemostasis; vessel pedicle clamping Right-angle (Mixter) clamps × 4 Passing ties around vessels; dissecting around vascular structures Mayo-Hegar needle drivers × 2 Suture placement; closure Lahey thyroid tenaculum Grasps and rotates the thyroid lobe for posterior exposure Army-Navy retractors × 2 Skin and subplatysmal flap retraction Small Richardson retractors × 2 Deeper retraction of strap muscles Gelpi or Weitlaner self-retaining retractor Hands-free strap muscle retraction during lobe dissection Nerve stimulator probe (fine-tip insulated) Intraoperative RLN identification and confirmation; connects to NIM system Harmonic scalpel or LigaSure vessel sealer Vessel ligation and tissue sealing; confirm surgeon device preference Bipolar electrosurgical forceps Hemostasis within 1 cm of the RLN; do NOT use monopolar near the nerve ESU pencil (monopolar) General hemostasis away from the nerve Yankauer suction Primary suction for blood and irrigation fluid JP drain (small, 7 mm flat) Optional drain placement; have available; 2-0 nylon for securing Small basin with saline Parathyroid autotransplant: mince any devascularized gland into this basin for SCM implantation

Position: Supine; shoulder roll under the shoulder blades; neck extended and elevated. Head in a donut ring. Slight reverse Trendelenburg.

Prep: CHG/alcohol; chin to upper chest, lateral to both SCM borders.

Draping: Head drape → four small towels framing the low collar incision → thyroid drape (split sheet).

The Knife: #15 blade on #3 handle. Kocher incision — low transverse collar incision in a natural skin crease, 2–4 cm above the sternal notch; 4–8 cm depending on gland size. Subplatysmal flaps developed above and below.

Retractors: Army-Navy retractors for skin and subplatysmal layers. Deep: small Weitlaner or Gelpi in the strap muscle layer. Small right-angle or vein retractors to rotate each thyroid lobe medially and expose the posterior surface where the RLN and parathyroids are found.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Incise platysma; elevate subplatysmal flaps cephalad to the thyroid cartilage, caudal to the sternal notch
  2. Separate strap muscles in the midline (avascular raphe); retract laterally
  3. Identify the superior pole; ligate the superior thyroid artery and vein close to the thyroid capsule (avoid the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve)
  4. Identify the RLN in the tracheoesophageal groove; trace it cephalad to its laryngeal entry; confirm with nerve stimulator; keep it in view for the entire case
  5. Identify and preserve all four parathyroid glands (brownish, 5 mm, on the posterior thyroid capsule); if one is accidentally devascularized, autotransplant it to the SCM
  6. Divide the ligament of Berry with fine dissection — this is where the RLN is closest to the thyroid and most often injured; work slowly
  7. Divide the isthmus; remove the thyroid specimen
  8. Confirm parathyroid identification; confirm RLN integrity with nerve stimulator

⚠ WATCH FOR - NIM silence: if the NIM tone disappears after a dissection step, the RLN may have been compressed, stretched, or cut — alert the surgeon; do not proceed until the nerve is visualized and stimulated again - Bipolar vs. monopolar near the nerve: when dissecting within 1 cm of the RLN, pass only bipolar — never pass the Bovie pencil in that region; the thermal spread from monopolar at this proximity causes nerve injury even without direct contact - Parathyroid color loss: if a parathyroid gland turns dusky or black, it has lost its blood supply; alert the surgeon immediately for autotransplant decision - Ligament of Berry dissection speed: the surgeon will slow down here deliberately; do not anticipate the next step; wait; pass only what is specifically asked for

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Layer Suture Notes Strap muscles 3-0 Vicryl running Approximate the midline raphe Platysma 3-0 Vicryl interrupted Subcutaneous 3-0 Vicryl Skin 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular + Steri-strips Cosmetically critical incision Drain Optional JP through separate stab 2-0 nylon; most surgeons omit for uncomplicated cases

Dressing: Steri-strips; light gauze.

docs.title: Tympanoplasty (Eardrum Repair)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: I (Clean) · Position: Supine, head turned away from the operative ear · Avg time: 60–90 min · Team: surgeon only · Microscope: required · Implants: temporalis fascia graft (autologous)

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Operating microscope sterile drape: drape it before the surgeon asks; confirm the focal length is set - [ ] Ear speculum set (multiple sizes): pull the largest that will fit comfortably in the patient's ear canal - [ ] Micro-ENT instruments: right-angle pick, Rosen needle, sickle knife, Bellucci scissors, cup forceps — all oriented for ear canal use (angled, small) - [ ] Gelfoam (gelatin sponge) cut into small pledgets: needed for middle ear packing and graft support - [ ] Temporalis fascia graft preparation: surgeon will harvest postauricularly; have a small flat surface (back of a glove wrapper or a clean card) ready to press the graft flat; allow 10–15 min to dry before placing - [ ] Local anesthetic (1% lidocaine + epinephrine) drawn up before the case: used for vasoconstriction in the ear canal - [ ] Do NOT use CHG near the ear canal — it is ototoxic; use betadine for the prep

Position: Supine; head turned away from the operative ear; foam donut ring.

Prep: Betadine; auricle, surrounding scalp, postauricular skin. Do not use CHG in or near the ear canal.

Draping: Head drape framing the ear → small fenestrated adhesive-bordered ear drape over the operative ear. Microscope draped with sterile scope cover before use.

The Knife: #15 blade. Postauricular approach: incision in the postauricular crease for graft harvest and wider access. Transcanal: fine sickle knife to incise the ear canal skin for the tympanomeatal flap.

Retractors: Ear speculum in the canal provides the visual field. The speculum is held by the operating microscope's focal axis; a speculum holder attached to the OR table may hold it hands-free. Postauricular: small Weitlaner or mastoid retractor.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Inject local anesthetic (1% lidocaine + epinephrine) into the ear canal for vasoconstriction
  2. Harvest temporalis fascia through the postauricular incision; press flat; allow to dry
  3. Incise the ear canal skin; elevate the tympanomeatal flap (tympanic membrane + canal skin as one unit); expose the middle ear and perforation
  4. Assess the ossicular chain; determine graft placement
  5. Underlay graft: place Gelfoam pledgets in the middle ear for graft support; place the dry temporalis fascia graft medial (underneath) to the tympanic membrane remnant, covering the perforation completely
  6. Reposition the tympanomeatal flap over the graft; pack the canal with Gelfoam pledgets

⚠ WATCH FOR - Graft too wet: if the fascia graft is still moist when placed, it will shrink as it dries and pull away from the perforation margins — allow full drying time; press the graft flat and wait at least 10 minutes - Gelfoam count: Gelfoam pledgets placed in the middle ear are small and must be tracked; account for each one placed and each one removed - Ossicular chain disruption: if the malleus or incus is inadvertently displaced during flap elevation, the surgeon will need to reposition it before placing the graft; have cup forceps and the right-angle pick immediately available - Instrument angle at the microscope: every instrument passed to the surgeon must arrive with the working tip pointing toward the ear canal; a backward-oriented instrument at microscopic scale is unusable

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Layer Suture Notes Postauricular periosteum + subcutaneous 3-0 Vicryl Postauricular skin 4-0 nylon interrupted or running Ear canal Gelfoam packing (no sutures) Canal heals around the packing

Dressing: Large cotton ball in the concha; cotton/gauze behind the ear; elastic circumferential Kerlix head wrap (mild compression to reduce hematoma).

docs.title: Functional Endoscopic Sinus Surgery (FESS)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: II (Clean-Contaminated) — nasal cavity is non-sterile · Position: Supine, slight reverse Trendelenburg · Avg time: 45–90 min · Team: surgeon only · No incision · Nasal decongestion required before starting

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Rigid nasal endoscopes: 0°, 30°, 45°, and 70° — confirm all are in the room and camera/light cord fits each - [ ] Microdebrider: blade loaded and spinning before the first polyp; confirm suction is running through it - [ ] Nasal instruments: Blakesley-Wilde forceps (straight and angled), backbiting forceps, Kerrison punch for bone, ball probe, sphenoid punch - [ ] Oxymetazoline-soaked pledgets: placed before draping to decongest the mucosa; removed before the endoscope enters - [ ] Merocel nasal packing or NasoPore: on the field for post-case packing; confirm surgeon preference - [ ] Suction Franger or Frazier: constant suction for blood and irrigation during the case - [ ] Eye protection confirmed: ointment + tape/shield in place before draping (lamina papyracea is millimeters from the orbital contents)

Position: Supine; slight reverse Trendelenburg (reduces nasal blood flow). Head neutral on a foam donut ring.

Prep: Betadine or CHG/alcohol applied to the face, avoiding the eyes. Oxymetazoline-soaked pledgets placed endoscopically before prep to decongest the mucosa.

Draping: Face drape with aperture exposing both nostrils and the patient's mouth. Eyes protected (ointment + taped closed or shielded) within the drape. Endoscope tower positioned at the head with the monitor visible to the surgeon.

The Knife: No external incision. All work is endoscopic through the nostrils. The first "cut" is a sickle knife incision into the uncinate process.

Retractors: The rigid nasal endoscope is the visualization and access tool. Held by the non-dominant hand; operative instrument in the dominant hand. No conventional retractors.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Insert 0° endoscope; inspect the nasal cavity; confirm nasal decongestion is adequate
  2. Uncinectomy: incise and remove the uncinate process with a sickle knife and Blakesley forceps — opens access to the ostiomeatal complex
  3. Anterior ethmoidectomy: open anterior ethmoid air cells systematically
  4. Maxillary antrostomy: widen the maxillary sinus ostium; trim with backbiting forceps
  5. Proceed to posterior ethmoid, sphenoid, or frontal sinuses as indicated
  6. Microdebrider used for polypectomy or large volumes of diseased mucosa
  7. Irrigate; achieve hemostasis; assess sinus drainage

⚠ WATCH FOR - Orbital entry through the lamina papyracea: if the bone is dehiscent or the instrument wanders laterally, the orbit can be entered; signs include orbital fat herniation, periorbital ecchymosis, and eye movement on the endoscope monitor; call it out immediately - Skull base violation (cribriform plate): if the instrument passes superiorly beyond the ethmoid roof, intracranial entry and CSF leak occur; clear fluid draining from the nose mid-case = CSF until proven otherwise; stop and alert the surgeon - Microdebrider suction failure: if suction stops mid-case, the microdebrider clogs with tissue; have a backup blade and know how to switch it; loss of suction during heavy polypectomy makes the field unworkable quickly - Bleeding obscuring the endoscope lens: blood on the endoscope tip blocks the view instantly; have a bulb syringe with warm saline to rinse the scope; surgeon cannot operate on a fogged lens

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
No external wound closure — all work is endonasal.

Nasal packing (if used): Merocel sponges or NasoPore placed into the nasal cavities; or no packing if the surgeon prefers.

Dressing: Nasal drip pad (folded gauze under the nose) taped to the upper lip.

docs.title: Tracheostomy
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: II (Clean-Contaminated) · Position: Supine, shoulder roll, neck extended · Avg time: 20–45 min · Team: surgeon + 1 assistant · Airway: coordinated with anesthesia — the ETT is withdrawn at the critical moment

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Tracheostomy tube: confirm size and type ordered; cuff inflated and tested before the case; obturator inside the tube; extra tube one size smaller on the field (for emergency if the initial tube won't seat) - [ ] Stay sutures: 2-0 nylon × 2 (placed through each lateral tracheal ring; allow emergency reopening of the stoma) - [ ] 12-gauge needle + syringe for tracheal aspiration (confirms entry into the trachea before incising) - [ ] Tracheostomy ties on the field for securing the tube after placement - [ ] Anesthesia coordination: confirm the anesthesia provider knows the sequence — they will withdraw the ETT to just above the tracheal incision site on your surgeon's call; do NOT cut the trachea while the ETT cuff is still at that level

Position: Supine; shoulder roll under the shoulder blades; neck extended. Slight reverse Trendelenburg.

Prep: CHG/alcohol; chin to upper chest, full width.

Draping: Four small towels framing the midline anterior neck incision → thyroid drape or split sheet.

The Knife: #15 blade on #3 handle. Transverse or vertical midline incision approximately 2 cm below the cricoid cartilage (at the level of the 2nd–4th tracheal rings); 3–4 cm.

Retractors: Army-Navy retractors for skin and subcutaneous. Small Weitlaner in the strap muscle layer. Small right-angle retractors hold the tracheal edges open after the tracheal incision is made.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Transverse skin incision; divide subcutaneous tissue and platysma
  2. Separate strap muscles vertically in the midline; identify and retract or divide the thyroid isthmus
  3. Identify the trachea; palpate the cricoid and count down to the 2nd–3rd tracheal ring
  4. Needle aspiration: 12-gauge needle + syringe; aspiration of air confirms tracheal entry before cutting
  5. Place stay sutures (2-0 nylon) through each lateral tracheal ring adjacent to the planned opening
  6. Anesthesia withdraws the ETT to just above the incision site
  7. Tracheal incision: vertical or horizontal between the 2nd and 3rd (or 3rd and 4th) tracheal rings; or Bjork flap (inferiorly-based cartilage flap sutured to the skin)
  8. Insert the tracheostomy tube with obturator; remove the obturator; anesthesia confirms ventilation; inflate the cuff

⚠ WATCH FOR - ETT cuff still at the tracheal incision level: if anesthesia has not withdrawn the ETT before the tracheal incision, the scalpel or scissors will cut the cuff — the patient loses their airway; confirm ETT withdrawal with anesthesia by name before handing the blade for the tracheal incision - Tracheostomy tube will not seat: if the tube does not advance easily, do not force it; the tube may be the wrong size or there may be a cartilaginous ring obstructing; have the next size smaller immediately available - Stay suture management: the two 2-0 nylon stay sutures hold the tracheal opening for the first 72 hours if the tube is accidentally dislodged; keep them labeled (left/right) and secure them to the skin before leaving the OR

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Layer Suture Notes Wound edges around tube 3-0 nylon Loosely closed; intentional gap prevents subcutaneous emphysema Tracheostomy tube Ties through flanges, around neck Two-finger rule: two fingers must fit between the ties and the neck

Dressing: Pre-cut gauze split around the tracheostomy tube at the flange.

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"In ENT, the Approach is often the narrowest pathway to the most critical anatomy. One millimeter of traction at the wrong moment ends a nerve."

  • During thyroidectomy, keep absolute silence when the surgeon stimulates the RLN and listens for the NIM tone. Background OR noise masks the signal.
  • In a T&A, every sponge that goes into the pharynx must come out before the gag is removed. A retained tonsil sponge is an immediate airway emergency.
  • For tympanoplasty, pass every instrument with the working tip oriented toward the ear canal. At microscope magnification, a backward instrument is useless.
  • In tracheostomy, confirm ETT withdrawal by name with anesthesia before handing the tracheal incision knife. This communication step prevents the most catastrophic complication in the entire procedure.

Next: Chapter 20 — Ophthalmology

docs.title: Anatomy You Need at the Table
docs.text:
External: Cornea (transparent anterior dome), sclera (white wall), limbus (cornea-sclera junction), conjunctiva (mucous membrane lining the inner lids and sclera).

Anterior segment: Anterior chamber (aqueous humor), iris, lens (crystalline; focuses light onto retina).

Posterior segment: Vitreous (gel-filled cavity), retina (macula = central vision), choroid (vascular layer beneath the retina), optic nerve (CN II).

IOP: Normal intraocular pressure 10–21 mmHg. Maintained by aqueous humor production and drainage.

Critical fact: The eye is a closed pressurized sphere. Any breach of the outer coats (cornea, sclera) that is not controlled causes sudden loss of IOP — which can trigger expulsive hemorrhage, vitreous loss, or collapse of the eye. Nothing in ophthalmic surgery is casual.

Why it matters at the back table: Ophthalmic surgery is the smallest-scale work in the OR. Every pass happens under a microscope. Instruments must arrive oriented correctly, silently, and precisely. An IOL touched by a glove is destroyed. A wrong solution instilled into the eye is catastrophic.

docs.title: Cataract Extraction with IOL Implantation (Phacoemulsification)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: I (Clean) · Position: Supine, head in foam donut or flat headrest · Avg time: 15–30 min · Team: surgeon only or + 1 scrub · Microscope: required · Implants: intraocular lens (IOL)

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] IOL confirmed: type and power confirmed with the surgeon before the case begins; IOL loaded into the injector cartridge per manufacturer's instructions — do NOT touch the IOL with gloves or instruments - [ ] Phacoemulsification machine: footpedal set up; confirmed tubing flushed and primed; BSS bags spiked - [ ] OVD (viscoelastic): Healon, DisCoVisc, or equivalent; on the field on the Mayo stand; syringe pre-drawn - [ ] Keratome (primary incision knife): correct width for the IOL system; on the Mayo stand - [ ] Paracentesis knife: on the Mayo stand; this makes the side-port entry - [ ] Cystotome (or capsulorhexis forceps): for the continuous curvilinear capsulorhexis - [ ] Eye speculum: size appropriate for the patient; confirm it opens to the correct width - [ ] BSS in a 3 mL syringe with a 27-gauge cannula: for hydrodissection and stromal hydration - [ ] Do NOT use CHG anywhere near the eye — it is directly toxic to the corneal endothelium; use betadine only - [ ] Dilating drops confirmed given 30–60 minutes before the case; confirm the pupil is fully dilated before incision

Position: Supine; head in a foam donut or flat headrest; operating microscope directly overhead. Slight reverse Trendelenburg in some setups.

Prep: 5% betadine drops instilled into the conjunctival sac immediately before draping — this is the primary antiseptic. External periocular skin prepped with betadine paint. Never use CHG near the eye.

Draping: Ophthalmic aperture adhesive drape placed directly over the periorbital skin; lashes are folded away from the operative field by the adhesive flap. The eye speculum is placed after draping. The microscope is draped with a sterile cover before the case.

The Knife: Keratome (2.2–2.8 mm, pre-formed angled blade) creates a self-sealing primary corneal incision at the limbus. A smaller paracentesis knife creates a side-port entry 90° away.

Retractors: Wire eye speculum holds the eyelids open. Irrigation fluid pressure within the anterior chamber maintains the globe's shape — this is the "retractor" for the anterior segment.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Eye speculum placed; confirm full pupil dilation
  2. OVD injection: viscoelastic injected through the paracentesis into the anterior chamber (protects the corneal endothelium; maintains chamber depth)
  3. Capsulorhexis: cystotome or capsulorhexis forceps tears a smooth, continuous circular opening in the anterior lens capsule (CCC — continuous curvilinear capsulorhexis)
  4. Hydrodissection: BSS injected under the anterior capsule; the lens nucleus rotates freely when complete
  5. Phacoemulsification: the phaco handpiece is inserted through the main incision; ultrasonic energy emulsifies the nucleus; simultaneous irrigation and aspiration removes the material
  6. Irrigation/Aspiration (I/A): a separate I/A handpiece removes remaining soft cortical material from within the capsular bag
  7. OVD re-injection: viscoelastic fills the capsular bag to support IOL deployment
  8. IOL implantation: foldable IOL loaded in the injector cartridge; inserted through the main incision; deployed and unfolded within the capsular bag; haptics positioned
  9. OVD removal: I/A handpiece removes all remaining viscoelastic
  10. Stromal hydration: BSS injected into the corneal stroma at the incision to create a watertight self-sealing closure

⚠ WATCH FOR - IOL contamination: if the IOL is touched by a glove, instrument, or falls off the field, it cannot be used; open a new one; do NOT attempt to place a contaminated lens - Phaco surge: if the phaco tip becomes occluded and suddenly releases, there is a surge of aspiration that can collapse the anterior chamber; BSS-filled syringe must be immediately accessible to restore the chamber - Posterior capsule rupture: if the posterior capsule tears during phacoemulsification, the case changes immediately — the surgeon may need different instruments (vitrectomy handpiece, different OVD); watch for the call and be ready - OVD not fully removed: retained viscoelastic causes a post-op IOP spike; the surgeon will confirm its removal — do not rush the I/A step - Wrong solution on the field: confirm every solution on the back table is labeled; solutions that look identical (BSS, viscoelastic, Miochol) have completely different effects if instilled in the wrong sequence

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
No sutures required — the keratome incision is self-sealing by design. If a wound leak is detected (Seidel test with fluorescein), a single 10-0 nylon suture closes the wound.

Subconjunctival injection: Antibiotic + steroid at the end of the case.

Dressing: Antibiotic + steroid drops; rigid plastic eye shield taped over the orbit (shield only — not a pressure patch).

docs.title: Pars Plana Vitrectomy (PPV)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: I (Clean) · Position: Supine, head in foam donut · Avg time: 60–180 min depending on pathology · Team: surgeon + 1 scrub · Microscope: required · Implants: none; tamponade agent (gas or silicone oil) at the end

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Vitrectomy machine: confirm gauge (23 vs. 25 gauge); tubing set primed; cutter running at the correct cut rate; infusion BSS primed; footpedal configured - [ ] Cannulas (3-port system): infusion, endoilluminator, and working port; matching gauge and length - [ ] Wide-angle viewing system (BIOM or AVI): coupling gel ready; confirm the viewing lens is sterile - [ ] Endolaser probe (532 nm or diode): in the room; tested for function before the case - [ ] Tamponade agent: confirm which agent the surgeon will use (gas type: C₃F₈ or SF₆, or silicone oil); have it ready but do NOT open until called - [ ] Silicone oil injection system (if applicable): confirm the injection set matches the oil viscosity ordered - [ ] Membrane forceps, pick, and any retinal instruments the surgeon requests for specific pathology - [ ] Do NOT use CHG near the eye; betadine only

Position: Supine; head in a foam donut; operating microscope directly overhead.

Prep: 5% betadine drops; betadine periocular skin prep; no CHG.

Draping: Ophthalmic aperture adhesive drape; eye speculum placed.

The Knife: Three MVR blade stab incisions at the pars plana (3.5–4 mm posterior to the limbus, over non-functional retina): - Inferotemporal: infusion cannula (sutured in place) - Superotemporal: endoilluminator (light pipe) - Superonasal or second temporal: working instrument port

Retractors: Eye speculum. The infusion cannula maintains IOP with continuous BSS infusion — this keeps the eye properly inflated throughout the case. The wide-angle viewing system provides panoramic visualization of the fundus.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Place three cannulas; suture the infusion cannula; confirm its tip is visible in the vitreous cavity before starting infusion
  2. Insert the vitrector; core vitrectomy to debulk the vitreous
  3. Address the pathology: peel epiretinal membranes, trim traction, drain subretinal fluid, treat retinal breaks with endolaser
  4. Endolaser: laser probe inserted through the working port; photocoagulation applied around retinal tears
  5. Fluid-air exchange (FAX): infusion switched to air; retina flattened
  6. Tamponade placement: air left in place, or gas (C₃F₈, SF₆) or silicone oil injected to maintain retinal tamponade

⚠ WATCH FOR - Infusion cannula position: if the infusion cannula tip is in the retina rather than the vitreous cavity, infusion flow will strip the retina; surgeon must confirm the tip position with direct visualization before starting infusion - Gas type and concentration: if the wrong gas or concentration is used for tamponade, the bubble volume is wrong; confirm the gas type and mixing ratio with the surgeon before injection - Silicone oil emulsification: if the wrong viscosity of silicone oil is used, it can emulsify and cause cataract or glaucoma post-op; confirm the viscosity specification before opening - Endolaser over normal retina: the laser must be applied only to the target area; extra passes risk permanent scotomas; pass the probe cautiously and confirm the surgeon sees the target clearly before activating

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
23/25-gauge incisions are self-sealing — no sutures. 20-gauge incisions require 7-0 Vicryl closure of each sclerotomy. Conjunctiva closed with 7-0 Vicryl at each cannula site, or self-sealing.

Subconjunctival injection: Antibiotic + steroid.

Dressing: Rigid eye shield taped over the orbit.

Post-op positioning: If gas tamponade placed, patient must maintain face-down positioning for 1–2 weeks. Alert the OR team and post-op nursing before the patient leaves the room.

docs.title: Strabismus Correction (Extraocular Muscle Surgery)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: I (Clean) · Position: Supine, head neutral · Avg time: 30–60 min · Team: surgeon + 1 · Microscope or loupes · Implants: none

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Muscle hooks (Jameson, Green, or squint hooks): multiple sizes; at least 3 on the Mayo stand — two to isolate the muscle, one for backup - [ ] 6-0 Vicryl loaded on a spatula needle × 2 for the muscle sutures - [ ] Westcott scissors (spring scissors) for the conjunctival peritomy and dissection - [ ] 0.12 mm Colibri forceps for conjunctival grasping - [ ] Calipers: for measuring the recession or resection distance on the sclera - [ ] Muscle clamp (e.g., Von Graefe): for resection cases - [ ] Oculocardiac reflex warning: alert anesthesia when the muscle hook goes under the muscle and any time traction is applied — bradycardia is common and expected

Position: Supine; head neutral; microscope or surgical loupes used.

Prep: 5% betadine drops; betadine periocular skin prep.

Draping: Ophthalmic aperture adhesive drape; eye speculum placed.

The Knife: Fine Westcott scissors or #15 blade makes a conjunctival peritomy (circumferential incision through the conjunctiva at the limbus) to expose the sclera and muscle insertions.

Retractors: Eye speculum holds the lids. Muscle hooks (Jameson, Green) are the primary retractors — hooked instruments slid under the targeted extraocular muscle to isolate it. Fine Colibri forceps grasp the conjunctiva during the peritomy.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Conjunctival peritomy; expose the targeted muscle's scleral insertion
  2. Pass the muscle hook under the muscle; place a second hook to ensure the full width is engaged
  3. Dissect the intermuscular membrane to free the muscle
  4. Place locking sutures (6-0 Vicryl double-arm) through the muscle belly
  5. Recession (weakening): cut the muscle at its scleral insertion; reattach it more posteriorly using calipers to measure the recession distance; 6-0 Vicryl suture
  6. Resection (strengthening): clamp the muscle posteriorly; excise the clamped segment; reattach at the original insertion; shorter muscle = stronger

⚠ WATCH FOR - Oculocardiac reflex: traction on any extraocular muscle triggers vagally-mediated bradycardia; this is not an emergency but it must be anticipated — warn anesthesia each time the hook is placed and each time traction is applied; the reflex resolves when traction is released - Scleral suture depth: if a suture is passed too deeply through the sclera, it can penetrate the eye and cause retinal hemorrhage; pass the spatula needle parallel to the scleral surface, not perpendicular - Muscle slip: if the muscle hook slips off the muscle during dissection, the muscle retracts into the orbit — a surgical emergency; have a second hook immediately available at all times - Caliper measurement: recession and resection amounts are calculated pre-op and are exact; confirm the measurement with the surgeon before tying the suture

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Layer Suture Notes Conjunctiva 6-0 plain gut or 7-0 Vicryl Interrupted or running at the peritomy site; or self-healing if opening is small

Dressing: Antibiotic eye ointment; eye pad for 4–6 hours or eye drops without patching (surgeon preference).

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"In ophthalmology, the Approach is measured in millimeters and the Procedure is measured in microns. The keratome and the speculum must be perfect before the eye is ever touched."

  • Never touch an IOL directly. Use only the injector cartridge. If the IOL contacts a glove, an instrument, or the floor, it goes in the trash — not in the eye.
  • Label every solution on the back table. BSS, viscoelastic, Miochol, and Miostat all look nearly identical. Instilling the wrong one into the anterior chamber changes the case immediately.
  • In strabismus, warn anesthesia every time you apply traction to a muscle. The oculocardiac reflex is predictable and manageable — but only if anesthesia expects it.
  • In vitrectomy, confirm the infusion cannula tip is visible in the vitreous cavity before turning on the infusion. This is not optional. Infusing into the retina strips it off the choroid.

Next: Chapter 21 — Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery

docs.title: The Reconstructive Ladder
docs.text:
Every wound management decision in plastic surgery starts at the simplest effective option and escalates as needed:

  1. Primary closure → 2. Secondary intention → 3. Delayed primary → 4. Skin graft → 5. Local flap → 6. Regional flap → 7. Free flap

The rung selected determines the Approach. A skin graft case is a completely different setup from a free flap case. Know which rung you're on before you scrub.

Why it matters at the back table: Plastic surgery lives or dies on technique precision and sterility. A contaminated graft bed, a pedicle that gets crushed by a heavy retractor, or an implant that touched skin — these failures begin before the Procedure, in the Approach. Your setup is part of the outcome.

docs.title: Split-Thickness Skin Grafting (STSG)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: I–II depending on recipient wound · Position: Varies with wound location · Avg time: 30–90 min · Team: surgeon + 1 assistant · Two sites: donor site + recipient site

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Dermatome: confirm type (Zimmer electric or equivalent); correct blade guard depth set per surgeon's specification (0.010"–0.030"); mineral oil for the donor site on the field - [ ] Mesh carrier (if meshing): confirm expansion ratio requested (1:1.5, 1:2, 1:3); assembled and ready before graft is harvested - [ ] Damp saline gauze on the back table: the harvested graft goes here dermis-down immediately; keep it moist at all times - [ ] Recipient bed preparation supplies: curette, electrocautery, and scissors for debridement - [ ] Graft fixation: staple gun loaded; or 4-0 chromic/plain gut on a small needle for edge suturing - [ ] Bolster or NPWT dressing: confirm which type the surgeon wants; have it prepared before the graft goes down - [ ] Donor site dressing: Adaptic or Xeroform + absorptive gauze + elastic bandage - [ ] Change outer gloves between harvesting from the donor site and handling the graft at the recipient site

Position: Determined by the wound location. Supine for lower extremity, trunk, face. Lateral or prone for back or posterior wounds.

Prep: CHG/alcohol; prep the recipient wound and the donor site. These may be the same field (thigh wound + thigh donor) or separate fields.

Draping: Recipient wound and donor site within the same drape field if possible. Donor site most commonly the anterolateral thigh.

The Knife (Donor Site): The dermatome is the cutting tool — it shaves a thin layer of skin at a preset depth. Set the blade guard before the case starts per the surgeon's specification. The thigh is stretched taut and lubricated with mineral oil before the dermatome is applied.

Retractors (Donor Site): No conventional retractors. The assistant and scrub tech use flat boards or their palms to stretch the skin taut on both sides of the advancing dermatome. A tongue depressor or skin grafting board pressed against the leading edge of the dermatome creates a flat, even surface for a uniform graft.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Mineral oil applied to the donor site; skin stretched taut
  2. Dermatome applied firmly; powered on; harvested graft rolls onto the guard
  3. Graft removed; placed dermis-down on a damp saline gauze
  4. Meshing (if indicated): graft threaded through the mesh carrier; perforated pattern allows expansion, drainage, and conformation to irregular surfaces
  5. Recipient wound bed prepared: excise non-viable tissue; ensure vascularity; control hemostasis completely
  6. Apply graft dermis-down onto the recipient bed; trim to fit; secure with staples, suture, or combination
  7. Complete immobile contact between graft and bed is required — hematoma, seroma, or shear movement = graft failure

⚠ WATCH FOR - Graft desiccation: if the harvested graft dries out before application, it will not take; keep it on a moist saline gauze and do not let it sit uncovered - Dermatome depth error: if the guard is set too deep, the donor site will not re-epithelialize on its own and will require a graft itself; confirm the depth setting with the surgeon before the first pass - Hematoma under the graft: even a small hematoma between the graft and the bed lifts the graft off the blood supply and kills it; achieve complete hemostasis in the recipient bed before the graft goes down; do not rush this step - Staple misalignment: staples that tent the graft edges upward lift the graft away from the bed at the margins; apply staples flat against the skin surface

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Donor site: Non-adherent dressing (Adaptic, Xeroform, or Mepitel) + absorptive gauze + elastic bandage. The donor site heals by re-epithelialization — it is not sutured.
