Graft (recipient site): Secured edges + bolster dressing (tie-over or NPWT) to maintain constant contact between the graft and the recipient bed.

docs.title: Z-Plasty (Local Flap)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: I (Clean) · Position: Varies with scar location · Avg time: 30–60 min · Team: surgeon only or + 1 · Used for: scar contracture release, scar reorientation

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Surgical marking pen: surgeon will draw the Z design on the skin before incising — confirm the marker is on the field - [ ] #15 blade × 2: fine, precise cuts required; have a fresh blade available - [ ] Metzenbaum scissors (small, curved): for flap elevation in the subcutaneous plane - [ ] Fine skin hooks: for flap tip retraction without crushing - [ ] 5-0 nylon (face) or 4-0 nylon (body) for final skin closure; 4-0 Monocryl for buried subcutaneous sutures

Position: Determined by the scar location. Supine or sitting for facial and neck Z-plasties. Extremity positioning as appropriate.

Prep: CHG/alcohol for extremities; betadine for face.

Draping: Small towel aperture drape for facial Z-plasties; extremity drapes for limb contractures.

The Knife: #15 blade. The Z design is drawn on the skin before incision: central limb along the scar; two diagonal limbs at 60° angles from each end in opposite directions. Three incisions define two triangular flaps.

Retractors: Fine skin hooks or Senn double-ended retractors to elevate and retract the triangular flap tips during dissection.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Incise all three limbs of the Z with the #15 blade
  2. Elevate both triangular flaps in the subcutaneous plane with Metzenbaum scissors
  3. Transpose the flaps: each triangle is rotated 90° and sutured into the opposite limb's position
  4. Result: scar reoriented 90° from its original direction; contracture tension released; total scar length increases

⚠ WATCH FOR - Flap tip ischemia: if the subcutaneous plane is elevated too thin (dermis only), the flap tip loses perfusion; maintain adequate subcutaneous depth under each flap - Geometric distortion: the 60° limb angles are precise; if the incisions are not drawn correctly before cutting, the transposition will not work; confirm the design is marked before handing the blade

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Layer Suture Notes Subcutaneous (dead space) 4-0 Monocryl buried interrupted Reduces skin tension Skin 5-0 nylon or Prolene (face) / 4-0 nylon (body) Interrupted

Dressing: Non-adherent + light gauze; or Steri-strips reinforcing the skin edges.

docs.title: Free Flap (Microvascular)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: I (Clean) · Position: Varies by flap and defect · Avg time: 4–10 hrs · Team: Two simultaneous scrubbed teams (donor site + recipient site) · Microscope: required for anastomosis · Most complex procedure in plastic surgery

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Two complete instrument setups: one for flap harvest (donor team); one for recipient site and anastomosis (recipient team) - [ ] Microvascular instruments: Castroviejo needle drivers × 2; jewelers forceps × 2; micro scissors; vessel dilators; background material (blue or yellow) for anastomosis; vessel loops (Silastic) - [ ] Anastomosis sutures: 9-0 or 10-0 nylon on a BV-1 needle × multiple; keep them moist - [ ] Venous coupler system (if used): confirm the correct sizes; assembler on the field - [ ] Doppler probe: sterile for intraoperative confirmation of flap perfusion after anastomosis - [ ] Operating microscope sterile drape: drape before the anastomosis step - [ ] Flap-specific instruments: for DIEP flap — abdominal retractors, perforator dissection instruments; for fibula — bone saw, plates and screws for jaw reconstruction - [ ] Communication between teams: confirm with both surgeons that the recipient vessels are ready before the pedicle is divided at the donor site

Position: Varies by flap and defect. DIEP flap (abdominal to breast): supine, arms extended. Fibula free flap (jaw reconstruction): lateral, leg extended. Two simultaneous surgical fields.

Prep: Both donor and recipient sites prepped simultaneously by two teams.

Draping: Two separate sterile fields, or one large combined field encompassing both sites.

The Knife: #15 or #10 blade for recipient site incisions and vessel exposure. #15 blade for flap harvest at the donor site.

Retractors: Recipient vessels: fine vein retractors; Silastic vessel loops around the recipient artery and vein. Operating microscope replaces conventional retractors during the anastomosis — vessel loops hold the vessels in position; the microscope provides the access.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Donor site: flap harvested with its pedicle (artery + vein dissected to maximum length); flap is NOT divided until recipient vessels are confirmed ready
  2. Recipient site: defect prepared; recipient vessels identified, dissected free, and controlled with vessel loops
  3. Pedicle division: when both teams are ready, the flap pedicle is divided; the flap is transferred to the recipient site; ischemia time begins
  4. Microvascular anastomosis:
  5. Arterial anastomosis first: 9-0 or 10-0 nylon interrupted; 6–10 sutures per anastomosis; under ×10–×25 magnification; Castroviejo needle drivers; jewelers forceps
  6. Venous anastomosis: same technique; or venous coupler for faster, reliable vein-to-vein anastomosis
  7. Vessel clamps removed; flow confirmed by Doppler; capillary refill and flap bleeding checked

⚠ WATCH FOR - Ischemia time: from pedicle division to anastomosis completion, every minute counts; the microvascular instruments must be immediately accessible; no searching during the anastomosis - 9-0/10-0 suture management: at this scale, sutures are nearly invisible; keep them moist; never let them dry, kink, or tangle; keep the microscope field clean - Anastomosis tension: if the pedicle is too short and the vessels are under tension, the anastomosis will fail; alert the surgeon before dividing the pedicle if the distance looks marginal - Compression on the flap: after the anastomosis, no compressive dressing or retractor pressure on the flap; compression occludes the anastomosis and the flap dies; communicate this to the circulator when they are applying the dressing

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Donor site: Primary closure (layered closure for DIEP; fibula leg closed in layers) or skin graft if primary closure is not possible.

Recipient site: Flap sutured to wound edges; drains placed under the flap; layered closure of remaining tissue planes.

Dressing: Non-compressive over the free flap. Window dressing (gauze with a clear window over the flap surface) allows regular monitoring of flap perfusion post-op.

docs.title: Mastectomy with Immediate Implant Reconstruction
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: I (Clean) · Position: Supine, operative arm on arm board · Avg time: 2–4 hrs · Team: surgeon + 1–2 assistants · Implants: tissue expander or direct-to-implant (DTI) with ADM · No-touch technique required

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Implant or tissue expander: confirm type and size with the surgeon before opening; keep it sealed until the pocket is ready - [ ] Triple antibiotic solution (gentamicin + bacitracin + polymyxin B in saline): prepared by the circulator; used to irrigate the implant pocket before implant placement - [ ] Change outer gloves before handling the implant — no exceptions; the no-touch technique prevents biofilm seeding - [ ] ADM (AlloDerm or equivalent): confirm size ordered; keep it moist in saline per manufacturer instructions until it is needed - [ ] JP drains × 2: one for the mastectomy flap space; one for the axilla; have 2-0 nylon for securing - [ ] Lighted retractor or fiberoptic headlight: essential for skin flap elevation; the thin flap plane is difficult to see with standard retractors alone

Position: Supine; operative arm extended on an arm board.

Prep: CHG/alcohol; entire chest from chin to umbilicus, including the axilla.

Draping: Four towels framing the chest → fenestrated mastectomy drape. Arm stockinette if the arm will be draped free; secured if not.

The Knife: #10 blade. Skin-sparing mastectomy: elliptical incision around the nipple-areola complex only; skin envelope preserved. Simple mastectomy: larger elliptical incision incorporating the entire nipple-areola complex.

Retractors: Army-Navy retractors initially. Skin flap elevation: Senn retractors, sharp Hohmann, or a lighted retractor to illuminate the subcutaneous plane. Axillary dissection (if performed): Richardson retractors and vein retractors to protect the axillary vein.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Elliptical skin incision; elevate skin flaps in the subcutaneous plane (thin flaps preserve the skin envelope)
  2. Divide the breast at the pectoralis major fascia; free the breast from the muscle; remove the specimen
  3. Sentinel lymph node biopsy (if indicated): blue dye and/or gamma probe-guided identification; remove and send for frozen section
  4. Axillary node dissection (if indicated): remove Level I/II axillary nodes
  5. Reconstruction:
  6. Tissue expander: deflated silicone shell placed under pectoralis major and serratus anterior; filled with saline intraoperatively; spacer until permanent exchange
  7. DTI (direct-to-implant): permanent silicone gel implant placed; ADM sewn to the inferior pectoralis border to form the lower pole pocket
  8. Irrigate the pocket with triple antibiotic solution; change outer gloves; introduce the implant with minimal handling

⚠ WATCH FOR - Skin flap thinness: if the flap elevation is too thin, the overlying skin loses its blood supply and will necrose over the implant — a catastrophic wound healing failure; the surgeon controls flap thickness, but do not create tension on the flap with retractors - Implant contact with skin: if the implant touches the skin before being placed in the irrigated pocket, change gloves and re-irrigate; biofilm seeding increases capsular contracture rates dramatically - ADM handling: ADM must be kept moist; dried ADM becomes stiff and is difficult to suture; keep it in saline until the moment it is needed - Drain count: two JP drains are standard; document their placement sites and confirm the count before closing

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Layer Suture Notes Pectoralis / ADM to chest wall 2-0 or 3-0 Vicryl Securing the inferior pocket Subcutaneous 3-0 Vicryl Skin 4-0 Monocryl subcuticular + Steri-strips JP drains × 2 2-0 nylon One in the mastectomy space; one in the axilla

Dressing: Gauze 4×4 + ABD pad; surgical bra or binder for compression.

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"In plastic surgery, your Approach must protect the thing that heals. A contaminated graft bed, a crushed flap pedicle, or an implant that touched skin are failures that begin before the Procedure."

  • Skin grafts fail without contact. Achieve complete hemostasis in the recipient bed before the graft goes down. A single hematoma pocket is enough to kill a graft.
  • In free flap cases, change your gloves before handling any anastomosis suture. Contamination at the anastomosis leads to thrombosis and flap loss.
  • The no-touch technique for implants is non-negotiable. Change gloves, irrigate the pocket, and minimize handling time. This is not about ritual — it is about biofilm.
  • In microvascular anastomosis, silence and stillness at the field when the surgeon is suturing. The sutures are nearly invisible. Every distraction costs a suture.

Next: Chapter 22 — Pediatric Surgery

docs.title: What Robotic Surgery Actually Is at the Back Table
docs.text:
The da Vinci surgical system (Intuitive Surgical) is a master-slave telemanipulation platform. The surgeon sits at a console and controls robotic arms in real time using hand and foot controls. The surgeon is not scrubbed. The robotic arms are scrubbed.

Your job as the scrub tech changes fundamentally: - You are not handing instruments to a surgeon standing across the table from you - You are standing at the patient cart (the robot), exchanging instruments on the robotic arms and passing everything through the assistant port - The console surgeon cannot hand anything to themselves — they depend entirely on you for sutures, clips, staplers, irrigation, and retraction through that port - You are the surgeon's hands at the bedside

Understanding this shift is the foundation of working robotic cases.

docs.title: The da Vinci Platform Family
docs.text:
System Arms Notes da Vinci Xi 4 arms Current flagship; overhead boom; rotatable patient cart; most versatile da Vinci X 4 arms Similar to Xi; fixed boom; cost-positioned system da Vinci SP 1 arm (3 instruments + camera through 1 port) Single-port; 25 mm cannula; ideal for urologic and GYN single-incision cases da Vinci Si 4 arms Older generation; still in service at many institutions

Most of what follows applies to the Xi, which is the predominant system in active use.

docs.title: System Architecture: Know Your Station
docs.text:
PATIENT CART (your station)
├── 4 robotic arms docked to trocars
├── Camera arm (Arm 1): holds the 3D endoscope
├── Instrument arms (Arms 2–4): hold EndoWrist instruments
└── Assistant port: your working channel

VISION CART
├── Camera/light source
├── Insufflator
└── Energy generator (for monopolar/bipolar)

SURGEON'S CONSOLE (non-sterile)
└── Surgeon sits here; controls all arm movements remotely

docs.title: EndoWrist Instruments — What They Are and How to Exchange Them
docs.text:
EndoWrist instruments attach to the robotic arm cannulas. They have a instrument life counter — each instrument has a limited number of uses (typically 10). The system tracks this and will not allow an expired instrument to be used.

Common EndoWrist instruments:

Instrument Use Maryland Bipolar Forceps Dissection + bipolar coagulation Monopolar Curved Scissors Sharp dissection + cut-mode cautery ProGrasp Forceps Tissue retraction; strong grasping Cadiere Forceps Gentle tissue grasping Large Needle Driver Suturing; most common needle driver Mega Needle Driver Heavy suturing; fascia closure Fenestrated Bipolar Forceps Atraumatic tissue grasping + bipolar Vessel Sealer Extend Vessel sealing (energy device equivalent) Tip-Up Fenestrated Grasper Bowel/delicate tissue handling da Vinci SureForm Stapler Robotic-controlled endoscopic stapler

Exchanging instruments on the robotic arm: 1. Confirm the console surgeon has released control (foot pedal off the instrument clutch) 2. Press the instrument release button on the arm 3. Pull the instrument straight out along the arm axis — do not twist 4. Seat the new instrument until the locking click is felt 5. Confirm the instrument name appears on the vision cart monitor

docs.title: Robotic Arm Draping
docs.text:
The robotic arms must be draped with dedicated sterile arm drapes before the robot is docked to the patient's ports. This happens after port placement, before docking.

Sequence: 1. The circulator holds the arm drape from the outside; the scrub tech receives the sterile portion 2. The arm drape rolls over the arm from the instrument attachment point toward the base 3. Snap the drape attachment point onto the arm; confirm it is locked 4. Repeat for all four arms 5. The sterile camera is inserted into the camera arm's sterile adapter 6. The robot is then docked — the arms swing over the patient and lock onto the trocars

Critical: The arm drapes must not have any wrinkles or caught edges. A drape breach during docking contaminates the field.

docs.title: The Assistant Port — Your Primary Tool
docs.text:
The assistant port is typically a 10–12 mm trocar placed specifically for the bedside assistant. Everything that cannot be passed through the robotic arms must come through this port.

What you pass through the assistant port: - Sutures (pre-loaded on a laparoscopic needle holder or passed as a suture loop) - Hem-o-lok clips (laparoscopic clip applier) - Irrigation (laparoscopic suction-irrigator) - Additional graspers or retractors - Specimen extraction bags (endobags) - SureForm stapler reloads (if not using robotic stapler) - Laparoscopic energy device (if surgeon switches to a non-robotic energy instrument)

Passing sutures through the assistant port: The most common and technically demanding task. Pre-load the suture on a laparoscopic needle holder with the needle oriented correctly for the surgeon's expected direction of suturing. Pass the needle holder smoothly through the port. The console surgeon grasps the needle with a robotic needle driver and takes control.

docs.title: Procedure-Specific Setups — See the Specialty Chapters
docs.text:
The full Before You Scrub checklists, procedure steps, Watch For callouts, and closure tables for da Vinci cases live in the specialty chapters alongside their conventional counterparts. Use this chapter for platform mechanics and troubleshooting; go to the specialty chapter for the actual case setup.

Procedure Chapter Robotic-Assisted Radical Prostatectomy (RARP) Chapter 18 — Urology Robotic-Assisted Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy (da Vinci TLH) Chapter 17 — OB/GYN Robotic Nissen Fundoplication (adult) Chapter 13 — General Surgery Robotic Nissen Fundoplication (pediatric) Chapter 22 — Pediatric Surgery Robotic VATS Lobectomy Chapter 16 — Cardiovascular & Thoracic

docs.title: Robotic Case Troubleshooting at the Bedside
docs.text: Problem Your Response Instrument won't release from the arm Do NOT force it; press the manual release button on the back of the arm; if still stuck, notify the console surgeon to release control Instrument counter expired mid-case Open a new instrument of the same type; the expired instrument must come off the arm Robotic arm drape tears intraoperatively The contaminated arm must be re-draped; declare the breach, stop the case momentarily, re-drape the affected arm Console surgeon calls "arm collision" Both arms must be manually repositioned to clear the collision; console surgeon will direct which arm to move first Smoke/plume at the port Increase suction through the assistant port; the AirSeal (if in use) handles this automatically Power failure / robot fault The robot locks in place; the console surgeon will direct manual arm repositioning if needed; laparoscopic backup instruments should be immediately available

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"In robotic surgery, the scrub tech at the bedside is the surgeon's hands. The console surgeon has complete visual control and zero physical access. You are the only person who can give them what they need."

  • Pre-load every suture before it's called. A console surgeon mid-dissection cannot stop and wait 90 seconds for you to find and load a 3-0 Monocryl. Know the case; know when the suture step is coming; have it in your hand.
  • Instrument arm crashes are preventable. When the robot is docking, watch for arms that are crossing each other. Alert the circulator before docking begins if a collision path is obvious.
  • Never grab a robotic arm to reposition it without confirming the console surgeon has released control. The arms move with significant torque — an arm under power will injure your hand.
  • Know the difference between the robotic energy instruments (EndoWrist Vessel Sealer, bipolar Maryland) and what can come through the assistant port. The two systems do not share instruments.

Next: Chapter 22B — Robotic Surgery: Orthopedic & Spine

docs.title: Navigation vs. Robotics: Know the Difference
docs.text:
These two terms are often used interchangeably in the OR. They are not the same thing.

Surgical navigation is a real-time GPS system for the OR. It tracks the position of instruments and anatomy in three-dimensional space and displays them on a screen. The surgeon uses navigation to confirm where they are — but their hands do all the cutting.

Surgical robotics uses a powered mechanical arm to physically position, guide, or constrain an instrument. Some systems (Mako, ROSA) use a haptic boundary that physically prevents the surgeon from cutting outside the planned zone. Others (Mazor X, ExcelsiusGPS) use the robotic arm to lock a drill guide at a pre-planned trajectory — then the surgeon drills manually through the guide.

Hybrid systems (ExcelsiusGPS, Stealth Autopilot with O-arm) combine real-time navigation with robotic arm guidance in one platform.

Why it matters at the back table: Navigation cases require patient and instrument tracker arrays to stay mounted and undisturbed throughout the case. Robotic arm cases require registration steps before implants are placed. Understanding which phase you're in determines what you should and should not be doing at any given moment.

docs.title: Procedure-Specific Setups — See the Specialty Chapters
docs.text:
The full Before You Scrub checklists, registration sequences, Watch For callouts, and closure tables for all orthopedic and spine robotic cases live in the specialty chapters alongside their conventional counterparts. Use this chapter for platform concepts, universal rules, and the quick-reference table; go to the specialty chapter for the actual case setup.

Platform Procedure Chapter Mako (Stryker) Robotic TKA Chapter 14 — Orthopedic Surgery Mako (Stryker) Robotic THA Chapter 14 — Orthopedic Surgery ROSA Knee (Zimmer Biomet) Robotic TKA Chapter 14 — Orthopedic Surgery Mazor X (Medtronic) Pedicle screw placement Chapter 15 — Neurosurgery ExcelsiusGPS (Globus Medical) Pedicle screw placement Chapter 15 — Neurosurgery Stealth Navigation + Autopilot (Medtronic) Spine navigation / pedicle screws Chapter 15 — Neurosurgery

docs.title: Navigation and Robotic Cases: Universal Rules
docs.text:
These apply regardless of the specific platform:

Rule Why Never disturb the reference array Moving the array invalidates the registration; every decision made after that is based on wrong data Clean tracker balls frequently Contaminated tracking balls drop off the navigation screen silently; the surgeon may not notice for several steps Step back for every O-arm spin Radiation exposure; also movement during the spin corrupts the new image Do not lean on the robotic arm drape Pressure on the drape can transmit force to the arm and shift its position Know the manual backup If the robot fails, navigation fails, or the O-arm is unavailable, the case may continue with standard fluoroscopy; have C-arm sterile drape and standard instruments ready in every robotic spine case Confirm implant system before opening Mako uses Stryker implants. ROSA uses Zimmer Biomet. Mazor X uses Medtronic. ExcelsiusGPS uses Globus. These systems are NOT cross-compatible. Opening the wrong implant set at any point in the case is an avoidable error.

docs.title: Platform Quick-Reference
docs.text: System Manufacturer Procedure Robot Does Surgeon Does da Vinci Xi/SP Intuitive Surgical Soft tissue (urology, GYN, thoracic, general) Holds and moves instruments All operative decisions; controls arms from console Mako Stryker THA, TKA, UKA Haptic boundary enforcement; guides burr Guides the arm; makes all cuts within the boundary ROSA Knee/Hip Zimmer Biomet TKA, THA Positions cutting jig Makes manual cuts through the robotic-guided jig Mazor X Medtronic Spine pedicle screws Positions drill guide at planned trajectory Drills, taps, and places screws manually ExcelsiusGPS Globus Medical Spine pedicle screws Locks drill guide at planned trajectory Drills, taps, and places screws manually Stealth Navigation Medtronic Spine, cranial, ENT Real-time instrument tracking on screen All cutting; navigation is guidance only Stealth Autopilot Medtronic Spine pedicle screws Positions drill guide (patient-mounted arm) Drills, taps, and places screws manually

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"Robots don't operate. Surgeons operate. The robot gives the surgeon more precision or more dexterity. Your job as the scrub tech doesn't change in principle — anticipate what's needed and have it ready — but the tools change entirely."

  • In every robotic or navigation case, the reference array is the most important thing on the field after the patient. Protect it. Announce its location to everyone at the table. If it moves, say so immediately.
  • Mako uses a burr; ROSA uses a saw. If you pull the standard oscillating saw for a Mako case, you have the wrong instrument. Know the system before you pull your instrumentation.
  • In a da Vinci case, silence during the anastomosis means you're doing your job. The console surgeon cannot stop to ask questions while suturing. Have the next instrument loaded and your hand on the assistant port before they finish the current step.
  • Navigation tracker balls fall off the Stealth screen the moment they get bloody. Make wiping them part of your routine — every time an instrument comes back to the back table.
  • If a robotic case converts to open or laparoscopic, have a complete conventional instrument set available in the room. Conversion happens. The room should be ready for it before the case starts.

Proceed to Part III: The Closure — Chapter 23

docs.title: Why Pediatric Surgery Demands a Different Approach
docs.text:
The ApC framework applies universally. But in pediatric surgery, every element must be recalibrated for a patient whose anatomy is smaller, whose physiology is more fragile, and whose tolerance for error is narrower than an adult's.

Before any pediatric case begins, confirm: - Correct instrument scale (baby instruments, not adult sets) - Correct suture size (3-0 or finer for bowel; 6-0 for vascular structures) - Temperature management is active (warming blanket; warm irrigation; room temp elevated) - Blood volume awareness: ~80 mL/kg; a 4 kg neonate has ~320 mL total blood volume; a 50 mL loss is 15% of their entire volume

Why it matters at the back table: You are not scrubbing a small adult. The same instrument pressure that holds tissue in an adult crushes it in a neonate. The same room temperature that is fine for an adult drops a neonate's core temp within minutes. Every decision is magnified.

docs.title: Pyloric Stenosis — Ramstedt Pyloromyotomy
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: I (Clean) · Position: Supine · Avg time: 20–40 min · Team: surgeon + 1 assistant · Age: typically 2–8 weeks old · Blood loss: minimal; but any loss in a neonate is significant

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Confirm baby-scale instruments: small Dennis Browne or baby Richardson retractors; Fredet-Ramstedt pyloric spreader (or a fine hemostat as an equivalent) - [ ] #15 blade × 2 for the skin incision and the seromuscular incision; a fresh blade for the pyloromyotomy - [ ] Warming blanket activated under the patient before positioning; warm irrigation saline on the back table - [ ] Mucosal integrity test: anesthesiologist will inject air through the NGT at the end; saline in the wound; watch for bubbles - [ ] 3-0 and 4-0 Vicryl for fascial closure; 5-0 Monocryl for skin; Dermabond on the field as an alternative - [ ] Room temperature elevated before the patient arrives — neonates lose heat rapidly through a large surface-area-to-volume ratio

Position: Supine; small shoulder roll; head in neutral.

Prep: CHG/alcohol; mid-chest to mid-thigh, full width. Do not apply cold prep solution directly on the infant's skin — minimize exposure time to reduce heat loss.

Draping: Pediatric laparotomy drape (smaller than the adult equivalent) or small towels + miniaturized aperture sheet. Minimal drape weight — heavy drapes compress small bodies and restrict respiratory movement.

The Knife: #15 blade. Open approach: small right upper quadrant transverse incision (2–3 cm) or circumumbilical incision at the superior edge of the umbilicus. Laparoscopic approach: umbilical 5 mm port + two 3 mm working ports; #11 blade for the 3 mm incisions.

Retractors: Open: tiny Denis Browne retractors or small baby Richardson retractors. The pylorus is a small, olive-shaped thickened mass. Retraction must be gentle — crushing the pylorus causes mucosal injury and post-op obstruction. Use the minimum traction necessary to deliver the pylorus.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Deliver the pylorus into the wound with a Babcock clamp or gentle digital pressure from beneath
  2. Incise the serosa and pyloric muscle longitudinally on the avascular anterior surface with a #15 blade; extend from the antrum to the pyloric vein (marks the duodenopyloric junction — do not cross this)
  3. Spread the incised muscle fibers with the pyloric spreader (or fine hemostat) — circular muscle fibers spring apart; the mucosa should bulge into the myotomy without protruding through it
  4. Mucosal integrity test: anesthesiologist injects air through the NGT; surgeon inspects the wound filled with saline for bubbles (air bubbles = perforation)
  5. If mucosa intact: complete; return pylorus to abdomen

⚠ WATCH FOR - Mucosal perforation: if the spreader tears through the mucosa, the air test will show bubbles; the perforation must be repaired with 4-0 absorbable suture and a Graham patch of omentum if possible; anesthesia must be notified immediately - Myotomy too short: if the incision does not extend far enough toward the duodenum, the stenosis is not fully released; the pyloric vein is the distal landmark — confirm with the surgeon the incision reaches it - Instrument weight on the pylorus: the pylorus in a neonate is tiny; a heavy instrument resting on it will perforate the mucosa without any force applied; keep instruments clear of the structure unless actively using them - Temperature drop during the case: monitor actively; if the baby's temp drops by 2°C, anesthesia may call the pace of the case; have warm irrigation ready

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Layer Suture Notes Fascia / peritoneum 3-0 Vicryl interrupted Subcutaneous 4-0 Vicryl Skin 5-0 Monocryl subcuticular Or Dermabond + Steri-strips

Dressing: Small adhesive bandage only. Bulky dressings in infants peel, shift, and are uncomfortable — keep it minimal.

docs.title: Pediatric Laparoscopic Appendectomy
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: II (Clean-Contaminated) · Position: Supine, slight left tilt after trocar placement · Avg time: 20–40 min · Team: surgeon + 1 · Pneumoperitoneum pressure: 8–10 mmHg (lower than adult)

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Confirm instrument sizing: 3–5 mm instruments for small children; 5 mm ports maximum for most pediatric cases - [ ] Pneumoperitoneum pressure setting: confirm 8–10 mmHg with the surgeon — never use adult pressure settings (14–15 mmHg) in small children; high pressure compromises the diaphragm - [ ] Endoloops × 2–3 (2-0 chromic or Vicryl) for appendiceal base ligation; or a small-gauge endo-GIA if the base is thickened - [ ] Specimen bag: confirm a size appropriate for extraction through a 5 mm umbilical port - [ ] 3-0 Vicryl for 5 mm port fascial closure; 5-0 Monocryl for skin; Dermabond available - [ ] Irrigation warm saline: warm, not cold — temperature matters in every pediatric case

Position: Supine; slight left lateral tilt after trocar placement (gravity displaces the cecum and appendix into view).

Prep: CHG/alcohol; nipple line to mid-thigh, full width. Keep the infant warm throughout.

Draping: Pediatric laparoscopic drape; or small towels + miniaturized lap sheet.

The Knife: #11 or #15 blade. Umbilical 5 mm port (camera). Two 3–5 mm working ports in the RLQ and suprapubic region. Smaller children may be managed with all 3 mm instruments.

Retractors: Pneumoperitoneum at 8–10 mmHg. Laparoscopic grasper retracts the cecum to expose the appendix. No other retractors needed.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
Same technique as adult laparoscopic appendectomy (Chapter 13), scaled to the smaller anatomy. Appendiceal base ligated with endoloops or a small-gauge endo-GIA. Specimen extracted through the umbilical port in a bag.

⚠ WATCH FOR - Pneumoperitoneum pressure: confirm the insufflator setting is pediatric-appropriate before creating the pneumoperitoneum; the pediatric diaphragm is easily splinted; high pressure → respiratory compromise in minutes - Port size creep: if the surgeon asks for a 10 mm port for a child under 15 kg, confirm this is intentional; 10 mm port fascial closure in a child requires 2-0 Vicryl and may require a separate stab incision with a fascial closure device - Specimen extraction bag fit: a bag designed for adults will not pass through a 5 mm port; confirm the bag size before opening it

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Layer Suture Notes 5 mm port site fascia 3-0 Vicryl Skin 5-0 Monocryl subcuticular Or Dermabond

Dressing: Small Tegaderm over each port.

docs.title: Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia (CDH) Repair
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: I (Clean) · Position: Supine, slight contralateral rotation · Avg time: 60–120 min · Team: surgeon + 1 assistant · Age: neonate (may be days old) · Left-sided defect is most common (Bochdalek)

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Neonatal instrument set: small Richardson retractors, small Deaver or malleable ribbon retractors — all baby-scaled - [ ] Suture for diaphragm repair: 2-0 or 3-0 Prolene or Ethibond interrupted for primary repair; confirm if Gore-Tex patch is needed (large defect); have both ready - [ ] Gore-Tex patch: confirm size and thickness if the defect is known to be large; keep it sterile until needed - [ ] Temperature management: warming blanket active; room temperature elevated; warm irrigation; overhead warming lamp if available - [ ] Monitor blood loss volume meticulously: 10 mL of blood in a 3 kg neonate is >4% of their total blood volume

Position: Supine; slightly rotated toward the contralateral side. Operative arm extended. Most CDH repairs are left-sided.

Prep: CHG/alcohol; axilla to mid-thigh on the operative side; include the entire abdomen.

Draping: Four towels framing a subcostal incision → pediatric laparotomy sheet. Infant warming measures throughout.

The Knife: #15 blade. Left subcostal incision (2–4 cm below and parallel to the costal margin); or transverse upper abdominal incision for large defects.

Retractors: Small baby Richardson retractors for the abdominal wall. Small Deaver or malleable ribbon retractor for the liver and stomach. Gentle — neonatal organs are soft and easily contused.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Enter the abdomen; identify herniated contents in the chest (bowel, stomach, spleen, sometimes the left lobe of the liver)
  2. Gently reduce herniated organs back into the abdomen — no traction; neonatal organs tear easily
  3. Inspect the diaphragm defect: size, location, and quality of diaphragmatic tissue at the margins
  4. Primary repair (small-to-moderate defects): horizontal mattress sutures (2-0 or 3-0 Prolene or Ethibond); interrupted; close the defect without tension
  5. Patch repair (large defects): Gore-Tex patch sutured to the diaphragmatic margins
  6. Confirm repair is tension-free; irrigate; check hemostasis

⚠ WATCH FOR - Temperature crash: neonatal CDH cases are long; the baby's core temperature must be checked every 15 minutes; a drop of 2°C is a physiological emergency that increases coagulopathy and cardiac instability; maintain all warming measures throughout - Lung hypoplasia: the ipsilateral lung is underdeveloped and will not fully expand at the time of repair; do not expect normal lung expansion on the operative side; the anesthesia team manages ventilation carefully to avoid high pressures - Organ fragility during reduction: the herniated bowel may be ischemic if it has been in the chest long; handle with extreme gentleness; Babcock clamps or very loose gauze contact only - Tension on the repair: if the diaphragm margins are too far apart for primary closure without tension, a patch is required; do not let the surgeon struggle with a primary repair that will dehisce — have the patch open and on the field

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text: Layer Suture Notes Fascia / musculoaponeurotic 2-0 or 3-0 Vicryl interrupted Subcutaneous 3-0 Vicryl Skin 5-0 Monocryl subcuticular Or skin glue Chest drain (if placed) Small-bore catheter (10–14 Fr) Many surgeons do NOT place a chest tube; surgeon preference

docs.title: PSARP — Imperforate Anus (Posterior Sagittal Anorectoplasty)
docs.text: QUICK FACTS Wound Class: II (Clean-Contaminated) · Position: Prone (jackknife/Kraske) · Avg time: 60–120 min · Team: surgeon + 1 assistant · Nerve stimulator: required for sphincter mapping · Critical anatomy: external sphincter complex must be preserved

docs.title: The Approach
docs.text:
BEFORE YOU SCRUB - [ ] Nerve stimulator (Pena stimulator): confirm it is in the room and functional before positioning the patient; test the response in a visible muscle before the surgical field is draped - [ ] Gelpi retractors: × 2 minimum for holding the posterior sagittal wound open - [ ] #15 blade for the posterior sagittal incision - [ ] 3-0 and 4-0 Vicryl for muscle and sphincter re-approximation - [ ] Betadine irrigation for the bowel/rectal field (contaminated bowel if the fistula is entered) - [ ] 5-0 Monocryl or 5-0 Vicryl for skin closure around the neo-anus - [ ] Bowel bag or towel to isolate the rectum once mobilized (contaminated organ)

Position: Prone; jackknife (Kraske) position; hips elevated as the highest point; table broken. Tape may be placed across the buttocks and secured to the OR table sides to part them and improve midline exposure.

Prep: Betadine; entire perineum, buttocks, and perianal region.

Draping: Prone perineal drape; buttocks exposed within the aperture. Small clear adhesive aperture drape may be placed over the anus.

The Knife: #15 blade. Midline posterior sagittal incision from the lower sacrum through the external sphincter muscle complex to the perineum.

Retractors: Gelpi retractors (self-retaining) hold the posterior sagittal wound open. The nerve stimulator is the critical guide — low-current probe touched to the muscle surfaces; when on the sphincter, the anus visibly contracts; this maps where cuts are made and where they must not go.

docs.title: The Procedure
docs.text:
1. Midline posterior sagittal incision; use the nerve stimulator to map the external sphincter complex before cutting any muscle
  2. Divide midline structures precisely — gluteus muscles, levator ani, puborectalis — always exactly in the midline (Pena technique: each structure split midline to preserve bilateral symmetry)
  3. Identify the rectum (or rectal fistula); dissect free; mobilize sufficient length to reach the perineum without tension
  4. Divide the fistula to the urethra, bladder, or vagina (malformation type determines location)
  5. Anoplasty: place the mobilized rectum in the center of the sphincter muscle complex; fix with interrupted sutures anchoring the rectal wall to the sphincter; create a neo-anus of the correct caliber

⚠ WATCH FOR - Nerve stimulator guidance before every cut: the surgeon must use the stimulator before cutting any muscle in the midline; do not hand the scalpel until the surgeon has confirmed the anatomy; a cut placed outside the midline destroys sphincter function - Fistula entry to the genitourinary tract: if the rectum opens into the urethra or bladder, urine and bowel contents are in the field; isolate the bowel with a towel or bowel bag; maintain strict sterile technique - Length of rectal mobilization: if the rectum is too short to reach the perineum without tension, the anoplasty sutures will tear; alert the surgeon if the bowel appears to be under tension during the anoplasty suture placement - Neo-anus caliber: if the opening is too tight, the sutures are being placed too close to the center; if too loose, the repair will not function; the surgeon will calibrate with a Hegar dilator

docs.title: The Closure
docs.text:
Layer Suture Notes Levator ani / puborectalis / external sphincter 3-0 Vicryl interrupted Precisely re-approximated in the midline Subcutaneous 3-0 or 4-0 Vicryl Skin 5-0 Monocryl subcuticular or 5-0 Vicryl interrupted Around the neo-anus

Dressing: Very light gauze + non-adherent. Any dressing in the perineum must not obstruct the neo-anus.

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"In pediatrics, the Approach is not just surgical — it's thermal, metabolic, and physiological. A cold baby on a poorly warmed table, with adult instruments, sutured with adult suture sizes, will fail in ways that have nothing to do with the surgeon's technique."

  • In pyloromyotomy, the mucosa is the boundary between success and perforation. The spreader applies force against muscle fibers. If you feel resistance increase suddenly and the surgeon stops, that is the mucosa pushing back — not an invitation to apply more force.
  • For PSARP, the nerve stimulator is your Approach guide. Never cut in the midline without confirming sphincter location first. Confirm its function before draping.
  • Temperature in neonatal cases is not a background detail. Check it actively. A 2°C drop in a 3 kg neonate is a physiological emergency.
  • Count blood loss in mL per sponge. A sponge soaked in an adult case is minor. In a neonate, a soaked 4×4 may represent 10% of the patient's blood volume.

Proceed to Part III: The Closure — Chapter 23

docs.title: 23.1 Principles of Wound Closure
docs.text:
The goal of closure is to create the conditions in which the body can heal: - Approximate tissue layers without excessive tension - Eliminate dead space where fluid can collect - Provide enough strength to withstand mechanical forces during healing - Minimize foreign material left in the wound - Achieve cosmetically acceptable skin closure when possible

Closure proceeds in anatomical layers — the same layers that were divided during the approach, restored in reverse order.

docs.title: Peritoneum
docs.text: The peritoneum is not always closed — evidence supporting routine peritoneal closure in laparotomy is mixed. When closed, it is approximated with a continuous absorbable suture (e.g., 3-0 Vicryl). The scrub tech must have irrigation ready — the peritoneal cavity may be irrigated with warm saline before closure.

docs.title: Fascia
docs.text:
The strongest layer — fascial closure bears the mechanical load of the closed abdomen. Dehiscence here results in evisceration (a surgical emergency). - Mass closure (en masse) of the linea alba: continuous or interrupted heavy absorbable suture (0 or #1 PDS or looped PDS) - Suture bites should be at least 1 cm from the fascial edge and 1 cm apart - Running closure should be approximately 4:1 (suture length: wound length ratio)

The scrub tech loads suture correctly, manages the needle, and may load multiple needles in advance for a running fascial closure.

docs.title: Subcutaneous Tissue
docs.text: Closed with absorbable suture (3-0 Vicryl) to eliminate dead space and take tension off the skin. Not closed when the wound is contaminated (Class III/IV) — leaving this layer open allows drainage.

docs.title: Skin
docs.text: Multiple closure options (see below). In contaminated wounds, skin may be left open with wet-to-dry dressings and closed 3–5 days later (delayed primary closure).

docs.title: Interrupted Sutures
docs.text:
Individual sutures; each tied separately.

Simple interrupted: single suture passes through both wound edges; tied; cut. If one suture fails, the others hold.

Mattress sutures: - Vertical mattress (Donati): deep and superficial bites in a U configuration; excellent eversion; used for tension and wound edges with poor blood supply - Horizontal mattress: parallel bites; compresses tissue; used for tension, hemostasis, fascia

Figure-of-eight: two crossing bites tied together; strong; used for fascia

Indications for interrupted skin: Areas subject to tension, areas where suture removal must be staged, contaminated wounds (can remove alternate sutures to allow drainage)

docs.title: Continuous (Running) Sutures
docs.text:
A single suture strand placed in a series; faster than interrupted; distributes tension evenly.

Simple running: continuous simple suture; fast; one failure compromises the entire suture line Running locking (blanket stitch): suture loops back through the previous throw; hemostatic; used for fascial closure, bowel Running mattress: continuous horizontal mattress; skin closure Subcuticular (intradermal): suture placed horizontally within the dermis; cosmetically superior; absorbable or removable via pull-through; no suture marks on skin

docs.title: Subcuticular Closure
docs.text: The preferred skin closure when cosmesis is important: 1. Enter dermis at one end of the wound 2. Advance through the dermis in alternating bites on each side 3. Exit dermis at the far end and tie; or use locking knot 4. Use absorbable suture (Monocryl, Vicryl Rapide) for buried; non-absorbable (Prolene) for pull-through removal

docs.title: Purse-String Suture
docs.text: A circular continuous suture around a circular opening (e.g., stoma site, gastrostomy site, colostomy). Tightened to close the opening.

docs.title: 23.4 Bowel Closure Techniques
docs.text:
In GI surgery, bowel anastomoses require specific suture techniques:

Two-layer hand-sewn anastomosis: - Inner layer (mucosal): full-thickness continuous absorbable suture (Connell or Cushing for inverting); brings mucosa-to-mucosa - Outer layer (seromuscular, Lembert suture): interrupted non-absorbable inverting sutures that bury the inner layer

One-layer anastomosis: - Single layer of interrupted full-thickness sutures (Gambee stitch); preserves luminal diameter; widely used

Stapled anastomosis: - GIA and/or EEA staplers (see Chapter 13); everts tissue at staple line

docs.title: 23.5 Retention Sutures
docs.text:
Retention sutures are heavy, through-and-through sutures placed to reinforce the fascial closure in high-risk abdominal wounds where dehiscence is likely.

Indications: - Morbid obesity - Malnourished or cachectic patients - Multiple prior abdominal surgeries - Immunosuppressed (steroids, chemotherapy) - Intra-abdominal infection - Postoperative ileus/distension expected

Materials: #2–#5 nylon or polypropylene; or heavy braided sutures

Technique: - Placed at 2–3 cm intervals through all layers (skin, subcutaneous, fascia, occasionally peritoneum) - Bolsters (short lengths of rubber tubing, plastic, or gauze rolls) placed under the suture bridges to distribute pressure and prevent suture cutting through the skin

Retention sutures are typically removed 2–3 weeks postoperatively; standard fascial sutures are permanent or absorbable with long retention.

docs.title: 23.6 The Scrub Technologist's Role at Closure
docs.text:
Closure phase is not less demanding than the procedure — it requires the same level of focus.

At closure: - Perform final count (see Chapter 6) before the surgeon begins fascial closure - Have sutures loaded and ready for each layer in sequence - Keep track of needles through the closure — every needle passed must be accounted for - Ensure irrigation is ready if the surgeon irrigates before closing peritoneum - Keep the field clean — sponges removed from the wound before closure, not after

Final check before skin: - Instruments, sponges, sharps counts all reconciled - Drain sutured in place if present - Implants documented - Specimen handled and labeled

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"The closure tells the whole story. A hasty close undoes a perfect procedure."

  • In long running fascial closures, manage the suture tail — don't let it tangle in the wound. Keep it draped cleanly across the field.
  • When subcuticular sutures are placed, keep the suture moist if using absorbable — dry Monocryl becomes stiff and difficult to handle.
  • If a count is off when closure begins, the surgeon must wait. Do not let a surgeon close on an unresolved count discrepancy.

Next: Chapter 24 — Drains, Dressings, and Packing

docs.title: 24.1 Surgical Drains
docs.text: A surgical drain removes blood, serum, bile, lymph, or air from a wound cavity, preventing accumulation that would otherwise create dead space, promote infection, or impair healing.

docs.title: Types of Drains
docs.text:
Closed-suction drains Maintain a vacuum, actively drawing fluid from the wound cavity. | Drain | Description | Common Use | |-------|-------------|-----------| | Jackson-Pratt (JP) | Flat, perforated silicone drain; connected to a compressible bulb reservoir | Breast surgery, soft tissue dissection, general surgery | | Hemovac | Flat, perforated drain; connected to a spring-loaded collapsible cylinder reservoir | Orthopedic (joint arthroplasty), larger wounds |

To activate: empty the reservoir; compress it fully; cap it — vacuum created draws fluid.

Passive drains Rely on gravity and pressure differential rather than active suction. | Drain | Description | Use | |-------|-------------|-----| | Penrose drain | Soft, flat, latex or silicone tube; no suction; fluid wicks along exterior | Abscess cavities, contaminated wounds | | Sump drain | Double-lumen; air enters one lumen while fluid drains through the other | Pancreatic, large abdominal cavities | | Malecot catheter | Multi-flanged mushroom-shaped catheter | Nephrostomy, biliary drainage |

Chest tubes Large-bore (20–40 Fr) tube placed in the pleural space to drain air, blood, or fluid. Connected to a water-seal drainage system (Pleur-evac): - Chamber 1: water seal (one-way valve) - Chamber 2: suction control - Chamber 3: drainage collection Continuously monitor for air leak (bubbling in water seal chamber).

docs.title: Drain Placement
docs.text:
Drains are placed by the surgeon; the scrub tech provides: - Drain of appropriate type and size - Clamp to pull drain through the skin exit site (counter-incision) - Suture for securing the drain to the skin (typically 2-0 nylon, interrupted, around the drain) - Drain collected in a sterile manner until the circulator can connect to the reservoir

Securing the drain: A single suture through the skin around the drain, tied snugly but not occlusive. The drain must not kink. Mark the drain exit site with a small amount of benzoin and adhesive dressing.

docs.title: 24.2 Wound Dressings
docs.text: A wound dressing: - Protects the wound from exogenous contamination - Absorbs wound drainage - Maintains an appropriate moisture environment - Provides mechanical protection

docs.title: Basic Dressing Layers (Three-Layer Principle)
docs.text:
1. Primary layer (contact layer): directly contacts the wound surface
  2. Secondary layer (absorptive layer): absorbs drainage
  3. Tertiary layer (outer layer): holds the dressing in place; adds mechanical protection

docs.title: Dressing Types
docs.text:
Gauze dressings Most basic and versatile. - Dry sterile gauze: placed over closed wounds with minimal drainage; clean/clean-contaminated - Wet-to-dry: saline-moistened gauze packed into open wounds; allows debridement as the dressing dries and is removed; standard for contaminated open wounds

Non-adherent dressings - Telfa (non-adherent pad): contact layer for wounds where sticking would be traumatic (donor sites, skin grafts) - Adaptic / Xeroform: fine-mesh gauze impregnated with petrolatum or bismuth tribromophenate; non-adherent; used for donor sites, burns, skin grafts

Film dressings - Tegaderm / OpSite: transparent, semipermeable adhesive film; waterproof; allows wound visualization without removal - Used for superficial wounds, IV sites, covering closed surgical incisions

Foam dressings Absorptive; used for moderately to heavily draining wounds.

Hydrocolloid dressings - DuoDERM: absorbs drainage; creates gel over wound; promotes moist wound healing; not for infected wounds

Alginate dressings Derived from seaweed; highly absorptive (absorbs up to 20× their weight); used for heavily draining wounds.

Antimicrobial dressings - Silver-impregnated dressings (Mepilex Ag, Aquacel Ag): broad-spectrum antimicrobial; used for burns, chronically infected wounds

docs.title: Applying Dressings in the OR
docs.text:
The scrub tech applies the initial wound dressing — typically just before the patient is transferred off the OR table. 1. Confirm final count is complete 2. Wipe any skin prep residue from around the wound edges 3. Apply primary layer (non-adherent if needed) 4. Apply absorptive gauze layer 5. Secure with silk tape, paper tape, or foam tape

For abdominal wounds: 4×4 gauze + ABD (abdominal) pad + tape. For facial wounds: Telfa or non-adherent + gentle securing. For orthopedic: bulky wrap or splint per surgeon direction.

docs.title: 24.3 Wound Packing
docs.text:
Open contaminated wounds may be packed rather than closed primarily.

Purpose: Allow drainage to escape; prevent closure of infected or contaminated space before the wound can heal; promote granulation tissue formation.

Materials: - Moist gauze (saline-moistened) or iodoform gauze (antiseptic-impregnated) - Packed loosely — tight packing impedes healing and can cause pressure necrosis - Changed daily or every 2–3 days per wound care orders

Packing an open wound in the OR: 1. Irrigate cavity copiously with warm saline 2. Loosely fill with moist gauze using ring forceps; do not pack tightly 3. Count packing material used (or document that the cavity was packed without formal count) 4. Cover with outer absorptive dressing

docs.title: 24.4 Negative Pressure Wound Therapy (NPWT / Wound VAC)
docs.text:
NPWT uses continuous or intermittent subatmospheric (negative) pressure applied to a wound to: - Remove wound exudate - Reduce wound edema - Promote granulation tissue - Mechanically contract the wound edges - Improve blood flow to the wound

Indications: Open wounds, fasciotomies, split-thickness skin graft bolstering, sternal wounds, diabetic foot ulcers, complex traumatic wounds

System (e.g., KCI ACTIVA, 3M PREVENA): 1. Foam (reticulated polyurethane or gauze-type): placed in the wound to fill the space 2. Occlusive drape: sealed over the foam and surrounding skin 3. Suction tubing: exits through a small hole in the drape; connected to the NPWT device 4. Device: applies vacuum at a set pressure (typically –125 mmHg continuous)

On the sterile field: The scrub tech provides the VAC foam, trimmed to fit the wound; the occlusive adhesive drape; and the tubing. The device itself is non-sterile and managed by the circulator.

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"The dressing protects everything that was accomplished in the OR. Apply it with the same care you brought to the case."

  • When applying dressings over a drain site, ensure the drain is clearly visible through or at the edge of the dressing — nursing staff need to assess and empty it.
  • Wound VAC foam must fill the wound completely — if it doesn't, it creates dead space. Cut and piece foam to fill all recesses.
  • If a drain is accidentally removed during dressing application, do not attempt to reinsert it — notify the surgeon.

Next: Chapter 25 — Specimen Handling and Documentation

docs.title: Tissue Specimens
docs.text:
The most common type. Tissue removed during surgery is submitted for histopathological examination to: - Confirm or establish a diagnosis (e.g., cancer, infection) - Verify surgical margins (is all the tumor removed?) - Guide further treatment - Establish staging

Tissue integrity depends on timely and appropriate handling. Autolysis (self-digestion by cellular enzymes) begins immediately after removal. Fixation stops this process.

docs.title: Fluid Specimens
docs.text:
• Peritoneal fluid, pleural fluid, joint fluid: sent for cell count, glucose, protein, LDH, culture
  • Bile, abscess contents: sent for culture and sensitivity
  • Lavage fluid: from peritoneal washings in suspected cancer; cytology

docs.title: Microbiology (Culture) Specimens
docs.text:
• Tissue from infected sites
  • Swabs from wounds
  • Fluid from joints, abscesses, pleural/peritoneal spaces
  • Do not fix culture specimens — fixatives kill organisms; culture requires viable tissue
  • Transport in culture media (aerobic + anaerobic) or in a sterile container

docs.title: Foreign Bodies
docs.text: Retained hardware, bullets, needles, or other foreign material may be removed and submitted. Chain-of-custody requirements are especially important for medico-legal specimens (bullets, fragments from traumatic injuries). Some may require law enforcement involvement.

docs.title: Implant-Associated Specimens
docs.text: Removed joint prostheses, explanted devices, infected hardware.

docs.title: 25.2 Specimen Labeling and Chain of Custody
docs.text: Every specimen must be accurately identified from the moment of removal to the moment it is received in the laboratory.

docs.title: Minimum Labeling Requirements
docs.text:
• Patient name (first and last)
  • Patient date of birth or MRN
  • Date of collection
  • Specimen type (what tissue/fluid it is)
  • Source/anatomical location (right breast, left lobe thyroid, appendix, etc.)
  • Surgeon name
  • Special instructions (e.g., "oriented specimen — clip at superior pole," "fresh for frozen section")

docs.title: Labeling Process
docs.text:
1. The surgeon removes the specimen; passes it to the scrub technologist
  2. The scrub tech accepts the specimen without touching the gloved/scrubbed hands — the specimen is placed in a basin or passed directly
  3. The scrub tech immediately passes the specimen to the circulator, verbally identifying it: "Right axillary sentinel lymph node, left and right clip for orientation"
  4. The circulator places it in a correctly labeled container with appropriate fixative or transport medium
  5. The circulator documents in the operative record and on the pathology requisition

docs.title: Orientation Markers
docs.text: For specimens that have a specific orientation relevant to margin assessment: - Marking sutures (e.g., "short suture = superior, long suture = lateral") - Metal clips placed by the scrub tech per surgeon instruction - Ink (applied by pathology at the bench) These allow pathology to assess which margins are positive and where in the specimen the margins occur.

docs.title: Chain of Custody
docs.text:
• Specimens must not be left unattended between removal and laboratory receipt
  • Transfer must be documented: who transferred it, to whom, at what time
  • Medico-legal specimens (bullets, foreign bodies in trauma) require especially rigorous custody documentation, including law enforcement chain of custody forms

docs.title: 10% Neutral Buffered Formalin (NBF)
docs.text:
• Standard fixative for routine surgical pathology
  • Volume: minimum 10:1 fixative-to-tissue ratio
  • Allow fixation time proportional to specimen thickness (at least 24 hours for most specimens before processing)
  • Do not fix specimens for culture or cytology — formalin kills organisms and cells

docs.title: Saline
docs.text:
• Transport medium for specimens to be reviewed fresh (before fixation)
  • Used for frozen section specimens (they must arrive unfixed to be cut on the cryostat)
  • Some surgeons pass small fresh specimens in saline for immediate inspection, then transfer to formalin

docs.title: Dry (No Fixative)
docs.text:
• Calculi (kidney stones): sent dry for stone analysis (mineralogy)
  • Some hardware/foreign bodies

docs.title: Special Media
docs.text:
• Culture media (thioglycollate broth, aerobic/anaerobic swab containers): for microbiological culture
  • RPMI / M-199 medium: for flow cytometry (lymphoma workup), chromosomal studies
  • Glutaraldehyde: for electron microscopy

docs.title: 25.4 Frozen Section
docs.text:
A frozen section is an intraoperative pathological consultation in which: 1. Fresh (unfixed) tissue is sent directly to pathology 2. The tissue is embedded in OCT (optimal cutting temperature) compound, frozen on a cryostat 3. Sections are cut and stained (H&E or similar) 4. A pathologist evaluates the section under the microscope 5. A verbal result is communicated back to the OR — typically within 15–20 minutes

When frozen sections are used: - Determine if margins are clear before closing (oncologic surgery) - Confirm tissue identity (e.g., parathyroid gland vs. lymph node vs. fat) - Assess sentinel lymph nodes for metastasis

Limitations of frozen section: - Accuracy less than permanent section (artifacts of freezing; tissue distortion) - Some tissues freeze poorly (fatty tissue, calcified tissue) - Pathologist may defer difficult cases to permanent sections ("defer to perm")

Scrub tech responsibilities: - Identify frozen section specimen to the circulator verbally and clearly - Keep fresh — do not allow tissue to dry; place on a moist telfa or sponge if not immediately transferred - Do not place in formalin — this destroys the ability to freeze the tissue - Document the time the specimen was sent and the time the result was received

docs.title: 25.5 Implant Documentation and Tracking
docs.text:
All implantable devices placed in patients must be documented per regulatory requirements (Safe Medical Devices Act of 1990; FDA Medical Device Tracking requirements).

Required documentation for each implant: - Device name and description - Manufacturer - Catalog number - Lot/serial number - Size - Expiration date (if applicable) - Date implanted - Patient name and MRN

This information is entered into the operative record, the implant log, and may be sent to a centralized device registry.

Why it matters: If a device is recalled, the manufacturer and facility must be able to identify every patient in whom that device was implanted. Missing documentation means patients cannot be notified.

The scrub tech's role: - Retain all implant packaging until the case is closed and documentation is complete - Read the lot number and size from each opened package to the circulator for documentation - Do not discard packaging until the circulator confirms documentation

docs.title: 25.6 Surgical Counts Documentation
docs.text:
Every count result — initial, closing, and final — is documented in the operative record by the circulating nurse. The scrub tech confirms accuracy.

Documentation includes: - Count correct/incorrect - What was counted (sponges, needles, instruments) - Count performed by (scrub tech name, circulator name) - Any discrepancy and the action taken - Disposition if discrepancy was not resolved (X-ray obtained, surgeon notified, etc.)

docs.title: Clinical Pearls
docs.text:
"Every specimen that leaves your field carries a patient's diagnosis with it. Handle it accordingly."

  • Never pass a specimen to the circulator without verbally confirming its identity. A mislabeled specimen can result in an incorrect cancer diagnosis — a harm that may be irreversible.
  • If you are unsure which fixative to use, ask before the case. Some specimens need to be in culture media within minutes; formalin cannot be undone.
  • Keep implant stickers/packaging until you hear "confirmed" from the circulator.

Next: Chapter 26 — Postoperative Care Transitions

docs.title: 26.1 End-of-Case Responsibilities
docs.text: The case is not over when the last suture is placed. The scrub technologist has a defined set of responsibilities that complete the procedure and prepare for safe patient transfer.

docs.title: Before Patient Leaves the Table
docs.text:
1. Final count confirmed: all instruments, sponges, and sharps reconciled — documented by circulator
  2. Wound dressed: dressing applied before transfer (minimizes contamination during transfer)
  3. Drains secured: all drains sutured in place; not kinked; labeled
  4. Specimens passed: all specimens in labeled containers with appropriate fixative; documentation complete
  5. Implant documentation: all implant labels collected; documentation confirmed by circulator
  6. Electrosurgery tip holstered and accounted for: pencil secured and counted
  7. Sharps secured: all needles in needle box or magnetic needle pad; counted

docs.title: Breaking Down the Sterile Field
docs.text: After the patient leaves the OR: 1. Do not break the sterile field until the patient is out of the room — contamination can still affect the patient if they return, and premature breakdown creates confusion about counts 2. Dispose of sharps in a sharps container (never in regular trash) 3. Dispose of sponges and used supplies in the appropriate waste containers 4. Collect instruments for transport back to sterile processing 5. All instruments should remain moist — use a pre-soak spray or wet towel; do not allow blood to dry onto instruments (makes decontamination more difficult and damages instruments)

docs.title: 26.2 Patient Transfer from the OR Table
docs.text: Patient transfer is a high-risk moment — falls, line/tube dislodgement, and position-related injury can occur.

docs.title: Transfer Principles
docs.text:
• Minimum four people for transfer of an adult patient from OR table to transport stretcher
  • Anesthesia provider maintains the airway and directs the transfer at the head
  • Three personnel along the patient's side, positioned at shoulders, hips, and legs
  • Use a slide board or roller to minimize lifting
  • All IV lines, monitoring leads, urinary catheter, and drains are managed during the transfer (assign one person to manage lines)
  • Move the patient on the count of "1–2–3" — unified, coordinated movement
  • Lock wheels on both the OR table and the stretcher before transfer

docs.title: After Transfer
docs.text:
• Reconnect monitoring on the transport stretcher
  • Ensure the patient is covered and warm — transport is cold
  • Confirm the patient's airway is secured if still intubated
  • Confirm urinary catheter drains freely; no kinks
  • Confirm IV lines are intact

docs.title: 26.3 Report and Handoff to PACU
docs.text:
The SBAR framework (Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation) is the standard structure for patient handoffs:

Component Content Situation Patient name, age, procedure performed, surgeon, anesthesia provider Background Relevant medical history, allergies, blood type, antibiotic given Assessment Estimated blood loss (EBL), fluid received, urine output, any intraoperative events or complications Recommendation Anticipated needs: pain management, drains and what to expect from them, wound dressing, postop orders, any concerns

The scrub technologist is typically not the primary person giving handoff report — this is the circulating nurse and anesthesia provider. However, the scrub tech may be asked about intraoperative events, count results, or specimen status.

docs.title: 26.4 Common Postoperative Complications
docs.text: While the scrub technologist's primary role ends when the patient leaves the OR, awareness of postoperative complications is part of comprehensive surgical education.

docs.title: Hemorrhage
docs.text:
• Primary hemorrhage: at the time of surgery (inadequate hemostasis)
  • Reactive hemorrhage: within 24 hours as vasospasm resolves; blood pressure normalizes after anesthesia
  • Secondary hemorrhage: 7–14 days postoperatively; slipping of a ligature; infection eroding a vessel

Signs: tachycardia, hypotension, decreased urine output, drain output >150 mL/hr, expanding hematoma

docs.title: Surgical Site Infection (SSI)
docs.text: Signs: fever (usually >48 hours postop), erythema, warmth, swelling, purulent drainage from wound Treatment: wound opening and drainage, antibiotics, wound care

docs.title: Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) and Pulmonary Embolism (PE)
docs.text:
• DVT: clot in deep veins (usually lower extremity); presents with calf pain, swelling, erythema
  • PE: clot travels to pulmonary vasculature; presents with sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hemoptysis, cardiovascular collapse
  • Prevention: sequential compression devices (SCDs), early ambulation, pharmacologic prophylaxis

docs.title: Pulmonary Complications
docs.text:
• Atelectasis: partial lung collapse; most common postop complication; prevented by incentive spirometry
  • Pneumonia: especially in patients with prolonged intubation or poor respiratory effort
  • Aspiration: more common with general anesthesia; gastric contents into the airway

docs.title: Ileus
docs.text: Bowel dysfunction after abdominal surgery; bowel motility returns typically in 2–3 days for small bowel, 3–5 days for colon. Prolonged ileus (>5 days) may require NGT decompression.

docs.title: Urinary Retention
docs.text: Common after pelvic surgery, spinal anesthesia, opioid use; managed with urinary catheterization.

docs.title: Anastomotic Leak
docs.text: Failure of a bowel anastomosis; presents 3–7 days postop with fever, abdominal pain, tachycardia. Serious complication requiring reoperation.

docs.title: Wound Dehiscence
docs.text: Fascial closure failure; may present with serosanguineous drainage ("salmon-colored drainage") before it is visually obvious. Risk of evisceration if full separation occurs. Evisceration = cover wound with moist saline gauze; emergent return to OR.

docs.title: Between-Case Cleaning (Turnover)
docs.text: After each case: 1. All soiled drapes, gowns, and materials are removed 2. All contaminated instruments collected and transported to sterile processing in closed containers 3. OR table cleaned with a hospital-grade disinfectant 4. All horizontal surfaces (lights, IV pole, anesthesia workstation) cleaned with disinfectant 5. Floor cleaned 6. Room restocked; new sterile supplies brought in for the next case

docs.title: Terminal Cleaning (End of Day)
docs.text: At the end of the surgical day, each OR receives a terminal clean: - All surfaces cleaned top-to-bottom with a hospital-grade disinfectant - Walls spot-cleaned - Floor scrubbed - Waste removed - All fixed equipment cleaned: OR table, lights, anesthesia machine, stands, monitors

docs.title: Isolation Cases
docs.text: Contact precaution cases (MRSA, VRE, C. difficile): - Terminal clean with appropriate disinfectant (C. difficile requires 10:1 bleach solution — sporicidal) - Personal protective equipment for cleaning staff - Room may be taken out of service until terminal clean is complete

docs.title: 26.6 The Complete Surgical Experience — Reflection
docs.text:
The surgical technologist who has mastered the ApC framework — Approach, Procedure, Closure — is not simply a person who hands instruments. They are:

  • A patient advocate who maintains the sterile field even when no one is watching
  • A team member who communicates clearly and professionally in moments of tension
  • A technical expert who understands the anatomy, the equipment, the procedure, and the plan
  • A safety officer who counts, labels, documents, and speaks up when something is wrong

Every patient on the OR table is someone's parent, partner, or child. They are unconscious, vulnerable, and entirely dependent on the team surrounding them. That team is only as strong as its least attentive member.

The ApC's are not just chapters in a textbook. They are the structure of professional practice and the framework of a career built in service of the most vulnerable moments in a human being's life.

Welcome to the profession.

Proceed to Appendices
